cases.title: Sudden right-sided weakness and trouble speaking
cases.chiefComplaint: His wife reports that during dinner he suddenly could not move his right side or speak clearly.
cases.history: A 68-year-old right-handed man is brought to the ED by his wife after she noticed, during dinner about 90 minutes ago, the sudden onset of right-sided facial drooping, weakness of the right arm and leg, and slurred, nonsensical speech. The deficits began abruptly and have not improved. He denies headache, neck pain, chest pain, palpitations, recent trauma, or seizure activity. There was no loss of consciousness. His medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker with a 30 pack-year history who quit five years ago and drinks alcohol socially. He is retired from construction work. Family history is notable for a myocardial infarction in his father at age 60 and a stroke in his mother. His last known well time is firmly established at 90 minutes before arrival.
cases.exam: The patient is alert but unable to follow commands consistently and produces limited, nonfluent speech with impaired naming and repetition. There is a forced gaze preference to the left and a right homonymous visual field defect. He has a right central facial palsy, with dense weakness of the right arm (0/5) and moderate weakness of the right leg (3/5). Sensation is reduced on the right. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk on the right with an upgoing right plantar response. There is no neck stiffness, no carotid bruit, and the cardiac rhythm is regular. The estimated NIHSS is 14.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a noncontrast CT head immediately
Order MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
Give aspirin 325 mg and admit for observation
stages.explanation: An acute focal neurologic deficit within the thrombolysis window is a stroke alert, and the single most important first step is a noncontrast CT head to distinguish ischemic stroke from intracranial hemorrhage, which would contraindicate thrombolytics. CT is fast and sensitive for blood. Administering alteplase empirically before excluding hemorrhage is dangerous and could be fatal. MRI is more sensitive for early ischemia but is slower and not required before treatment in most centers, delaying time-critical therapy. Giving aspirin and admitting prematurely closes the door on thrombolysis and ignores the time-sensitive opportunity for reperfusion. Imaging guides every subsequent decision.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: no hemorrhage; hyperdense left MCA sign; early loss of the insular ribbon; ASPECTS 9
Fingerstick glucose: 122 mg/dL
Platelets: 240 ×10³/µL
INR: 1.0
Last known well: 90 minutes ago

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Which vascular territory best explains this presentation?
stages.explanation: Right face, arm, and leg weakness with aphasia, a left gaze preference, and a right visual field cut localizes to the dominant left middle cerebral artery territory; the cortex controls the contralateral body and houses language in the dominant hemisphere. A right MCA lesion would cause left-sided weakness and neglect, not aphasia. A left PCA lesion typically causes an isolated right homonymous hemianopia, sometimes with alexia, but not dense hemiparesis or gaze deviation. A basilar artery occlusion produces crossed signs, quadriparesis, oculomotor abnormalities, or coma rather than an isolated unilateral hemispheric syndrome. The eyes deviate toward the side of a destructive hemispheric lesion, here the left.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best next management step?
options: Administer IV thrombolysis (alteplase or tenecteplase) now
Withhold thrombolysis because the deficit is too severe
Lower blood pressure to <140/90 before any treatment
stages.explanation: He presents within 90 minutes of last known well with a disabling deficit and no hemorrhage on CT, making him an excellent candidate for IV thrombolysis. His blood pressure of 182/96 is below the 185/110 threshold and no other contraindication is present. Withholding thrombolysis because the deficit is severe is wrong; severe, disabling deficits are exactly those that benefit, and severity alone is not a contraindication. Lowering BP to <140/90 is unnecessary and risks hypoperfusion; the pre-thrombolysis target is simply <185/110. Waiting for MRI wastes valuable time in a clear clinical-CT scenario and can push the patient out of the window. Time is brain.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After starting thrombolysis, what is the best next step?
options: CT angiography of the head and neck
stages.explanation: After initiating thrombolysis, the next priority is vascular imaging with CT angiography of the head and neck to identify a large vessel occlusion that may be amenable to mechanical thrombectomy, which improves outcomes when added to or instead of thrombolysis. The hyperdense MCA sign already hints at a proximal clot. Carotid duplex evaluates the cervical carotid for secondary prevention but does not assess intracranial occlusion acutely. Echocardiography looks for a cardioembolic source and is part of the later workup, not the emergent decision. EEG is irrelevant without suspicion for seizure. Identifying a large vessel occlusion promptly enables timely transfer to an angiography suite.

cases.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke in the left middle cerebral artery territory
pearls: Sudden focal deficit with aphasia and gaze deviation toward the lesion localizes to the dominant (usually left) MCA.
Noncontrast CT comes first to exclude hemorrhage; early ischemic signs include the hyperdense MCA sign and loss of the insular ribbon.
IV thrombolysis is offered within 4.5 hours of last known well if BP is <185/110 and there are no contraindications.
Always obtain CTA to identify a large vessel occlusion amenable to mechanical thrombectomy.

cases.title: Found with weakness on waking
cases.chiefComplaint: Her husband found her unable to move her left side when she woke up this morning.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman is brought to the ED at 8:00 AM after her husband found her with left-sided weakness when she awoke. She went to bed at 11:00 PM and was last seen normal at that time; she was discovered with the deficit on waking, so her last known well time is about 9 hours ago. She cannot move her left arm and tends to ignore the left side. She denies headache or preceding symptoms. Her history includes hypertension and atrial fibrillation, for which she was prescribed but inconsistently took an anticoagulant. She also has osteoarthritis. Medications include amlodipine, atorvastatin, and apixaban, which she frequently misses. She has no known allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. She lives independently with her husband. Family history is unremarkable. There is no recent trauma, surgery, or bleeding. Because the exact onset is unknown and falls well outside the 4.5-hour thrombolysis window, advanced imaging will be needed to guide treatment decisions.
cases.exam: The patient is awake with eyes deviated to the right and a left homonymous hemianopia. She has a dense left facial droop and left hemiplegia (arm and leg 0/5) with left-sided sensory loss. She demonstrates prominent left visual and tactile neglect, extinguishing to double simultaneous stimulation. Speech is dysarthric but language is intact. The pulse is irregularly irregular. There is no neck stiffness, no carotid bruit, and no signs of trauma. The estimated NIHSS is 16, consistent with a large right hemispheric syndrome.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study?
stages.explanation: Even in a wake-up stroke, the immediate step is a noncontrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage before any reperfusion decision; it is fast and rules out a bleed. MRI is more sensitive for early ischemia and can be used in some protocols (DWI-FLAIR mismatch) but is slower and not the universal first study. Jumping straight to CT perfusion skips the essential step of excluding hemorrhage and is typically obtained together with or after the noncontrast CT and CTA. Carotid ultrasound is a secondary-prevention test with no role in the hyperacute triage of a possible large vessel occlusion. The noncontrast CT anchors the rest of the pathway.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: no hemorrhage; ASPECTS 8; no established large infarct
CT angiography: occlusion of the right M1 segment of the middle cerebral artery
ECG: atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 88
Glucose 110 mg/dL, INR 1.1, platelets 210 ×10³/µL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is outside the 4.5-hour window. What is the best next step to determine thrombectomy eligibility?
options: Obtain CT perfusion (or MRI) to assess core-penumbra mismatch
Proceed directly to thrombectomy without further imaging
Administer IV alteplase based on time of symptom discovery
Admit for observation and antiplatelet therapy
stages.explanation: Her last known well was bedtime, roughly 9 hours ago, well beyond the 4.5-hour thrombolysis window, so management hinges on advanced imaging. Obtaining CT (or MR) perfusion to assess the core-penumbra mismatch is the key step that determines thrombectomy eligibility in the extended window. Proceeding directly to thrombectomy without imaging abandons the evidence-based selection that defines benefit. Giving alteplase based on discovery time is incorrect because the clock starts at last known well, not symptom recognition, placing her outside the window. Admitting for antiplatelet observation forgoes a potentially disabling-stroke-preventing intervention. Perfusion imaging operationalizes the DAWN and DEFUSE-3 selection criteria.

stages.label: PERFUSION IMAGING
stages.content:
CT perfusion: ischemic core volume 15 mL
Hypoperfused (penumbra) volume 90 mL
Mismatch volume 75 mL; mismatch ratio 6.0

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The favorable mismatch on CT perfusion most strongly supports which conclusion?
options: Endovascular thrombectomy is indicated in the extended (6-24 hour) window
The patient has a completed infarct and no intervention is warranted
IV thrombolysis is now safe regardless of time
Findings are nondiagnostic; repeat imaging in 6 hours
stages.explanation: A small ischemic core (15 mL) with a large volume of hypoperfused but salvageable tissue (90 mL), giving a mismatch ratio of 6, is a favorable profile. DAWN and DEFUSE-3 showed that selected patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusion and this kind of core-penumbra mismatch benefit from endovascular thrombectomy 6-24 hours after last known well. The findings are not a completed infarct; the point is that most threatened tissue is still salvageable. Thrombolysis is not made safe by perfusion imaging and remains outside its time window. The data are clearly diagnostic, so repeating imaging in 6 hours would only allow the penumbra to infarct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Should IV thrombolysis be administered?
options: Yes, give alteplase since she is within 9 hours of waking
Give half-dose alteplase as a bridge to thrombectomy
Thrombolysis is mandatory before thrombectomy in all cases
No - last known well is >4.5 hours, so she is outside the window; proceed to thrombectomy
stages.explanation: Her last known well is over 4.5 hours ago, so IV thrombolysis is contraindicated by time; the correct path is mechanical thrombectomy based on the favorable perfusion profile. Giving alteplase because she presented within 9 hours of waking confuses discovery time with last known well and would expose her to bleeding risk without proven benefit. Half-dose alteplase as a bridge is not a validated strategy here and still violates the time window. Thrombolysis is not mandatory before thrombectomy; in late-window, mismatch-selected patients, thrombectomy alone is appropriate. Selection by physiology, not the clock alone, drives late-window care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should her newly recognized atrial fibrillation be anticoagulated after this large infarct?
options: Start full-dose anticoagulation immediately in the ED
Delay oral anticoagulation several days to about two weeks, guided by infarct size and hemorrhage risk
Start lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy instead
stages.explanation: She has newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation and a large completed infarct. Starting full-dose anticoagulation immediately markedly increases the risk of hemorrhagic transformation of a large infarct. Never anticoagulating is wrong because AFib with a high CHA2DS2-VASc score confers substantial recurrent embolic risk. Indefinite dual antiplatelet therapy is inferior to anticoagulation for cardioembolic prevention and adds bleeding risk. The correct approach is to delay oral anticoagulation, guided by infarct size and bleeding risk, often using a staged 1-3-6-12 day strategy in which larger strokes wait longer, after confirming no hemorrhagic transformation on follow-up imaging. This balances recurrence against bleeding.

cases.diagnosis: Right M1 large vessel occlusion stroke treated with late-window mechanical thrombectomy
pearls: Wake-up and late-presenting strokes can still be treated: CT/MR perfusion identifies salvageable penumbra.
DAWN and DEFUSE-3 support thrombectomy 6-24 hours from last known well in selected LVO patients with favorable mismatch.
A small ischemic core with a large penumbra (high mismatch ratio) predicts benefit from reperfusion.
The thrombolysis clock starts at last known well, not when symptoms were discovered.

cases.title: One-sided weakness without other symptoms
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden weakness of the right face, arm, and leg that started two hours ago.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents to the ED with the sudden onset, about 2 hours ago, of weakness involving the right side of his face, arm, and leg. The weakness came on while he was watching television and affects all three regions roughly equally. He has no difficulty understanding or producing speech, no visual complaints, no headache, no neck pain, and no numbness. He denies dizziness, double vision, or trouble swallowing. His medical history is significant for long-standing, poorly controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 1000 mg twice daily, though he admits frequently forgetting doses. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He works as a long-haul truck driver. Family history includes hypertension and stroke in both parents. He has never had a similar episode before today.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, fully oriented, and conversant with normal fluent speech, intact comprehension, naming, and repetition. There is a right central facial weakness with equal weakness of the right arm and right leg (4-/5 throughout). Importantly, there is no aphasia, no neglect, no visual field deficit, and no gaze preference. Sensation is intact to all modalities, and there is no limb ataxia or dysarthria out of proportion. Reflexes are slightly brisk on the right with an equivocal plantar response. Carotid auscultation is clear and the cardiac rhythm is regular. The estimated NIHSS is 4.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step?
stages.explanation: As with any acute focal deficit, the first step is a noncontrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage and guide acute treatment decisions, including thrombolysis eligibility. Lumbar puncture has no role and would be unsafe and irrelevant for a suspected ischemic event. Carotid endarterectomy is a secondary-prevention procedure for symptomatic carotid stenosis and is never an emergent first step before imaging or diagnosis. Starting an IV heparin drip is not standard acute stroke care, does not treat the lesion, and increases bleeding risk before hemorrhage has been excluded. The CT head is fast, widely available, and determines whether the deficit reflects infarction or hemorrhage.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: no hemorrhage; chronic microvascular changes; no large cortical hypodensity
Glucose 168 mg/dL, HbA1c 8.9%, LDL 142 mg/dL, INR 1.0
MRI DWI (next day): small (<1.5 cm) acute infarct in the posterior limb of the left internal capsule

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The absence of aphasia, neglect, and visual field deficit with equal face-arm-leg weakness best points to what kind of lesion?
options: Subcortical lacunar infarct in the internal capsule
stages.explanation: Equal weakness of the face, arm, and leg with completely intact cortical function (no aphasia, neglect, visual field loss, or gaze deviation) is the classic pure motor lacunar syndrome, localizing to a subcortical structure such as the posterior limb of the internal capsule, where descending motor fibers are tightly packed. A cortical MCA infarct would almost always produce cortical signs such as aphasia or neglect. A lobar hemorrhage is a hemorrhage, excluded by the CT, and usually causes cortical signs or headache. A brainstem hemorrhage would typically cause crossed findings, cranial nerve deficits, or depressed consciousness. The pure motor pattern with spared cortex is the key clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute management?
options: Withhold thrombolysis because lacunar strokes are too small to treat
Offer IV thrombolysis as he is within the time window and otherwise eligible
stages.explanation: He is only 2 hours from a clear onset with a disabling deficit and no hemorrhage, so he should be offered IV thrombolysis; lacunar strokes are eligible and benefit from reperfusion within the window. Withholding thrombolysis because lacunar strokes are small is a common misconception; size does not exclude treatment, and the deficit can be disabling. Starting therapeutic anticoagulation is not appropriate acute therapy for a presumed small-vessel stroke and adds bleeding risk. Immediate carotid stenting is irrelevant to a deep lacunar infarct and is not an emergent intervention. Treat within the window first, then pursue secondary prevention.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the best long-term secondary-prevention strategy?
options: Aggressive risk-factor control (BP, glucose, lipids) plus antiplatelet and statin therapy
stages.explanation: Lacunar infarcts arise from chronic small-vessel disease driven by hypertension and diabetes, so the cornerstone of long-term prevention is aggressive vascular risk-factor control (blood pressure, glucose, lipids) combined with antiplatelet therapy and a statin. Long-term warfarin is not indicated absent a cardioembolic source such as AFib and adds bleeding risk. No treatment ignores a high recurrence risk. Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding without long-term benefit; short-course dual therapy is used only in specific minor-stroke or high-risk-TIA scenarios, not indefinitely. Optimizing modifiable risk factors yields the greatest reduction in recurrent lacunar events.

cases.diagnosis: Lacunar (pure motor) stroke of the posterior limb of the internal capsule
pearls: Pure motor hemiparesis affecting face, arm, and leg equally with no cortical signs is the classic lacunar syndrome.
Lacunar strokes result from small-vessel lipohyalinosis driven by chronic hypertension and diabetes.
Absence of aphasia, neglect, and visual field loss argues against a large cortical (MCA) infarct.
Lacunar strokes remain eligible for IV thrombolysis; prevention centers on risk-factor control, antiplatelet, and statin.

cases.title: Brief arm weakness that resolved
cases.chiefComplaint: My left arm went weak this morning for about 20 minutes and then went back to normal.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic the same afternoon after an episode of left arm weakness earlier in the day. While preparing breakfast, she suddenly noticed her left arm felt clumsy and weak and she dropped her coffee cup. The weakness lasted about 20 minutes and resolved completely on its own before she left home. She had no headache, no speech difficulty, no facial droop that she noticed, no visual symptoms, and no loss of consciousness. She has never had a similar event. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She has never smoked and does not drink alcohol. She is a retired teacher who lives with her husband. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. She is anxious that the episode could be a warning sign of a stroke.
cases.exam: On examination the patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Mental status is normal with fluent speech and intact comprehension. Cranial nerves are intact with no facial asymmetry and full visual fields. Motor strength is 5/5 in all extremities, including the previously affected left arm, with normal tone and no pronator drift. Sensation, coordination, and gait are normal. Reflexes are symmetric and plantar responses are flexor. There is no carotid bruit appreciated. The cardiac rhythm is regular without murmurs. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A sudden focal neurologic deficit (left arm weakness) that fully resolved within about 20 minutes and left a normal examination is a transient ischemic attack: transient, ischemic, focal, and now without deficit. A completed ischemic stroke would typically leave a persistent deficit or show infarction. A focal seizure usually produces positive phenomena (jerking, automatisms) and often postictal symptoms, not isolated negative weakness that resolves cleanly. A complicated migraine typically features a gradually spreading aura with headache and is less likely as a first event at age 70. The transient, purely negative deficit with vascular risk factors fits TIA and demands urgent workup.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Her ABCD2 score is 5. What does this indicate?
options: Moderate-to-high short-term stroke risk warranting expedited evaluation
Low risk; routine follow-up in one month is adequate
stages.explanation: The ABCD2 score (Age ≥60, Blood pressure ≥140/90, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes) estimates short-term stroke risk after a TIA. Her score of 5 (age, elevated BP, unilateral weakness, duration) indicates moderate-to-high risk and warrants expedited evaluation and secondary prevention, ideally within 24-48 hours. Calling it low risk with follow-up in a month dangerously delays a workup during the highest-risk window. Concluding the episode was nonvascular ignores the classic focal vascular pattern and her risk factors. IV thrombolysis is for acute infarction with an ongoing deficit, not a fully resolved TIA. Rapid risk stratification drives timely prevention.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
MRI brain with DWI: no acute infarction
Carotid duplex ultrasound: 70-79% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
LDL 138 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.6%

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the duplex findings, what is the best next step?
options: Refer for carotid revascularization (endarterectomy) given symptomatic 70-79% stenosis
stages.explanation: Her left arm symptoms localize to the right hemisphere, and the duplex shows a symptomatic 70-79% right internal carotid stenosis. Symptomatic high-grade (70-99%) carotid stenosis benefits substantially from carotid revascularization, usually endarterectomy, performed early alongside medical therapy. Continuing medical therapy alone misses a major reduction in recurrent stroke risk for tight symptomatic stenosis. Anticoagulation with warfarin is not the treatment for carotid atherosclerotic disease and adds bleeding risk without benefit. Repeating ultrasound in 6 months inappropriately delays definitive treatment in a symptomatic patient. The symptomatic, high-grade lesion ipsilateral to symptoms is the surgical target.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What immediate medical therapy should be started?
options: Begin antiplatelet therapy and a high-intensity statin
No medication is needed since symptoms resolved
Anticoagulate with a direct oral anticoagulant
stages.explanation: Regardless of whether she undergoes carotid surgery, every TIA patient needs immediate medical secondary prevention: antiplatelet therapy and a high-intensity statin, plus blood pressure and risk-factor control. Doing nothing because symptoms resolved leaves her at high risk for an early disabling stroke. Anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant is reserved for cardioembolic sources such as atrial fibrillation, which she does not have on her normal-sinus ECG. A triptan treats migraine and is irrelevant to an ischemic TIA. Antiplatelet plus statin therapy, started promptly, is the evidence-based foundation of TIA management.

cases.diagnosis: Transient ischemic attack due to symptomatic right internal carotid stenosis
pearls: A TIA is a transient focal neurologic deficit from ischemia without acute infarction; symptoms typically resolve within an hour.
ABCD2 stratifies short-term stroke risk; higher scores warrant urgent evaluation within 24-48 hours.
TIA workup includes brain imaging, vascular imaging (carotid duplex/CTA), and cardiac evaluation for AFib.
Symptomatic 70-99% carotid stenosis benefits from carotid endarterectomy plus antiplatelet and high-intensity statin therapy.

cases.title: Sudden weakness and headache with very high blood pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Coworkers report he developed a sudden severe headache, left-sided weakness, and vomiting at work.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man is brought to the ED by coworkers after the sudden onset, about one hour ago, of a severe headache followed by weakness of the left side of his body and vomiting. He became progressively more drowsy. There was no preceding trauma, no seizure, and no fever. His coworkers report he has long-standing hypertension and has not been taking his medication reliably for months. His only prescribed medication is amlodipine 10 mg daily, which he frequently skips. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes one pack of cigarettes daily and drinks several alcoholic beverages most evenings. He works in a warehouse and lives alone. Family history includes hypertension and stroke in his father. He takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. On arrival his blood pressure is markedly elevated. The clinical picture of abrupt headache, vomiting, decreased alertness, and a focal deficit raises concern for an intracranial catastrophe.
cases.exam: The patient is somnolent but arousable to voice and follows simple commands intermittently. There is a forced gaze deviation to the right and a left central facial droop. He has dense left hemiplegia (0/5 in the left arm and leg) with reduced sensation on the left and dysarthria. Pupils are equal and reactive. There is no neck stiffness initially. Fundoscopy is limited but shows no obvious papilledema. Reflexes are increased on the left with an extensor left plantar response. The cardiac rhythm is regular. The general examination is otherwise notable only for the markedly elevated blood pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate step?
stages.explanation: An abrupt severe headache with vomiting, decreased consciousness, and a focal deficit demands an immediate noncontrast CT head to identify hemorrhage, which is hyperdense and instantly visible, and to distinguish it from ischemia. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when a mass or hemorrhage with possible elevated intracranial pressure is suspected, and it would not be the first step. IV thrombolysis would be catastrophic if given for a hemorrhage and is contraindicated before excluding bleeding. MRI with contrast is slower, less available, and unnecessary for initial detection of acute blood. CT rapidly differentiates the bleed and guides emergent management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: 3.2 cm (≈30 mL) hyperdense hematoma in the right putamen/basal ganglia with mild surrounding edema; no intraventricular extension; midline shift 3 mm
Platelets 230 ×10³/µL, INR 1.0, glucose 140 mg/dL
Urine toxicology: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A chronically hypertensive, nonadherent patient with an abrupt deficit and a CT showing a hyperdense hematoma centered in the putamen/basal ganglia has a hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage, the classic deep location from small-vessel disease. An ischemic MCA stroke would appear hypodense, not hyperdense, and would not show an acute hematoma. Subarachnoid hemorrhage tracks blood in the basal cisterns and sulci with a thunderclap headache, not a focal parenchymal clot in the basal ganglia. A brain tumor with edema presents subacutely with progressive symptoms, not an abrupt deficit, and looks different on CT. Deep location plus hypertension equals hypertensive ICH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How should his blood pressure be managed?
options: Do not treat the blood pressure to maintain perfusion
Lower systolic BP to a target around 140 mmHg using a titratable IV agent such as nicardipine
Lower BP abruptly to below 110 systolic
stages.explanation: In acute intracerebral hemorrhage, the correct strategy is controlled blood pressure lowering to a systolic target around 140 mmHg using a titratable IV agent such as nicardipine, which reduces hematoma expansion while preserving perfusion. Not treating the pressure at 222/120 allows ongoing bleeding and expansion. Abrupt reduction below 110 systolic risks cerebral hypoperfusion and is not recommended; lowering should be smooth and not excessive. IV thrombolysis is absolutely contraindicated and would worsen the hemorrhage. Smooth, moderate BP control to roughly 140 systolic is the guideline-based approach for hypertensive ICH.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain neurosurgical consultation and monitor for hematoma expansion; reverse any coagulopathy
stages.explanation: The dominant early threat in intracerebral hemorrhage is hematoma expansion, so the priorities are urgent neurosurgical consultation and serial clinical and imaging monitoring, along with reversing any coagulopathy. Prophylactic anticoagulation would be disastrous and promote bleeding. Aspirin and a statin are antithrombotic and lipid therapies appropriate for ischemic disease, not an acute brain hemorrhage. Immediate lumbar puncture is contraindicated with an intracranial mass lesion and risk of herniation and offers no benefit. Neurosurgical involvement is essential for considering evacuation, external ventricular drainage for hydrocephalus, and intracranial pressure management, particularly for cerebellar or large bleeds.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage of the right basal ganglia (putamen)
pearls: Deep (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum) hemorrhages in a chronically hypertensive patient reflect small-vessel disease (Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms).
Guidelines support lowering systolic BP to roughly 140 mmHg with a titratable IV agent; avoid overly aggressive reduction.
Hematoma expansion is the major early threat; reverse any coagulopathy and monitor with serial exams and imaging.
Obtain early neurosurgical consultation, especially for cerebellar hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, or significant mass effect.

cases.title: Worst headache of my life
cases.chiefComplaint: A sudden, explosive headache that reached its worst within seconds while gardening.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents to the ED with a severe headache that began suddenly about 6 hours ago while she was gardening. She describes it as the worst headache of her life, reaching maximal intensity within seconds. The headache is global and associated with neck stiffness, nausea, two episodes of vomiting, and sensitivity to light. She had a brief loss of consciousness at onset, witnessed by her husband, lasting seconds. She denies focal weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty. Her medical history includes hypertension. She takes hydrochlorothiazide and is a current smoker with a 20 pack-year history. She drinks alcohol occasionally and has no known drug allergies. Family history is significant for a mother who suffered a ruptured brain aneurysm in her fifties. She has never experienced a headache like this before and typically does not get migraines. She remains alert but is in obvious distress from the pain.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented but appears uncomfortable and prefers a darkened room. There is nuchal rigidity with resistance to passive neck flexion and reported photophobia. Cranial nerves are intact, including symmetric pupils and full extraocular movements; fundoscopic examination shows no subhyaloid hemorrhage or papilledema. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with no pronator drift, and sensation is intact. There is no aphasia or neglect. Coordination and gait, though limited by discomfort, show no focal deficit. Reflexes are symmetric with flexor plantar responses. The cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test?
stages.explanation: A thunderclap headache that peaks within seconds, with neck stiffness and brief loss of consciousness, is subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise, and the immediate test is a noncontrast CT head, which detects subarachnoid blood with very high sensitivity early after onset. MRI is less practical and not the first-line acute study for SAH. Performing lumbar puncture first is incorrect; CT comes before LP, and LP is reserved for when CT is negative but suspicion persists. CT venography evaluates venous thrombosis, not the leading concern of aneurysmal SAH here. The noncontrast CT is fast, available, and the appropriate initial study.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: no acute hemorrhage, no mass, no hydrocephalus
Time from symptom onset: approximately 6 hours
Glucose 110 mg/dL, INR 1.0, platelets 245 ×10³/µL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The CT is negative. What is the best next step?
options: Perform lumbar puncture to evaluate for xanthochromia and red blood cells
Discharge with analgesia and outpatient follow-up
Reassure that a normal CT excludes thunderclap headache
stages.explanation: Her CT is negative, but she presented at 6 hours and CT sensitivity declines after the first several hours, so a negative scan does not exclude SAH in a classic thunderclap presentation. The correct next step is lumbar puncture to look for xanthochromia and red blood cells that do not clear between tubes. Discharging her or reassuring her that a normal CT excludes the diagnosis risks missing a sentinel aneurysmal bleed that could be fatal if it re-ruptures. Treating as migraine with triptans ignores the dangerous thunderclap pattern. When suspicion is high and CT is non-diagnostic, LP (or CTA) is mandatory.

stages.label: LUMBAR PUNCTURE
stages.content:
Opening pressure 18 cm H2O
CSF tube 1: 4500 RBC/µL; tube 4: 4300 RBC/µL (no clearing)
CSF xanthochromia: present
CSF WBC 5/µL, glucose normal, protein mildly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Xanthochromia confirms the bleed. What is the best next test to identify the source?
options: CT angiography (or digital subtraction angiography) to identify a cerebral aneurysm
stages.explanation: CSF xanthochromia with persistently elevated red cells that do not clear from tube 1 to tube 4 confirms subarachnoid hemorrhage rather than a traumatic tap. The essential next step is vascular imaging, CT angiography or digital subtraction angiography, to identify the culprit cerebral aneurysm so it can be secured. EEG has no role in diagnosing the bleeding source. Carotid duplex images the cervical carotid and cannot detect an intracranial aneurysm. Repeating a noncontrast CT in 24 hours delays definitive diagnosis and risks re-rupture. Localizing the aneurysm is the priority once SAH is established.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management once aneurysmal SAH is confirmed?
options: Administer oral nimodipine, control blood pressure, and secure the aneurysm by coiling or clipping
stages.explanation: Once aneurysmal SAH is confirmed, management includes oral nimodipine to reduce delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm, careful blood pressure control, and prompt securing of the aneurysm by endovascular coiling or surgical clipping to prevent re-rupture. IV heparin would worsen bleeding and is contraindicated. IV alteplase is for acute ischemic stroke and would be catastrophic in hemorrhage. Discharging once pain is controlled ignores the high early risk of re-rupture and vasospasm, which are leading causes of death and disability. Nimodipine plus aneurysm securing is the core of SAH care.

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (CT-negative, LP-confirmed)
pearls: A thunderclap headache (maximal within seconds to a minute) is subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise.
Noncontrast CT sensitivity for SAH is highest in the first 6 hours and declines thereafter; a negative CT does not exclude SAH later.
When CT is negative but suspicion remains, lumbar puncture looks for xanthochromia and non-clearing red blood cells.
Once SAH is confirmed, CTA/DSA localizes the aneurysm; give nimodipine, control BP, and secure the aneurysm by coiling or clipping.

cases.title: Severe headache and a seizure after delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: A worsening headache for days that culminated in a generalized convulsion at home.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman presents to the ED 10 days after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with a 4-day history of progressively worsening headache that culminated in a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home this morning. The headache is constant, diffuse, and worse when she lies down or strains; over-the-counter analgesics have not helped. She also reports intermittent blurred vision. She has no history of seizures, migraines, or clotting disorders. Before pregnancy she used combined oral contraceptive pills. She takes only prenatal vitamins and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and does not drink alcohol while breastfeeding. She has been less able to keep up with fluids since delivery. Family history is unremarkable. The combination of a postpartum (hypercoagulable) state, a positional headache suggestive of raised intracranial pressure, and a new seizure is concerning for a cerebral venous process.
cases.exam: The patient is drowsy and postictal but rousable and oriented once stimulated. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There is mild weakness of the left arm (4/5) without a dense hemiplegia, and a subtle left facial asymmetry. Pupils are equal and reactive, and extraocular movements are intact. There is no neck rigidity. Sensation is grossly intact. Reflexes are slightly brisk on the left. She has bitten the lateral tongue. The remainder of the general examination is notable for mild dehydration; the cardiovascular and abdominal examinations are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step?
stages.explanation: A postpartum woman with progressive headache and a new seizure needs urgent neuroimaging, and the practical first study is a noncontrast CT head to detect hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, or a venous infarct and to triage emergently. Immediate lumbar puncture is inappropriate before imaging given possible mass effect or hemorrhage and risk of herniation. IV alteplase is contraindicated and irrelevant without an arterial ischemic diagnosis, and dangerous if hemorrhage is present. Carotid ultrasound evaluates the cervical arteries and is useless for a suspected venous or intracranial process. The noncontrast CT is the fast initial step before dedicated venous imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Noncontrast CT head: right parietal hemorrhagic infarct that does not conform to an arterial territory; possible hyperdense 'cord sign' in the superior sagittal sinus; mild diffuse edema
Glucose 96 mg/dL, INR 1.0, platelets 280 ×10³/µL, WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL
D-dimer markedly elevated

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The CT suggests a hemorrhagic infarct that does not respect arterial territories plus a possible cord sign, classic for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, so the best confirmatory test is dedicated venous imaging with CT venography or MR venography, which shows the filling defect or absent flow in the sinus. Digital arterial angiography for vasculitis targets the wrong vascular bed and the wrong disease. Repeating a noncontrast CT in 12 hours delays diagnosis of a treatable, dangerous condition. Carotid duplex images the neck arteries and cannot assess the dural venous sinuses. Venography directly confirms or excludes CVST.

stages.label: VENOGRAPHY
stages.content: CT venography: filling defect with non-opacification of the superior sagittal sinus and right transverse sinus, consistent with thrombosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A peripartum, hypercoagulable woman with raised-ICP headache, seizure, papilledema, a non-arterial hemorrhagic infarct, and venographic absence of flow in the superior sagittal and transverse sinuses has cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. An arterial MCA infarct follows an arterial territory and would not show sinus filling defects. PRES typically shows posterior vasogenic edema in the setting of hypertension or eclampsia, not sinus thrombosis with a cord sign. A primary hypertensive ICH is a deep parenchymal bleed from chronic hypertension, not a venous infarct in a young postpartum patient. The venous occlusion on imaging seals the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment despite the hemorrhagic infarct?
options: Withhold anticoagulation because of the hemorrhagic component
Start therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., heparin) despite the venous hemorrhagic infarct
stages.explanation: The defining teaching point is that therapeutic anticoagulation, typically with heparin, is the treatment for CVST even when a venous hemorrhagic infarct is present; the hemorrhage is a consequence of venous congestion, and anticoagulation relieves the obstruction and improves outcomes. Withholding anticoagulation because of the hemorrhage is the classic mistake and worsens prognosis. IV alteplase is not standard initial therapy and would increase bleeding. Proceeding directly to decompressive craniectomy is reserved for impending herniation, not routine management. Anticoagulate despite the venous hemorrhagic infarct.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: How should her seizure and underlying risk be addressed?
options: Start an antiseizure medication and continue anticoagulation; investigate prothrombotic and peripartum risk factors
No antiseizure medication is ever indicated in CVST
Stop anticoagulation now that the seizure has occurred
Begin antiplatelet therapy instead of anticoagulation
stages.explanation: She had a provoked seizure from the venous infarct, so the correct plan is to start an antiseizure medication for seizure control while continuing anticoagulation, and to investigate underlying prothrombotic and peripartum risk factors. Saying antiseizure medication is never indicated is wrong; symptomatic seizures with a supratentorial lesion warrant treatment. Stopping anticoagulation because a seizure occurred misunderstands the pathophysiology and removes the essential therapy. Switching to antiplatelet therapy is inadequate for CVST, which requires anticoagulation. Treat the seizure, keep anticoagulating, and search for thrombophilia.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with venous hemorrhagic infarct
pearls: Suspect CVST with headache, seizures, focal deficits, or raised ICP in hypercoagulable states (peripartum, OCPs, thrombophilia).
A hemorrhagic infarct that does not respect arterial territories, or an empty-delta/cord sign, suggests CVST; confirm with CT or MR venography.
Therapeutic anticoagulation (heparin) is the treatment even with a venous hemorrhagic infarct.
Manage seizures with antiseizure medication and investigate underlying prothrombotic risk factors.

cases.title: Neck pain and a droopy eyelid after a neck adjustment
cases.chiefComplaint: Right neck and face pain with a drooping right eyelid that started after a chiropractic neck adjustment.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents to the ED with right-sided neck and facial pain and a drooping right eyelid that developed over the past two days. His symptoms began the day after a cervical spinal manipulation performed by a chiropractor for neck stiffness. He describes a steady right-sided headache and noticed in the mirror that his right pupil looks smaller and the eyelid is partially closed. He also had a brief episode of tingling in the left hand that resolved. He denies diplopia, vision loss, dysarthria, or limb weakness currently. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He has no known drug allergies. He is a nonsmoker, exercises regularly, and drinks alcohol rarely. He works as an accountant. Family history is unremarkable, with no known connective tissue disease. He is otherwise healthy and alarmed by the new eye findings and persistent neck pain.
cases.exam: On examination the patient is alert and oriented with normal, fluent speech. There is partial ptosis of the right upper eyelid and right-sided miosis; the pupil reacts to light but is smaller than the left, and facial sweating appears preserved. Extraocular movements are full without diplopia, and visual acuity and fields are normal. There is tenderness over the right anterolateral neck. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact. Motor strength is 5/5 in all limbs with no drift, sensation is intact, and coordination and gait are normal. No carotid bruit is heard and the cardiac rhythm is regular.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of unilateral ptosis and miosis indicates which syndrome?
stages.explanation: Unilateral ptosis combined with miosis is the hallmark of Horner syndrome, reflecting disruption of the oculosympathetic pathway. A third nerve palsy causes ptosis with a dilated (mydriatic) pupil and an eye that is down and out, the opposite pupil finding. Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but does not produce anisocoria or miosis. Bell palsy is a peripheral facial nerve palsy affecting facial muscles, not the pupil or lid elevation in this pattern. Recognizing the ptosis-plus-miosis combination as Horner syndrome, especially with neck pain after manipulation, points toward carotid dissection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best next step to confirm the suspected cause?
options: CT or MR angiography of the head and neck
stages.explanation: Painful Horner syndrome after neck manipulation strongly suggests internal carotid artery dissection, so the best next step is vascular imaging with CT or MR angiography of the head and neck, which can show the intramural hematoma, flame-shaped taper, or crescent sign and assess for ischemia. A noncontrast CT head alone cannot evaluate the arterial wall or lumen and would miss the dissection. Lumbar puncture is irrelevant to this vascular diagnosis. Acetylcholine receptor antibody testing targets myasthenia gravis, which does not explain the pupil findings or neck pain. Dedicated arterial imaging confirms the dissection.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography neck: long tapering ('flame-shaped') narrowing of the right internal carotid artery with an eccentric intramural hematoma and crescent sign, beginning about 2 cm above the bifurcation
Noncontrast CT head: no hemorrhage, no established infarct
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The CTA shows a long tapering narrowing of the internal carotid with an eccentric intramural hematoma and crescent sign in a young man after cervical manipulation, which is diagnostic of internal carotid artery dissection. Atherosclerotic carotid stenosis occurs at the bifurcation in older patients with calcified plaque, not an intramural hematoma in a healthy 38-year-old. A carotid body tumor is a vascular paraganglioma at the bifurcation causing a splaying mass, not a tapering luminal narrowing with mural hematoma. Giant cell arteritis affects older patients and involves the extracranial cranial arteries with systemic inflammatory features. The imaging pattern is classic dissection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Begin antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) and avoid further neck manipulation
stages.explanation: Management of carotid dissection centers on antithrombotic therapy to prevent thromboembolic stroke, either an antiplatelet agent or anticoagulation (shown equivalent in the CADISS trial), along with avoiding further neck manipulation. Emergent carotid endarterectomy is not indicated for an acute dissection and risks extending it. High-dose corticosteroids treat inflammatory arteritis, not a mechanical dissection. Discharging without treatment leaves the patient at significant risk for embolic stroke from the dissection flap. Antithrombotic therapy plus avoidance of further trauma is the standard approach, with endovascular intervention reserved for selected refractory cases.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: Why is facial sweating preserved (partial Horner) in internal carotid dissection?
options: The lesion spares the first-order central neuron
Sympathetic sudomotor fibers to the face travel along the external carotid artery, so they are spared in internal carotid lesions
Anhidrosis never occurs in Horner syndrome
stages.explanation: In carotid dissection the Horner syndrome is partial, sparing facial sweating, because the sudomotor sympathetic fibers destined for the face travel along the external carotid artery, whereas the dissection involves the internal carotid; thus the oculosympathetic fibers (to the pupil and lid) running with the internal carotid are affected but facial sweating is preserved. Sparing of the first-order central neuron is not the mechanism for preserved sweating in a postganglionic carotid lesion. Saying anhidrosis never occurs in Horner syndrome is false; central and preganglionic lesions can cause hemifacial anhidrosis. Parasympathetic damage does not explain miosis, which results from unopposed parasympathetic tone after sympathetic loss.

cases.diagnosis: Internal carotid artery dissection with painful partial Horner syndrome
pearls: Carotid dissection classically presents with the triad of ipsilateral neck/face pain, partial Horner syndrome, and later cerebral or retinal ischemia.
The Horner is painful and partial (ptosis and miosis without facial anhidrosis) because sudomotor fibers follow the external carotid and are spared.
Dissection is often preceded by minor trauma, including cervical manipulation; confirm with CTA or MRA/MRI of the neck.
Treat with antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation, equivalent in CADISS) and avoid further neck manipulation.

cases.title: Sudden dizziness, hoarseness, and trouble swallowing
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt spinning dizziness with a hoarse voice and difficulty swallowing.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents to the ED with the sudden onset, about 3 hours ago, of severe vertigo with nausea and vomiting, hoarseness of his voice, difficulty swallowing, and persistent hiccups. He also reports unsteadiness when walking, veering to the right, and numbness of the right side of his face along with reduced sensation on the left side of his body. He denies any limb weakness, headache, or chest pain. His medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 35 pack-year smoking history. Medications are amlodipine and atorvastatin, though adherence is inconsistent. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially and works as a mechanic. Family history includes hypertension and coronary disease. The striking combination of vertigo, bulbar symptoms, and a crossed pattern of sensory loss points toward a brainstem localization rather than a hemispheric stroke.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and fully oriented with hoarse, dysarthric speech. There is right-sided ptosis and miosis (Horner syndrome) with horizontal nystagmus on right gaze. Pinprick and temperature sensation are reduced over the right side of the face and the left side of the body, while light touch is relatively preserved. The right palate is lower with a diminished gag reflex. There is dysmetria and past-pointing of the right arm and leg with a tendency to fall to the right. Notably, limb strength is full (5/5) throughout with no lower facial weakness. The cardiac rhythm is regular.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best imaging study to confirm the lesion?
options: Noncontrast CT head (high sensitivity for the posterior fossa)
stages.explanation: A posterior fossa stroke is suspected, and although a noncontrast CT is often obtained first to exclude hemorrhage, CT is insensitive for acute brainstem and cerebellar infarcts, so the best test to confirm the lesion is MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging, which reliably detects acute medullary infarction. Stating that CT has high sensitivity for the posterior fossa is false; bone artifact and small infarct size limit it. Lumbar puncture is not indicated for a suspected ischemic brainstem stroke. Carotid duplex evaluates the anterior circulation and would miss vertebrobasilar disease. DWI MRI is the imaging study of choice for this localization.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Where does this constellation of findings localize?
stages.explanation: Ipsilateral facial and contralateral body loss of pain and temperature (crossed sensory loss), an ipsilateral Horner syndrome, hoarseness and dysphagia, vertigo with nystagmus, and ipsilateral limb ataxia with preserved strength localize precisely to the lateral medulla. The internal capsule produces contralateral pure motor or sensory deficits without crossed findings or bulbar signs. A cortical lesion would cause contralateral face and body deficits together with possible cortical signs, not crossed sensory loss. The cervical spinal cord does not explain facial sensory loss, Horner syndrome, vertigo, or bulbar dysfunction. The crossed pattern is the signature of lateral medullary localization.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
MRI brain DWI: acute infarct in the right lateral medulla
CT angiography: occlusion of the right vertebral artery near the PICA origin
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
LDL 150 mg/dL

stages.label: VASCULAR SUPPLY
stages.question: Which artery is most commonly responsible for this syndrome?
options: Posterior inferior cerebellar artery / vertebral artery
stages.explanation: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome most commonly results from occlusion of the vertebral artery or its branch, the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA), which supplies the lateral medulla. The anterior cerebral artery supplies the medial frontal lobe and would cause contralateral leg weakness, not a brainstem syndrome. The middle cerebral artery supplies the lateral hemisphere, causing face/arm weakness and cortical signs. The anterior spinal artery supplies the medial medulla, producing a medial medullary syndrome with contralateral hemiparesis and tongue weakness, not the lateral pattern here. Vertebral/PICA territory disease underlies Wallenberg syndrome.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most important immediate measure to prevent a common early complication?
options: Encourage early oral intake to maintain hydration
Keep the patient NPO and obtain a bedside swallow evaluation to reduce aspiration risk
stages.explanation: The most dangerous early complication of lateral medullary infarction is dysphagia from involvement of the nucleus ambiguus, which markedly raises aspiration risk, so the priority is to keep the patient NPO and obtain a bedside swallow evaluation before allowing oral intake. Encouraging early oral intake risks aspiration pneumonia. Prophylactic antibiotics do not prevent aspiration and are not indicated without infection. Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation is not the routine acute treatment for this ischemic stroke and adds bleeding risk without indication. Protecting the airway and swallowing function is the key immediate supportive measure.

cases.diagnosis: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome from right vertebral/PICA-territory infarct
pearls: Wallenberg syndrome produces crossed sensory loss: ipsilateral facial and contralateral body loss of pain and temperature.
Other features include ipsilateral Horner, ataxia, vertigo/nystagmus, and dysphagia/hoarseness (nucleus ambiguus) with preserved limb strength.
It usually results from vertebral artery or PICA occlusion; MRI is far more sensitive than CT for posterior fossa infarcts.
Dysphagia carries a high aspiration risk, so keep the patient NPO until a swallow evaluation is completed.

cases.title: Abrupt left-sided weakness with an irregular heartbeat
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted with sudden left-sided weakness, now found to have an irregular heart rhythm on telemetry.
cases.history: An 80-year-old woman was admitted to the medical ward 2 days ago after presenting with the sudden onset of left-sided facial droop and weakness of the left arm and leg. She arrived well beyond the thrombolysis window and was not a candidate for acute reperfusion therapy; she was diagnosed with an acute right hemispheric ischemic stroke. On continuous telemetry during this admission, she has been found to have atrial fibrillation that was not previously known; she was not on any anticoagulation. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Home medications are amlodipine and atorvastatin. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. She lives with her daughter and was previously independent. Family history is noncontributory. The team is now deciding when and how to begin antithrombotic therapy to prevent another cardioembolic event while minimizing the risk of bleeding into the infarct.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented with fluent speech and intact comprehension. The pulse is irregularly irregular at about 104 beats per minute. She has a left central facial droop and weakness of the left arm (4-/5) and left leg (4/5) that has modestly improved since admission, with mild left-sided sensory inattention. There is no aphasia. Pupils are equal and reactive, extraocular movements are full, and there is no new headache or vomiting. Reflexes are slightly brisk on the left with an extensor left plantar response. Cardiac auscultation confirms an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely stroke mechanism?
stages.explanation: Sudden, simultaneous onset of a large hemispheric deficit in a patient now found to be in atrial fibrillation is most consistent with a cardioembolic stroke from AFib; emboli typically cause abrupt, maximal-at-onset cortical strokes. A lacunar small-vessel stroke produces pure motor or sensory syndromes without cortical signs and is not typically associated with neglect. Watershed hypoperfusion follows hypotension or critical stenosis and causes border-zone deficits, not this pattern with new AFib. Carotid dissection is more common in younger patients, often with neck pain and Horner syndrome, and is not suggested here. Newly documented AFib with a cortical stroke points to a cardioembolic mechanism.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before deciding on anticoagulation, what is essential?
options: Confirm brain imaging to assess infarct size and exclude hemorrhagic transformation, and obtain echocardiography
Start full-dose anticoagulation immediately on the ward
No further workup needed; discharge on aspirin
stages.explanation: Before deciding on anticoagulation, it is essential to characterize the infarct, so the correct step is to confirm brain imaging to assess infarct size and exclude hemorrhagic transformation and to obtain echocardiography to evaluate for cardiac thrombus and structural disease. Starting full-dose anticoagulation immediately risks hemorrhagic transformation, especially in a large infarct. IV thrombolysis is irrelevant 2 days after onset and far outside any window. Discharging on aspirin without workup ignores a high-risk cardioembolic source and the need to plan anticoagulation. Imaging and echocardiography inform the safe timing and choice of therapy.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
CT head (day 2): established infarct in the right MCA territory, moderate size; no hemorrhagic transformation
Transthoracic echo: left atrial enlargement, LVEF 55%, no thrombus visualized
ECG/telemetry: atrial fibrillation
CHA2DS2-VASc score: 6

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: When should oral anticoagulation be initiated?
options: Within the first 24 hours regardless of infarct size
Never, given her age and bleeding risk
Delay several days, guided by infarct size (e.g., the '1-3-6-12 day' approach), once hemorrhagic transformation is excluded
Treat with aspirin alone indefinitely instead of anticoagulation
stages.explanation: After excluding hemorrhagic transformation, the evidence-based approach to a moderate-sized cardioembolic infarct is to delay oral anticoagulation for several days, guided by infarct size and bleeding risk, commonly using the staged 1-3-6-12 day strategy in which larger strokes wait longer. Starting within 24 hours regardless of size increases hemorrhagic transformation risk for larger infarcts. Never anticoagulating leaves a high CHA2DS2-VASc patient unprotected from recurrent embolism. Aspirin alone indefinitely is inferior to anticoagulation for AFib-related stroke prevention. Timing balances early recurrence against hemorrhagic transformation.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the preferred long-term agent for stroke prevention?
options: A direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) is preferred over aspirin
Aspirin is equally effective and preferred
stages.explanation: For long-term stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation with a high CHA2DS2-VASc score, a direct oral anticoagulant such as apixaban is preferred over aspirin and over warfarin for most patients, offering better safety and efficacy. Saying aspirin is equally effective and preferred is incorrect; antiplatelet therapy is markedly inferior to anticoagulation in AFib. Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding without matching anticoagulation's benefit. No therapy leaves a high-risk patient unprotected. A DOAC is the guideline-preferred long-term agent once it is safe to start.

cases.diagnosis: Cardioembolic stroke from newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation
pearls: Atrial fibrillation causes cardioembolic strokes, which are often larger and carry a higher risk of hemorrhagic transformation.
CHA2DS2-VASc guides long-term anticoagulation; a high score in AFib mandates anticoagulation over antiplatelet therapy.
Timing of anticoagulation after cardioembolic stroke balances recurrence against hemorrhagic transformation (commonly the '1-3-6-12 day' approach).
DOACs are preferred over warfarin for nonvalvular AFib stroke prevention.

cases.title: Room spins when she rolls over in bed
cases.chiefComplaint: Every time I roll over in bed the whole room spins for about thirty seconds.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with one week of brief spinning spells. Each episode begins seconds after she rolls over in bed onto her right side, looks up to reach a high shelf, or bends forward to tie her shoes; the room whirls violently for 20–40 seconds and then settles completely. She has nausea during spells but no vomiting. Between episodes she feels mildly 'off balance' for a minute and then entirely normal. She denies hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear fullness, and has no headache, double vision, slurred speech, numbness, weakness, or trouble walking between spells. There was no head trauma and no recent viral illness. Past medical history includes hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. She takes no other medications and has no drug allergies. She works as a librarian, does not smoke, and drinks an occasional glass of wine. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: Alert and comfortable while sitting quietly between spells, in no acute distress. No spontaneous or gaze-evoked nystagmus is seen in primary or eccentric gaze. Cranial nerves II through XII are intact; hearing is grossly normal to finger rub bilaterally, and otoscopy shows normal, mobile tympanic membranes without effusion. There is no dysarthria, and finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing are accurate. Gait is steady, tandem gait is intact, and Romberg testing is negative. Orthostatic vital signs are negative. Strength is 5/5 throughout with symmetric reflexes and intact sensation to light touch and pinprick.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the best next step to evaluate her brief, position-triggered vertigo?
options: MRI of the brain with internal auditory canal protocol
Perform the Dix-Hallpike maneuver at the bedside
stages.explanation: Recurrent vertigo lasting under a minute, triggered by rolling over and head extension, with a normal interictal neurologic examination is classic for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, and the Dix-Hallpike maneuver is the diagnostic test of choice. It is performed at the bedside in seconds and both confirms the diagnosis and identifies the affected ear and canal, which directs treatment. MRI with attention to the internal auditory canals is reserved for atypical features — persistent vertigo, unilateral hearing loss, or central signs — none of which are present. Audiometry evaluates cochlear symptoms such as hearing loss, tinnitus, and fullness, which she explicitly lacks. Carotid duplex assesses anterior-circulation atherosclerosis; vertigo of vascular origin involves the posterior circulation, so this test contributes nothing here.

stages.label: PROVOCATIVE TESTING
stages.content:
Dix-Hallpike, right ear down: after a 5-second latency, mixed upbeating–torsional nystagmus toward the right (dependent) ear lasting about 20 seconds, with reproduction of her vertigo; the response fatigues with immediate repeat testing
Dix-Hallpike, left ear down: no nystagmus, no symptoms
Supine roll test: no horizontal nystagmus in either direction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Right posterior canal benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
stages.explanation: The Dix-Hallpike response — short latency, transient upbeating–torsional nystagmus toward the dependent right ear, reproduction of vertigo, and fatigability on repetition — is diagnostic of posterior canal BPPV of the right ear, caused by free-floating otoconia within the canal. Vestibular neuritis produces continuous vertigo for days with spontaneous unidirectional nystagmus, not second-long positional spells. Ménière's disease causes episodic vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours accompanied by fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness, all absent here. Vestibular migraine can cause positional symptoms but requires a migraine history and typically headache, photophobia, or aura with attacks. Orthostatic hypotension produces lightheadedness on standing, not true rotational vertigo on rolling over in bed, and her orthostatic vitals were negative.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Meclizine 25 mg three times daily for two weeks
stages.explanation: The Epley canalith repositioning maneuver is first-line therapy for posterior canal BPPV: a sequence of head positions uses gravity to move displaced otoconia out of the posterior semicircular canal and back into the utricle. It resolves symptoms after one to three sessions in roughly 80% of patients and can be repeated immediately if nystagmus persists. Scheduled meclizine is inferior — vestibular suppressants do not remove the otoconia, blunt central compensation, and cause sedation and falls in older adults; at most a dose may be used briefly for severe nausea. Corticosteroids have a role in vestibular neuritis, not BPPV. Sodium restriction with a thiazide is maintenance therapy for Ménière's disease and is irrelevant to a mechanical canalithiasis problem.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her vertigo resolves after two repositioning sessions. Three months later she returns with identical brief spells on rolling to the right, and Dix-Hallpike again shows the same fatigable right posterior canal response. What is the best next step?
options: MRI brain with internal auditory canal protocol
Referral for posterior semicircular canal occlusion surgery
Repeat the Epley canalith repositioning maneuver
stages.explanation: BPPV recurs in roughly 15–50% of patients over the following years, and recurrence with the identical, classic positional response is managed the same way as the first episode — repeat canalith repositioning, which remains highly effective. Teaching the patient a home repositioning program (self-Epley or Brandt-Daroff exercises) is a reasonable adjunct. MRI is unnecessary because the recurrence is stereotyped, fatigable, and peripheral in pattern; imaging is for atypical, persistent, or central-appearing nystagmus. Canal occlusion surgery is a last resort reserved for rare, truly intractable BPPV that has failed many properly performed repositioning attempts over a prolonged period. Indefinite daily meclizine does not treat the underlying canalithiasis, impairs compensation, and adds anticholinergic and sedative risk in a 58-year-old.

cases.diagnosis: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (right posterior semicircular canal)
pearls: Vertigo lasting under a minute and triggered by rolling over or head extension is BPPV until proven otherwise — diagnose it with Dix-Hallpike, not MRI.
Posterior canal BPPV shows latency, upbeating–torsional nystagmus toward the dependent ear, and fatigability on repeat testing.
The Epley maneuver treats the cause by repositioning otoconia and beats any medication; vestibular suppressants delay central compensation.
Recurrence is common (15–50%) and is simply retreated with repositioning; teach patients a home program.

cases.title: Spinning that will not stop
cases.chiefComplaint: Triage note: constant room-spinning with repeated vomiting since this morning, unable to walk straight.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents with 18 hours of continuous, severe spinning vertigo. It built over about two hours while he was at work and has not remitted since; any head movement makes it dramatically worse, but it persists even when he lies perfectly still. He has vomited four times and walks 'like a drunk,' veering to the right. He recovered from an upper respiratory infection about a week ago. He denies any change in hearing, tinnitus, or ear fullness, and denies headache, neck pain, double vision, slurred speech, trouble swallowing, facial droop, numbness, or limb weakness. He has never had similar episodes, and nothing like this is triggered by position alone — it simply never stops. Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. He smokes one pack daily (20 pack-years), drinks socially, and uses no recreational drugs. He takes no anticoagulants and has no known drug allergies. Family history: father had a myocardial infarction at 62.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, clutching an emesis basin, prefers to keep his eyes closed. Spontaneous horizontal nystagmus with the fast phase beating left in primary gaze, intensifying on leftward gaze and lessening on rightward gaze; it visibly dampens with visual fixation. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Speech normal; face symmetric with intact sensation; palate elevates symmetrically. Strength 5/5 in all limbs; no dysmetria on finger-to-nose. He stands unassisted but veers to the right when walking. No Horner syndrome, no limb ataxia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with an acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo, nystagmus, head-motion intolerance, and gait unsteadiness), what is the best next step to distinguish a peripheral from a central cause?
options: Immediate MRI of the brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
Three-step HINTS examination (head impulse, nystagmus, test of skew)
stages.explanation: For the acute vestibular syndrome, a properly performed HINTS examination by a trained clinician is the most accurate immediate way to separate peripheral vestibular failure from a posterior-circulation stroke — it outperforms even early MRI, since diffusion-weighted imaging is falsely negative in up to 15–20% of posterior fossa infarcts within the first 24–48 hours. Non-contrast CT is very insensitive for acute posterior fossa ischemia and serves mainly to exclude hemorrhage. Immediate MRI is reasonable when HINTS is central or unreliable, but a normal early MRI cannot exclude stroke and should not precede the bedside assessment. Dix-Hallpike is designed for brief, episodic positional vertigo, not continuous vertigo, and can be misleading here. Audiometry is not urgent in a patient without auditory complaints.

stages.label: BEDSIDE TESTING
stages.content:
Head impulse test: corrective catch-up saccade with rapid head rotation to the right; normal (no saccade) with rotation to the left
Nystagmus: unidirectional, left-beating in all gaze positions, intensifies on leftward gaze, suppresses with visual fixation
Test of skew: no vertical deviation on alternate cover testing
Finger-rub hearing: intact bilaterally
Point-of-care glucose: 108 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: All three HINTS components are reassuringly peripheral: an abnormal head impulse toward the right (a catch-up saccade indicating a failed right vestibulo-ocular reflex), strictly unidirectional nystagmus that suppresses with fixation, and no skew deviation. Combined with the recent viral illness, intact hearing, and an otherwise nonfocal examination, this is right vestibular neuritis — the nystagmus beats away from the hypofunctioning side and he veers toward it. A lateral medullary infarct typically shows the dangerous HINTS pattern (normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew) plus crossed sensory loss, Horner syndrome, dysphagia, or hoarseness, all absent. Labyrinthitis is the same syndrome plus acute hearing loss, which he does not have. BPPV causes seconds-long positional spells, not 18 hours of continuous vertigo. Ménière's attacks last 20 minutes to 12 hours with auditory symptoms.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Intravenous alteplase within the thrombolysis window
Scheduled meclizine around the clock for four weeks
Brief symptomatic antiemetics and vestibular suppressants (≤72 hours), early mobilization with vestibular rehabilitation, and consideration of a short corticosteroid taper
stages.explanation: Vestibular neuritis is managed supportively: antiemetics and vestibular suppressants (meclizine, dimenhydrinate, or benzodiazepines) for no more than about 72 hours to control the worst symptoms, followed by early mobilization and vestibular rehabilitation exercises, which drive the central compensation that produces recovery. Many clinicians add a short corticosteroid taper if started early, which may speed recovery of vestibular function, though long-term benefit is debated. Thrombolysis is not indicated — the bedside examination just demonstrated a peripheral lesion, not an infarct. Four weeks of scheduled meclizine is actively harmful because prolonged vestibular suppression delays the central compensation the brain needs and prolongs disability. Decompressive surgery is a treatment for large cerebellar infarcts with swelling and brainstem compression, a diagnosis that has been excluded clinically.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: Which finding, had it been present, would have mandated immediate MRI and a full stroke evaluation?
options: A normal (bilaterally intact) head impulse test in this continuously vertiginous patient
Nystagmus that suppressed with visual fixation
More than three episodes of vomiting
Veering to one side while still able to walk unassisted
stages.explanation: In acute vestibular syndrome, a normal head impulse test is paradoxically alarming: if the vestibulo-ocular reflex is intact on both sides, peripheral vestibular failure cannot explain the continuous vertigo, so the lesion is presumed central (most often a PICA-territory cerebellar or brainstem infarct) until MRI proves otherwise. The other dangerous HINTS findings are direction-changing (gaze-evoked) nystagmus and skew deviation; severe truncal ataxia with inability to stand or walk is an additional red flag. Duration beyond 24 hours and repeated vomiting are typical of vestibular neuritis itself and carry no localizing value. Fixation suppression of nystagmus is a peripheral feature — central nystagmus persists despite fixation. Veering toward the affected side while remaining ambulatory is the expected peripheral pattern, in contrast to the profound inability to stand seen with cerebellar stroke.

cases.diagnosis: Right vestibular neuritis
pearls: In acute vestibular syndrome, HINTS by a trained examiner beats early MRI-DWI for excluding posterior-circulation stroke; CT is nearly useless for posterior fossa ischemia.
An abnormal head impulse (catch-up saccade) is reassuring — it localizes the problem to the peripheral vestibular apparatus.
Dangerous signs: normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, skew deviation, or inability to stand — any one mandates MRI.
Limit vestibular suppressants to about 72 hours; early vestibular rehabilitation drives the central compensation that produces recovery.

cases.title: Hours of spinning with a roaring, full ear
cases.chiefComplaint: I get attacks where the room spins for hours while my left ear roars and feels blocked.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman describes four attacks of vertigo over the past six months.
