Each begins with pressure and fullness in the left ear and a low-pitched roaring tinnitus, followed by intense spinning lasting two to four hours with nausea and vomiting that forces her to lie still until it passes. During attacks her hearing on the left becomes noticeably muffled; it improves afterward, though she feels the left ear has never returned fully to baseline. Attacks are not triggered by position change, and between them she is largely well apart from intermittent left tinnitus. She denies headache with the attacks, visual aura, photophobia, weakness, numbness, or slurred speech. She had migraines without aura in her twenties that resolved. Past medical history: hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 µg daily. She eats a high-salt diet and drinks three coffees daily. She does not smoke and drinks wine on weekends. No drug allergies. No family history of hearing loss.
cases.exam: Comfortable and in no distress; examined between attacks. Otoscopy shows normal, mobile tympanic membranes bilaterally without effusion. Weber testing at 512 Hz lateralizes to the right ear; Rinne shows air conduction greater than bone conduction bilaterally — together consistent with a left sensorineural hearing loss. There is no spontaneous or gaze-evoked nystagmus, and Dix-Hallpike testing is negative on both sides. The remaining cranial nerves are intact, including facial strength, sensation, and corneal reflexes. There is no limb dysmetria on finger-to-nose; gait and tandem gait are normal, and Romberg testing is negative. No facial droop or limb weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which test is the most appropriate next step in her evaluation?
options: CT of the temporal bones without contrast
stages.explanation: Episodic vertigo lasting hours with unilateral aural fullness, roaring tinnitus, and fluctuating hearing strongly suggests Ménière's disease, and formal audiometry is the essential next step: documenting low- to mid-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear is required by diagnostic criteria and establishes a baseline for following the disease. The bedside tuning-fork findings already hint at left sensorineural loss, but audiometry quantifies it. Carotid ultrasound evaluates anterior-circulation disease and has no role in episodic vertigo with auditory symptoms. CT of the temporal bones shows bony anatomy — useful for cholesteatoma or superior canal dehiscence — but cannot demonstrate endolymphatic hydrops or quantify hearing. Tilt-table testing addresses syncope and orthostatic intolerance, not true rotational vertigo accompanied by unilateral cochlear symptoms.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Pure-tone audiogram: left ear 45 dB sensorineural loss at 250–500 Hz rising (improving) to 20 dB at 2–8 kHz — a low-frequency, up-sloping sensorineural pattern; right ear within normal limits
Speech discrimination: 84% left, 100% right
Tympanometry: type A curves bilaterally
MRI brain with internal auditory canal protocol (obtained to exclude a retrocochlear lesion): no vestibular schwannoma or other abnormality

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ménière's disease of the left ear
stages.explanation: She meets criteria for definite Ménière's disease: two or more spontaneous vertigo attacks lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours, audiometrically documented low- to mid-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear, and fluctuating aural symptoms (fullness, tinnitus, hearing change) in that same ear, with other causes excluded — the normal MRI rules out a retrocochlear lesion. The underlying pathology is endolymphatic hydrops. Vestibular migraine is the best mimic, but her attacks lack headache, photophobia, or aura, and migraine does not produce documented unilateral low-frequency hearing loss. Vestibular schwannoma causes slowly progressive unilateral hearing loss and imbalance rather than discrete hours-long vertigo attacks, and the MRI was normal. BPPV produces seconds-long positional spells without any auditory symptoms, and her Dix-Hallpike was negative.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Sodium restriction (under 2 g/day) with caffeine and alcohol moderation, plus a trial of a thiazide diuretic or betahistine
stages.explanation: First-line management of Ménière's disease is conservative: sodium restriction and moderation of caffeine and alcohol aim to limit fluctuations in endolymph volume, and a thiazide diuretic (or betahistine, widely used in Europe) is added to reduce attack frequency. Most patients achieve meaningful control with these measures, and her high-salt, high-caffeine diet offers an obvious target. Intratympanic gentamicin is an ablative chemical labyrinthectomy that risks permanent hearing loss; it is reserved for refractory disease after conservative and intermediate options fail. Endolymphatic sac surgery is likewise not a first step and has contested efficacy. Daily meclizine indefinitely treats neither the hydrops nor the attack frequency, sedates, and impairs vestibular compensation; brief use during an acute attack is its only sensible role.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: One year later, despite documented adherence to the low-salt diet and a thiazide, she still has monthly disabling attacks. Hearing in the left ear remains serviceable. What is the most appropriate next step?
stages.explanation: For Ménière's disease refractory to lifestyle change and diuretics, the usual escalation is intratympanic therapy, and because her hearing is still serviceable, intratympanic corticosteroid is the preferred next step — it reduces vertigo attacks without the ototoxic risk of gentamicin. Intratympanic gentamicin is more effective for vertigo control but causes hearing loss in a meaningful fraction of patients, so it is held in reserve when hearing is already poor or steroids fail. Surgical labyrinthectomy destroys all hearing in the ear and is a last resort for nonserviceable hearing with intractable attacks. Cochlear implantation addresses profound bilateral hearing loss, not vertigo. Carbamazepine treats trigeminal neuralgia and some paroxysmal neurologic disorders; it has no established role in Ménière's disease.

cases.diagnosis: Ménière's disease (left ear)
pearls: Definite Ménière's = two or more vertigo attacks lasting 20 minutes to 12 hours + audiometric low-frequency sensorineural loss + fluctuating aural symptoms in the same ear.
Audiometry is the key diagnostic test; MRI is obtained to exclude vestibular schwannoma as the retrocochlear mimic.
First-line therapy is salt, caffeine, and alcohol restriction with a thiazide or betahistine; escalate stepwise to intratympanic steroid, then gentamicin.
Ablative treatments (gentamicin, labyrinthectomy) trade hearing for vertigo control — reserve them for refractory disease, gauging hearing serviceability first.

cases.title: Foggy and headachy since Friday's game
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother reports: 'He took a big hit Friday night and he's been headachy and in a fog ever since.'
cases.history: A 17-year-old varsity linebacker presents three days after a helmet-to-helmet collision in Friday night's game. Teammates report he was never unconscious but appeared dazed, was slow to get up, and was confused about the play for several minutes; the athletic trainer removed him from the game. Since then he describes a dull, global headache (4–6/10) worse with screen time, mental fogginess and slowed thinking, sensitivity to light and noise, irritability, and sleeping two extra hours nightly. He vomited once the first night but not since, and overall his symptoms are slowly improving. He denies worsening headache, neck pain, seizures, focal weakness, numbness, slurred speech, or vision loss. This is his first concussion. He has no medical problems, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He is an honors student and denies alcohol or drug use. His mother has migraines. He is eager to play in next week's playoff game.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, GCS 15, fully oriented. Pupils equal and reactive; fundi without papilledema. Extraocular movements full, though smooth pursuit and rapid horizontal saccades mildly provoke his headache. Face symmetric; hearing intact. Strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes 2+ and symmetric, plantar responses flexor. Finger-to-nose accurate. Tandem gait performed slowly but accurately. Cervical spine nontender with full, painless range of motion. No scalp hematoma or bony tenderness.

stages.label: IMAGING DECISION
stages.question: Three days after the injury, with improving symptoms and a normal neurologic examination, what is the most appropriate approach to neuroimaging?
options: MRI of the brain without contrast
No neuroimaging — make the diagnosis clinically
stages.explanation: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis of functional brain disturbance; conventional CT and MRI are normal by definition, so imaging serves only to exclude structural injury when red flags are present. Validated decision rules (PECARN in children, the Canadian CT Head Rule in adults) target findings such as GCS below 15, focal deficit, repeated vomiting, progressively worsening headache, post-traumatic seizure, or signs of skull fracture — none of which he has three days out, with symptoms already improving. CT here adds radiation to a developing brain without diagnostic yield. MRI is reserved for prolonged, worsening, or atypical recoveries. Skull radiographs are obsolete in modern head-injury evaluation because they neither show the brain nor change management.

stages.label: COGNITIVE & VESTIBULAR SCREEN
stages.content:
SCAT-6 symptom evaluation: 11 of 22 symptoms endorsed, severity 28/132 (headache, fogginess, and light sensitivity predominate)
Immediate recall: 4/5 words; delayed recall: 3/5 words
Months-in-reverse: completed with one error
Modified balance error scoring system (mBESS): 4 errors
Vestibular/ocular motor screen: mild symptom provocation with smooth pursuit and horizontal saccades
No red flags identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A biomechanical head impact followed immediately by transient confusion, then days of headache, fogginess, photophobia, phonophobia, sleep change, and irritability — with a normal neurologic examination and abnormal symptom/cognitive/balance screening — is the classic picture of concussion, a functional (metabolic) brain injury without macroscopic structural damage. An epidural hematoma declares itself within hours with deteriorating consciousness, not three days of slow improvement in a GCS 15 patient. Cervicogenic headache requires cervical findings — his neck is nontender with full painless motion. New-onset migraine cannot account for the temporal link to head trauma, the cognitive slowing, or the balance errors; his mother's migraine history is relevant only as a risk factor for somewhat longer concussion recovery, not as an alternative diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Strict rest in a dark room ('cocooning') until completely symptom-free
Immediate return to non-contact practice to maintain conditioning
24–48 hours of relative rest, then stepwise reintroduction of school and light aerobic activity kept below the symptom-exacerbation threshold
Start daily amitriptyline for headache prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Current consensus guidance recommends only 24–48 hours of relative physical and cognitive rest, after which patients should gradually resume daily activities, school (with temporary accommodations such as reduced screen time and rest breaks), and light aerobic exercise, keeping intensity below the level that more than mildly worsens symptoms. Early sub-symptom-threshold aerobic activity actually speeds recovery. Strict prolonged 'cocoon' rest is outdated and associated with longer recovery, deconditioning, and mood symptoms. Returning straight to practice skips the mandated progression and risks re-injury while the brain is metabolically vulnerable. Amitriptyline is sometimes used for persistent post-traumatic headache lasting weeks to months, not for a typical improving headache three days after injury.

stages.label: RETURN TO PLAY
stages.question: He asks when he can play in the playoff game. When may he return to full-contact football?
options: After completing a stepwise return-to-sport progression, remaining symptom-free at each stage (each lasting at least 24 hours), with final medical clearance
Exactly seven days after the injury, regardless of symptoms
As soon as ibuprofen controls the headache
After a normal CT scan of the head
stages.explanation: Return to play follows a graded progression — symptom-limited activity, light aerobic exercise, sport-specific drills, non-contact training, full-contact practice, then competition — with each stage separated by at least 24 hours and any symptom recurrence sending the athlete back a step. Full contact requires being symptom-free off medications, back to baseline at school, and cleared by a healthcare professional; no athlete returns to play the same day as a concussion. A fixed seven-day clock ignores individual recovery, which is often longer in adolescents. Masking headache with ibuprofen does not mean the brain has recovered, and 'symptom-free' must be assessed off analgesics. A normal CT is expected in concussion and says nothing about functional recovery, so it can never serve as a clearance test.

cases.diagnosis: Concussion (mild traumatic brain injury)
pearls: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis — CT/MRI are normal; image only for red flags identified by decision rules such as PECARN or the Canadian CT Head Rule.
Prescribe 24–48 hours of relative rest, then graded sub-symptom-threshold activity; strict prolonged rest delays recovery.
Return to learn precedes return to sport; the stepwise return-to-play progression requires ≥24 hours per stage and medical clearance, with no same-day return.
Premature return risks prolonged recovery and the rare but catastrophic second-impact syndrome in adolescents.

cases.title: Growing confusion and a shuffling gait
cases.chiefComplaint: His daughter reports: 'Dad has been confused and shuffling for two weeks, and today he couldn't find his words.'
cases.history: An 82-year-old man is brought in by his daughter for two to three weeks of progressive confusion, daytime drowsiness, and a slow, shuffling gait with two near-falls. Today she noticed word-finding pauses and that he could not operate the television remote. He admits to a mild, dull headache for about a week. Roughly five weeks ago he tripped on a rug and bumped his head on a doorframe — there was no loss of consciousness and he 'seemed fine,' so no care was sought. There has been no fever, neck stiffness, vomiting, or witnessed seizure, and no new incontinence. Past medical history: atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg twice daily, hypertension on amlodipine 10 mg daily, and benign prostatic hyperplasia on tamsulosin. He does not drink alcohol, is a retired accountant, lives with his daughter, and was independent in all activities until this month. No known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable to voice; oriented to person and place but not date. Speech fluent with word-finding pauses. Pupils equal and reactive; no papilledema. Mild right pronator drift with right-sided strength 4+/5, left 5/5. Reflexes brisker on the right with an upgoing right plantar response. Sensation grossly intact. Gait slow, shuffling, and wide-based, requiring one-person assistance. No neck stiffness. Heart irregularly irregular without murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
Immediate non-contrast CT of the head
Outpatient MRI of the brain with contrast next week
Serum B12, TSH, and referral for outpatient cognitive evaluation
stages.explanation: A subacutely confused anticoagulated 82-year-old with headache, a lateralizing examination (right pronator drift, asymmetric reflexes, upgoing right toe), and a head strike five weeks ago needs emergency neuroimaging, and non-contrast head CT is the fastest, most appropriate test — it readily demonstrates extra-axial blood, mass effect, and midline shift. Lumbar puncture before imaging is dangerous in a patient with possible mass effect and an anticoagulated state, risking herniation and spinal hematoma. Deferring to outpatient MRI delays diagnosis of a potentially reversible, enlarging intracranial lesion in someone deteriorating today. A metabolic dementia workup (B12, TSH) is reasonable later but cannot explain acute focal signs. EEG evaluates seizures and encephalopathy patterns but should never precede structural imaging when focal deficits point to a mass lesion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head non-contrast: 18-mm left frontoparietal crescent-shaped, mixed hypo- and isodense extra-axial collection layering over the convexity and crossing suture lines; 7-mm rightward midline shift; effacement of the left lateral ventricle
INR 1.2; platelets 212 ×10³/µL; hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL
Na 134 mEq/L; glucose 118 mg/dL; creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
ECG: atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 74

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: A crescent-shaped extra-axial collection that crosses suture lines is a subdural hematoma; its mixed hypo- and isodense appearance weeks after minor trauma indicates a chronic collection with components of different ages, classic in an elderly anticoagulated patient whose atrophic brain stretches fragile bridging veins so that trivial trauma tears them. The indolent course — weeks of fluctuating confusion, drowsiness, gait decline, and gradually emerging hemiparesis — fits the slow expansion of a chronic subdural. An epidural hematoma is biconvex, bounded by sutures, hyperacute, and arterial in tempo, evolving over hours. Normal-pressure hydrocephalus causes gait apraxia, cognitive slowing, and incontinence with ventriculomegaly, not an extra-axial collection with midline shift. An MCA infarct is intra-axial and abrupt in onset. Glioblastoma is an enhancing intraparenchymal mass, not a convexity collection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Intravenous vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
Resume apixaban once his symptoms stabilize
Hold apixaban, reverse anticoagulation with andexanet alfa or 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate, and obtain urgent neurosurgical consultation for evacuation
Mannitol infusion and observation in the emergency department
stages.explanation: A symptomatic 18-mm subdural hematoma with 7 mm of midline shift in a deteriorating patient requires holding and reversing the anticoagulant plus urgent neurosurgical consultation; collections of this size with shift and progressive deficits are treated with surgical evacuation, most often burr-hole drainage for chronic subdurals. Apixaban, a factor Xa inhibitor, is reversed with andexanet alfa or, where unavailable, 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate. Vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma reverse warfarin, not direct Xa inhibitors — a near-normal INR does not exclude a therapeutic apixaban effect. Resuming apixaban now would feed ongoing hemorrhage; reintroduction is deferred until after treatment and imaging stability, balanced against his stroke risk. Mannitol with passive observation temporizes intracranial pressure at best and ignores both the ongoing anticoagulant effect and the definitive surgical problem.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He undergoes burr-hole evacuation. Which perioperative measure has the strongest evidence for reducing recurrence of the hematoma?
options: Early mobilization within six hours of surgery
Placement of a subdural drain for 24–48 hours after evacuation
stages.explanation: Randomized trial evidence shows that leaving a subdural drain in place for 24–48 hours after burr-hole evacuation roughly halves recurrence and lowers six-month mortality, making it the standard of care wherever drain placement is feasible. Recurrence is otherwise common — about 10–20% — because the residual subdural space and friable neomembranes re-accumulate fluid. Early mobilization has not been shown to reduce recurrence; if anything, a brief period of recumbency is often favored, and mobilization timing is individualized. Prolonged seizure prophylaxis is not indicated after chronic subdural evacuation; when used at all, antiepileptics are short-course. Restarting apixaban at 24 hours invites re-bleeding into a fresh surgical cavity — resumption is typically deferred days to weeks, timed to imaging stability and the patient's thromboembolic risk from atrial fibrillation.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic subdural hematoma
pearls: In an elderly anticoagulated patient, weeks of fluctuating confusion, headache, and gait decline after minor (even forgotten) head trauma is a chronic subdural until CT proves otherwise.
Subdural blood is crescentic and crosses sutures; mixed densities mean bleeding of different ages. Bridging-vein tears in an atrophic brain are the mechanism.
A normal INR does not exclude a direct oral anticoagulant effect — reverse apixaban with andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC before surgery.
After burr-hole drainage, a subdural drain left for 24–48 hours significantly reduces recurrence and mortality.

cases.title: Knocked out, woke up, now fading fast
cases.chiefComplaint: EMS report: assault with a bat to the head, brief loss of consciousness, then talking, now increasingly drowsy.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man was struck on the left side of the head with a bat during an altercation about 90 minutes ago. Witnesses say he was knocked out for roughly a minute, then woke up, stood, and conversed normally — he even argued with bystanders and refused an ambulance at first. Over the past 30 minutes he has developed a severe, worsening left-sided headache, vomited twice, and become progressively confused and sleepy; EMS was called when he stopped answering questions appropriately. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and reports no anticoagulant use. He is not known to use illicit drugs. He drank two beers earlier in the evening. No known drug allergies. He has no prior surgeries and no bleeding disorder. Family history is unknown. He arrives on a backboard with cervical spine precautions in place.
cases.exam: GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5), drowsy and intermittently agitated. A boggy hematoma overlies the left temporal scalp. The left pupil is 5 mm and sluggishly reactive; the right pupil is 3 mm and brisk. There is a right pronator drift with right-sided weakness (4-/5) and an extensor right plantar response. Reflexes are brisker on the right. Neck is immobilized and not assessable for stiffness. No battle sign or raccoon eyes yet. Fundi without papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After airway, breathing, and circulation are addressed, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage
Emergent non-contrast CT of the head
MRI of the brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
Administer intravenous mannitol and observe for one hour
stages.explanation: A head-injured patient with a lucid interval who is now deteriorating, with a unilateral blown pupil and contralateral weakness, is showing early transtentorial (uncal) herniation from an expanding intracranial hematoma — this demands an emergent non-contrast head CT, which is fast, sensitive for acute blood, and directs immediate neurosurgical management. Lumbar puncture is absolutely contraindicated with a probable mass lesion and impending herniation; it risks precipitating fatal herniation. MRI takes far too long, is logistically impractical in an unstable, agitated trauma patient, and would dangerously delay surgery. Mannitol is a useful temporizing measure for raised intracranial pressure on the way to definitive care, but giving it and merely observing wastes the critical window — imaging and the neurosurgeon cannot wait an hour.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head non-contrast: 22-mm biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense extra-axial collection over the left temporoparietal convexity that does NOT cross the coronal suture; associated left temporal bone fracture
9-mm rightward midline shift with effacement of the left lateral ventricle and early left uncal herniation
No intraparenchymal hemorrhage; basal cisterns compressed
Coagulation profile: INR 1.0, platelets 245 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Epidural hematoma from middle meningeal artery injury
stages.explanation: A biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection that does not cross suture lines, paired with a temporal bone fracture, is the signature of an acute epidural hematoma — here from laceration of the middle meningeal artery, which runs in a groove on the inner table of the temporal bone and is torn by temporal fractures. The classic lucid interval followed by rapid arterial-pressure deterioration matches perfectly. A subdural hematoma is crescentic, crosses sutures, and is venous and slower. Diffuse axonal injury follows high-velocity acceleration-deceleration with coma out of proportion to a near-normal CT, not a focal lens-shaped clot. Traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage tracks within sulci and cisterns. A cerebral contusion is an intraparenchymal bruise, not an extra-axial lens-shaped collection bounded by sutures.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management?
options: Admission for serial neurologic checks and repeat CT in 6 hours
Levetiracetam loading and blood pressure control alone
Therapeutic hypothermia protocol in the ICU
stages.explanation: A large epidural hematoma (22 mm, >30 mL) with 9 mm of midline shift, a declining GCS, and clinical signs of herniation is a neurosurgical emergency requiring emergent craniotomy for clot evacuation and hemostasis of the bleeding middle meningeal artery. Promptly evacuated epidural hematomas, especially in young patients without underlying brain injury, carry an excellent prognosis. Watchful waiting with serial exams and delayed CT is appropriate only for small epidural hematomas in neurologically intact, stable patients — the opposite of this case — and here would be lethal. Seizure prophylaxis and blood pressure control are useful adjuncts but do not address the expanding clot. Therapeutic hypothermia has no role in evacuating a surgical mass lesion and only delays the operation he needs now.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While the operating room is being readied, his left pupil dilates further and his right-sided weakness worsens. Which temporizing measure is most appropriate to bridge him to surgery?
options: Aggressive hypoventilation to a PaCO2 of 25 mmHg sustained for hours
Head elevation to 30 degrees, brief controlled hyperventilation, and hyperosmolar therapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol)
Lowering the systolic blood pressure below 100 mmHg with nitroprusside
stages.explanation: While awaiting emergent evacuation, signs of worsening herniation are temporized by elevating the head of the bed to 30 degrees and keeping the neck midline to promote venous drainage, brief controlled hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of roughly 30–35 mmHg for transient cerebral vasoconstriction, and hyperosmolar therapy with hypertonic saline or mannitol to draw water out of the brain. These buy minutes to the operating room. Sustained aggressive hyperventilation to 25 mmHg causes excessive vasoconstriction and ischemia and is avoided except as a brief last resort. Hypotonic fluids worsen cerebral edema. Dropping the systolic pressure below 100 mmHg compromises cerebral perfusion pressure — his bradycardia and hypertension reflect the Cushing response to intracranial pressure, and overcorrecting blood pressure risks cerebral hypoperfusion.

cases.diagnosis: Epidural hematoma (middle meningeal artery)
pearls: Classic epidural hematoma = head trauma, brief loss of consciousness, lucid interval, then rapid deterioration as arterial blood accumulates.
On CT the clot is biconvex (lens-shaped) and does NOT cross suture lines; a temporal bone fracture tearing the middle meningeal artery is the usual cause.
A blown pupil with contralateral hemiparesis signals uncal herniation — get an emergent CT and the neurosurgeon simultaneously.
Definitive treatment is emergent craniotomy; promptly evacuated epidural hematomas in young patients have an excellent prognosis.

cases.title: Confused, unsteady, and seeing double
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by a shelter worker: 'He's confused, walking like he's drunk, and his eyes look crossed.'
cases.history: A 54-year-old man with long-standing alcohol use disorder is brought in by a shelter worker for several days of worsening confusion, unsteadiness, and double vision. He has reportedly been drinking up to a liter of vodka daily for years, eats poorly, and has lost weight; staff have not seen him eat a real meal in over a week. He is inattentive and a poor historian, giving inconsistent answers and at times confabulating. There is no reported head trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or seizure. Past medical history includes alcoholic liver disease and a prior episode of alcohol withdrawal. He takes no medications reliably and has no known drug allergies. He is homeless, smokes, and has no reliable family contact. On arrival the triage nurse, noting his disorientation and a fingerstick glucose of 58 mg/dL, asks whether she should start an ampule of D50 and a banana bag.
cases.exam: Thin, disheveled, inattentive, and disoriented to time and place; drowsy but arousable. Horizontal gaze-evoked nystagmus with bilateral partial abduction deficits (impaired bilateral lateral rectus function) and double vision on lateral gaze. Pupils equal and reactive. Marked truncal ataxia — he cannot sit unsupported or stand without falling — and dysmetria on heel-to-shin. Strength grossly 5/5; reflexes symmetric. No asterixis. No focal facial weakness. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The nurse is about to give intravenous dextrose for the glucose of 58 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Give intravenous dextrose immediately, then thiamine afterward
Administer intravenous thiamine before (or together with) the dextrose
Order an urgent non-contrast head CT before giving anything
Give intravenous lorazepam for presumed alcohol withdrawal first
stages.explanation: This is a thiamine-deficient alcoholic with the classic triad pointing to Wernicke encephalopathy, and the cardinal rule is to give thiamine before or together with any glucose. Administering dextrose to a thiamine-depleted patient consumes the last available thiamine as a cofactor for glucose metabolism and can precipitate or worsen Wernicke encephalopathy. Thiamine is given parenterally in high doses. Giving dextrose first and thiamine afterward inverts the safe sequence and risks harm. A head CT may follow to exclude other causes but must not delay immediate thiamine in a patient with this syndrome. Lorazepam treats withdrawal and may be needed later, but it neither addresses the thiamine deficiency nor explains the ophthalmoplegia and ataxia, and it could deepen his obtundation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose 58 mg/dL; Na 134 mEq/L; K 3.3 mEq/L; Mg 1.2 mg/dL (low); phosphate 2.1 mg/dL
AST 96 U/L; ALT 44 U/L; total bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL; INR 1.3
Ethanol level 210 mg/dL
CT head non-contrast: no hemorrhage, no mass, age-appropriate atrophy
MRI brain (later): symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the mammillary bodies and medial thalami

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The triad of encephalopathy (inattention, disorientation, confabulation), ophthalmoplegia (bilateral lateral rectus weakness with gaze-evoked nystagmus), and ataxia in a malnourished alcoholic is Wernicke encephalopathy, caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency. The MRI finding of symmetric signal change in the mammillary bodies and medial thalami is highly characteristic. Note that the full triad is present in only a minority of cases, so a low threshold to treat is essential. Hepatic encephalopathy causes confusion and asterixis but not ophthalmoplegia, and he has no asterixis. Pure alcohol intoxication does not produce a fixed bilateral abduction palsy or this MRI pattern. A single cerebellar stroke would not cause symmetric ocular and thalamic findings. Meningitis is unlikely without fever or meningismus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment regimen?
options: Oral thiamine 100 mg once daily with a multivitamin
High-dose intravenous thiamine (e.g., 500 mg three times daily) plus magnesium repletion, then continued thiamine
A single 100 mg intramuscular dose of thiamine on discharge
stages.explanation: Established Wernicke encephalopathy is treated with high-dose parenteral thiamine — a common regimen is 500 mg intravenously three times daily for several days — followed by lower maintenance dosing, because gastrointestinal absorption is unreliable and the consequence of undertreatment is permanent Korsakoff amnesia. Magnesium is a required cofactor for thiamine-dependent enzymes and must be repleted (his magnesium is low) or thiamine therapy may fail. Low-dose once-daily oral thiamine is grossly inadequate for acute Wernicke and will not reverse the deficit fast enough. A single intramuscular dose at discharge badly underdoses a neurologic emergency. IVIG and steroids treat inflammatory and autoimmune conditions and have no role here; this is a nutritional deficiency reversed by repleting the missing vitamin.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite delayed recognition, he survives but is left with a profound deficit. Which residual syndrome is he most at risk of developing?
options: Korsakoff syndrome with anterograde amnesia and confabulation
stages.explanation: Untreated or undertreated Wernicke encephalopathy can progress to Korsakoff syndrome, a largely irreversible chronic amnestic disorder marked by profound anterograde (and variable retrograde) memory loss with confabulation, reflecting permanent damage to the mammillary bodies and the diencephalic memory circuits — the very structures abnormal on his MRI. This is why prompt, adequate thiamine is mandatory. Locked-in syndrome results from a ventral pontine lesion (often basilar thrombosis), not thiamine deficiency. Central pontine myelinolysis follows overly rapid correction of hyponatremia; his sodium is near normal and stable. Parkinsonism arises from nigrostriatal dopaminergic degeneration or toxins and is not a sequela of Wernicke encephalopathy. The Wernicke-to-Korsakoff continuum is the high-yield association tested here.

cases.diagnosis: Wernicke encephalopathy
pearls: Wernicke triad = encephalopathy + ophthalmoplegia + ataxia, but the full triad appears in a minority — treat on suspicion in any malnourished or alcoholic patient.
Always give thiamine before or with glucose; dextrose alone can precipitate Wernicke in a thiamine-deficient patient.
Treat established Wernicke with high-dose IV thiamine and replete magnesium, an essential cofactor; oral dosing is inadequate acutely.
Undertreated Wernicke progresses to irreversible Korsakoff amnesia with confabulation — damage centers on the mammillary bodies and medial thalami.

cases.title: Sudden droopy lid and double vision with a headache
cases.chiefComplaint: My right eyelid dropped and I'm seeing double, and I have a bad headache behind that eye.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with the sudden onset, about four hours ago, of a drooping right eyelid, horizontal and vertical double vision, and a severe boring ache behind the right eye and in the right temple. The diplopia worsens when he tries to look in most directions, and he noticed the right pupil looks larger than the left. He denies trauma, fever, neck stiffness, weakness, numbness, slurred speech, or prior similar episodes. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin (last A1c 7.8%) and hypertension on lisinopril; he also has hyperlipidemia. He smokes half a pack daily for 30 years and drinks alcohol occasionally. No known drug allergies. He takes no anticoagulants. Family history includes a sister with a 'brain aneurysm' repaired in her fifties. He is anxious and holding the right side of his head.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Marked ptosis of the right upper lid. The right eye is deviated 'down and out' at rest. There are deficits of adduction, elevation, and depression of the right eye, with preserved abduction. The right pupil is 6 mm and poorly reactive to light; the left pupil is 3 mm and briskly reactive. Right-sided periorbital pain. Visual acuity and fundoscopy are normal. The remaining cranial nerves, strength, sensation, coordination, and gait are normal. No meningismus, no facial weakness, no Horner syndrome.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a painful right third-nerve palsy with a fixed, dilated pupil, what is the single most important next step?
options: Reassure and arrange outpatient follow-up, assuming a diabetic (microvascular) nerve palsy
Check an erythrocyte sedimentation rate and start high-dose steroids for giant cell arteritis
Emergent CT angiography (or CT plus CT angiography) of the head to evaluate for an intracranial aneurysm
stages.explanation: A third-nerve palsy that involves the pupil — especially when painful and acute — must be treated as a posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing the nerve until proven otherwise, and the next step is emergent vascular imaging, typically CT angiography of the head (often with non-contrast CT to look for subarachnoid blood). The pupillomotor fibers travel on the outer surface of the third nerve and are first damaged by external compression, so pupil involvement flags a surgical aneurysm at risk of catastrophic rupture. Assuming a benign diabetic palsy is dangerous precisely because microvascular palsies typically spare the pupil. Giant cell arteritis causes ischemic ophthalmoplegia and visual loss but not an isolated pupil-involving third-nerve palsy as the lead finding. Myasthenia never affects the pupil, so a tensilon test misses the emergency.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head non-contrast: no acute hemorrhage or infarct
CT angiography of the head: a 7-mm saccular aneurysm at the junction of the right posterior communicating artery and the internal carotid artery, abutting the cisternal segment of the right oculomotor nerve
No evidence of subarachnoid hemorrhage
Point-of-care glucose 162 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ischemic (microvascular) oculomotor nerve palsy from diabetes
Compressive oculomotor nerve palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
stages.explanation: The imaging confirms a posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing the right oculomotor nerve, producing a pupil-involving, painful third-nerve palsy. The pupil is dilated and unreactive because superficial parasympathetic fibers are crushed by extrinsic compression. A diabetic microvascular palsy classically spares the pupil because it infarcts the central somatic fibers while the peripherally located pupillary fibers retain their vasa nervorum blood supply — and his pupil is clearly involved. Myasthenia produces fatigable ptosis and diplopia but never pupillary abnormality. Cavernous sinus pathology usually involves multiple cranial nerves (III, IV, V1, VI) and proptosis, not an isolated third-nerve palsy. A Weber midbrain infarct gives an ipsilateral third-nerve palsy with contralateral hemiparesis, which he does not have.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent neurosurgical/endovascular referral for aneurysm securing (clipping or coiling)
Tight glycemic control and observation, expecting spontaneous recovery in 3 months
Therapeutic anticoagulation with a heparin infusion
stages.explanation: An unruptured posterior communicating artery aneurysm that is symptomatic — here compressing the third nerve and causing pain — is at high risk of rupture and warrants urgent treatment to secure it, either by endovascular coiling or microsurgical clipping, with prompt neurosurgical and neurointerventional involvement. Securing the aneurysm both prevents subarachnoid hemorrhage and often allows the nerve palsy to recover. Glycemic control and watchful waiting apply to pupil-sparing microvascular palsies, not to a confirmed compressive aneurysm, and would risk a fatal bleed. Corticosteroids treat inflammatory and giant cell arteritis-related processes, not an aneurysm. Anticoagulation is contraindicated — it does nothing for the aneurysm and would be dangerous if the aneurysm were to rupture, increasing hemorrhage severity.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: Had imaging been unremarkable and his palsy been deemed microvascular, which feature would have most reassured you that the cause was ischemic rather than compressive?
options: A normal-sized, briskly reactive pupil (pupil sparing)
Involvement of the inferior rectus causing an elevation deficit
stages.explanation: Pupil sparing — a normal-sized, briskly reactive pupil despite weakness of the extraocular muscles supplied by the third nerve — is the hallmark that most reassures against a compressive aneurysm and favors an ischemic microvascular palsy, common in diabetics and hypertensives. The peripherally situated pupillary fibers keep their blood supply during microvascular infarction of the nerve's core, so the pupil stays normal. Pain is unhelpful for localization because both ischemic and aneurysmal palsies can be painful. Ptosis and deficits of adduction, elevation, and depression occur in third-nerve palsy of any cause and do not distinguish the two. The pupil rule is the high-yield discriminator: pupil-involving palsies demand emergent vascular imaging, whereas truly pupil-sparing complete palsies in vasculopaths can often be observed for spontaneous recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Compressive oculomotor (CN III) palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm
pearls: A pupil-involving third-nerve palsy is a posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise — get emergent CT/CT angiography.
Superficial pupillary fibers are crushed by external compression (dilated pupil) but spared in microvascular infarction (pupil sparing).
The eye sits 'down and out' with ptosis because unopposed lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV) remain active.
Symptomatic aneurysms need urgent clipping or coiling; pupil-sparing microvascular palsies in vasculopaths usually recover over about 3 months.

cases.title: Droopy lid and a dry, sunken eye with arm pain
cases.chiefComplaint: My right eyelid is droopy, that pupil looks small, and my right arm and shoulder have been aching for weeks.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man presents with several weeks of a subtly droopy right upper eyelid and a noticeably smaller right pupil that his wife pointed out in photos. He also reports a deep, aching pain in the right shoulder, medial arm, and hand, with some tingling along the inner forearm and weakness of grip. He notes that the right side of his face does not seem to sweat. Over three months he has had a nagging cough, a 15-pound unintentional weight loss, and drenching night sweats. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. Past medical history includes COPD on a tiotropium inhaler and hypertension. He drinks beer on weekends and has no drug allergies. He worked in construction. Family history: father died of lung cancer. He has not had hemoptysis or fever.
cases.exam: Thin man. Right eye shows mild ptosis of the upper lid and slight elevation of the lower lid, with a 2-mm right pupil versus a 4-mm left pupil; the anisocoria is greater in dim light, and both pupils react to light. The right hemiface appears dry. Extraocular movements full; visual acuity normal. There is wasting of the right intrinsic hand muscles with grip weakness and reduced sensation along the medial forearm and fifth digit (lower trunk/C8–T1 distribution). Supraclavicular fullness on the right. Lungs with diminished breath sounds at the right apex. No lymphadenopathy elsewhere.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His ptosis, miosis, and facial anhidrosis are most consistent with which localization?
stages.explanation: Ptosis (mild, from loss of the sympathetically innervated Müller muscle), miosis with anisocoria worse in dim light, and facial anhidrosis together define Horner syndrome, a disruption of the oculosympathetic pathway. The miosis worsening in the dark reflects a sympathetic dilation failure, and the small pupil still reacts to light because the parasympathetic constrictor is intact. A third-nerve palsy produces the opposite pupil — a dilated, poorly reactive one — with a 'down and out' eye and marked ptosis. Myasthenia causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but never affects the pupil or sweating. Physiologic anisocoria shows an equal pupil-size difference in light and dark with no ptosis or anhidrosis. An Adie tonic pupil is large and poorly reactive with light-near dissociation, again the opposite of this small pupil.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Considering his smoking history, weight loss, apical findings, and lower brachial plexus signs, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: MRI of the brain with contrast
CT (or contrast-enhanced imaging) of the chest focused on the lung apex and thoracic outlet
Topical cocaine eye drop testing only, then reassurance
stages.explanation: A Horner syndrome combined with ipsilateral lower brachial plexus signs (C8–T1 pain, intrinsic hand wasting, medial forearm sensory loss), an apical lung abnormality, weight loss, and heavy smoking is a Pancoast (superior sulcus) tumor until proven otherwise, so chest imaging directed at the lung apex and thoracic outlet is the key next step to find the mass invading the sympathetic chain and lower plexus. Brain MRI evaluates central causes but would miss the apical tumor responsible here. Carotid ultrasound targets carotid dissection — a cause of painful Horner syndrome, but it does not explain the plexopathy or constitutional symptoms. A tensilon test addresses myasthenia, which spares the pupil and sweating. Pharmacologic eye drops can confirm and localize Horner syndrome, but in this high-risk presentation they must not delay the imaging that finds a treatable malignancy.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
CT chest with contrast: 4.2-cm spiculated mass in the right lung apex (superior sulcus) invading the adjacent first and second ribs and extending into the lower brachial plexus and sympathetic chain
No distant metastases on staging PET-CT; mediastinal nodes not enlarged
CT- guided core biopsy: non-small cell lung carcinoma (squamous cell type)
MRI thoracic inlet: tumor abutting the C8 and T1 nerve roots without spinal canal extension

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the unifying diagnosis?
options: Pancoast (superior sulcus) tumor causing Horner syndrome
Cervical rib with thoracic outlet syndrome
stages.explanation: The biopsy-proven apical non-small cell carcinoma invading the lower brachial plexus, ribs, and sympathetic chain unifies every finding: the Horner syndrome (sympathetic chain involvement), the C8–T1 plexopathy (arm and hand pain, intrinsic wasting, medial sensory loss), and the constitutional symptoms — a Pancoast tumor. Carotid dissection can cause a painful Horner syndrome but not apical lung mass, plexopathy, or weight loss. Wallenberg syndrome is a lateral medullary stroke producing a central Horner syndrome with crossed sensory loss, ataxia, and dysphagia, not a lung mass. A cervical rib can cause thoracic outlet syndrome with lower plexus signs but neither a malignant apical mass nor constitutional symptoms. Parsonage-Turner neuritis causes acute painful patchy arm weakness without Horner syndrome or an invasive apical tumor.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After tissue diagnosis, what is the most appropriate next step in his oncologic management?
options: Complete staging (brain MRI, PET-CT, mediastinal nodal assessment) and multidisciplinary planning, typically concurrent chemoradiation followed by surgery for resectable disease
Palliative care referral only, with no further oncologic treatment
Empiric antibiotics for presumed apical pneumonia and repeat imaging in 6 weeks
stages.explanation: Once a superior sulcus non-small cell carcinoma is confirmed, management begins with complete staging — brain MRI, PET-CT, and mediastinal nodal evaluation — to define resectability, followed by multidisciplinary planning; for potentially resectable superior sulcus tumors the standard is neoadjuvant concurrent chemoradiation followed by surgical resection, which yields meaningful long-term survival. Single-modality surgery alone is inferior for these locally invasive apical tumors. Palliative-care-only is premature when staging shows no distant metastases and the tumor may be resectable after induction therapy. Empiric antibiotics for 'apical pneumonia' would be a dangerous error given a spiculated, rib-invading, biopsy-proven malignancy, delaying definitive cancer treatment. Accurate staging precedes any decision because the presence of metastatic or extensive nodal disease would change the entire treatment paradigm.

cases.diagnosis: Horner syndrome due to a Pancoast (superior sulcus) tumor
pearls: Horner syndrome = ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis; the small pupil reacts to light and the anisocoria is greater in dim light.
Horner syndrome with ipsilateral C8–T1 arm pain, hand wasting, and constitutional symptoms in a smoker points to a Pancoast tumor — image the lung apex.
A pupil-sparing small pupil (Horner) is the mirror image of the dilated pupil of a third-nerve palsy; sweating and pupil are spared in myasthenia.
Resectable superior sulcus non-small cell lung cancer is treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by surgery after thorough staging.

cases.title: Fever and worsening back pain with failing legs
cases.chiefComplaint: My back has been killing me for a week, I've had fevers, and now my legs are getting weak.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents with one week of severe, constant mid-to-low thoracic back pain that is worse at night and unrelieved by position. For two days he has had subjective fevers and chills, and since this morning he has noticed progressive weakness and heaviness in both legs with new difficulty starting his urine stream. He reports a band of numbness around his trunk. He injects heroin several times weekly and has used a shared needle recently; he has a history of skin abscesses. Past medical history includes hepatitis C and poorly controlled type 2 diabetes. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He smokes, does not drink heavily, and is unstably housed. He has had no recent trauma. On review he denies headache, neck stiffness, or chest pain. He appears uncomfortable and diaphoretic and cannot find a comfortable position on the gurney.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing and diaphoretic. Focal midline tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic spine. Track marks on both forearms. Lower-extremity strength 4-/5 proximally and 4/5 distally bilaterally and worsening over hours; tone mildly increased. Hyperreflexia at the knees and ankles with bilateral extensor plantar responses (upper motor neuron pattern). A sensory level to pinprick around T8–T9. Palpable bladder with post-void residual on bedside scan. Upper extremities normal. No meningismus. No spinal deformity or overlying skin breakdown.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given fever, focal spinal pain, and an evolving myelopathy in an injection drug user, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Plain radiographs of the thoracolumbar spine
Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
Emergent MRI of the whole spine with and without contrast
CT of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast
stages.explanation: Fever, focal spinal tenderness, and a progressive myelopathy with a sensory level and bladder involvement in an injection drug user is a spinal epidural abscess until excluded, and emergent MRI of the entire spine with and without gadolinium is the diagnostic test of choice — it defines the abscess, the extent of cord compression, and skip lesions that occur in up to 15% of cases, which is why the whole spine is imaged. Plain films are insensitive early and cannot show the epidural collection or cord. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated because the needle may traverse and seed the abscess and provides little that changes acute management. CT of the abdomen and pelvis does not evaluate the cord. Electrodiagnostics are far too slow for a time-critical compressive emergency and add nothing acutely.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with left shift; ESR 92 mm/hr; CRP 188 mg/L
Blood glucose 268 mg/dL; HbA1c 10.2%
Blood cultures x2 drawn (results pending)
MRI whole spine with contrast: rim-enhancing posterior epidural collection from T7 to T9 compressing the spinal cord, with adjacent T8–T9 vertebral osteomyelitis and discitis; cord signal change at the compressed segment
Procalcitonin elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Spinal epidural abscess with vertebral osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: A rim-enhancing epidural collection compressing the cord with adjacent vertebral osteomyelitis and discitis, on a background of fever, markedly elevated inflammatory markers, injection drug use, and diabetes, is a spinal epidural abscess — the classic clinical triad is fever, back pain, and neurologic deficit, though all three are present in a minority at first contact. Transverse myelitis is non-infectious and would not produce a rim-enhancing epidural pus collection with bone involvement. Metastatic epidural compression causes pain and myelopathy but typically shows a tumor mass and bony metastasis without fever, leukocytosis, or an abscess, and he has no cancer history. Guillain-Barré is a lower motor neuron, areflexic ascending paralysis without fever, sensory level, or epidural mass — his exam is upper motor neuron with hyperreflexia and a sensory level. A herniated disc does not cause fever or rim enhancement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has worsening paraparesis over hours with cord compression on MRI. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Empiric IV antibiotics alone with serial neurologic monitoring
Emergent neurosurgical decompression and drainage plus empiric IV antibiotics covering MRSA
High-dose IV corticosteroids and plasma exchange
CT-guided aspiration alone, then await cultures before antibiotics
Oral antibiotics and outpatient spine follow-up
stages.explanation: A spinal epidural abscess with cord compression and a progressing neurologic deficit is a surgical emergency: emergent decompression and drainage combined with empiric intravenous antibiotics gives the best chance of neurologic recovery, and the speed of surgery is the strongest determinant of outcome — deficits that persist beyond 24–48 hours often become permanent. Empiric coverage must include MRSA (vancomycin) plus a gram-negative agent because Staphylococcus aureus, frequently methicillin-resistant in injection drug users, is the leading pathogen. Antibiotics alone may suffice only for select patients without compression or deficit, not this one. Steroids and plasma exchange treat inflammatory myelitis, not pyogenic abscess, and would worsen infection. Aspiration alone is inadequate for cord compression, and withholding antibiotics to await cultures dangerously delays therapy in a deteriorating, septic patient.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY
stages.content:
Blood cultures (both bottles): Staphylococcus aureus, methicillin-resistant (MRSA)
Intraoperative epidural cultures: MRSA, same susceptibility profile
Transthoracic echocardiogram: no valvular vegetations identified
Vancomycin trough therapeutic

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After decompression and identification of MRSA, what is the most appropriate definitive antimicrobial plan?
options: A 7-day course of oral linezolid on discharge
Prolonged intravenous MRSA-directed therapy (e.g., vancomycin) for approximately 6 weeks, guided by infectious disease and inflammatory markers
Discontinue antibiotics now that the abscess has been surgically drained
A single dose of intravenous vancomycin, then no further antibiotics
stages.explanation: Spinal epidural abscess, particularly with accompanying vertebral osteomyelitis and discitis, requires prolonged pathogen-directed intravenous therapy — typically about 6 weeks of an agent such as vancomycin for MRSA — with duration and de-escalation guided by infectious disease consultation and the trend of inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP). A 7-day oral course is far too short to sterilize infected bone and epidural space and would relapse. Stopping antibiotics after drainage ignores the persistent osteomyelitis and the high relapse risk; surgery debulks but does not sterilize. A single vancomycin dose is grossly inadequate for a deep-seated bone and central nervous system-adjacent infection. The combination of timely surgical decompression and a full prolonged antibiotic course offers the best chance to halt the deficit and cure the infection.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal epidural abscess (with T8–T9 vertebral osteomyelitis)
pearls: The classic triad is fever, back pain, and neurologic deficit, but all three are present in a minority — keep a very low threshold in injection drug users and diabetics.
Emergent whole-spine MRI with contrast is the test of choice; skip lesions occur in up to 15%, and LP is contraindicated.
Staphylococcus aureus (often MRSA) is the leading pathogen; empiric therapy must cover MRSA plus gram-negatives.
Outcome hinges on speed — emergent surgical decompression plus prolonged IV antibiotics; deficits persisting beyond 24–48 hours often become permanent.

cases.title: Neck pain and dizziness after a day at the amusement park
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe neck pain, dizziness, and slurred speech since riding roller coasters yesterday.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man presents with severe right-sided posterior neck pain and occipital headache that began shortly after riding several high-speed roller coasters at an amusement park the previous day. Overnight he developed vertigo, nausea, and unsteadiness, veering to the right when walking. This morning he noted clumsiness of the right hand and slurred speech, prompting his wife to bring him in. He denies fever, recent infection, or other trauma. He had a deep-tissue neck massage two days earlier. There were no preceding focal deficits and no prior similar episodes. Past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no regular medications or anticoagulants. He describes himself as 'double-jointed' but has no diagnosed connective-tissue disease. Social history: software developer, social alcohol use, lifelong nonsmoker, no recreational drugs; he lifts weights recreationally. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with a cerebral aneurysm. He has no known drug allergies. He is right-handed. The headache and dizziness have progressively worsened over 24 hours, and the new speech and hand symptoms today finally brought him to the emergency department.
cases.exam: Alert and in moderate distress from headache. Speech is mildly dysarthric. There is right-sided partial ptosis and miosis (anisocoria greater in dim light) with reduced sweating over the right face. Pinprick sensation is decreased on the right face and on the left trunk and limbs, a crossed sensory pattern. Right-sided limb dysmetria is present on finger-nose and heel-shin testing. Gait is wide-based, veering to the right. The palate elevates asymmetrically, the gag is diminished on the right, and the voice is hoarse. Motor strength is full in all extremities; reflexes are symmetric with flexor plantar responses. There is no neck stiffness. The remaining cranial nerves are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Non-contrast CT head alone, then discharge if normal
Dix-Hallpike maneuver followed by Epley repositioning
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage
CT angiography of the head and neck along with a non-contrast head CT
stages.explanation: In a young patient with neck pain, occipital headache, vertigo, dysarthria, and crossed brainstem signs after neck strain or manipulation, vertebral artery dissection with posterior-circulation ischemia is the leading concern. The right first step is CT angiography of the head and neck paired with a non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage; CTA rapidly shows luminal narrowing, an intimal flap, or the 'string sign.' A non-contrast CT alone followed by discharge would miss both the dissection and early posterior-fossa ischemia. Dix-Hallpike and Epley target benign positional vertigo and ignore his focal deficits. An outpatient MRI in a week dangerously delays diagnosis of an evolving stroke. Lumbar puncture is not indicated without suspicion of subarachnoid hemorrhage or infection and would not address the vascular question. Rapid vascular imaging is what changes management here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage
CT angiography neck: tapered narrowing of the right vertebral artery with a 'string sign' consistent with dissection
MRI brain (DWI): acute infarct in the right lateral medulla
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Which clinical syndrome best explains his findings?
options: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome from vertebral artery/PICA territory
Lacunar pure motor stroke from a lenticulostriate vessel
stages.explanation: The combination of ipsilateral facial pain/temperature loss with contralateral body pain/temperature loss (a crossed sensory pattern), ipsilateral Horner syndrome, ipsilateral limb ataxia, vertigo, dysphagia, hoarseness, and a diminished gag localizes to the lateral medulla, the Wallenberg syndrome, typically from vertebral artery or PICA ischemia. An anterior cerebral artery infarct causes contralateral leg-predominant weakness, not crossed sensory loss. Medial medullary syndrome produces contralateral arm/leg weakness, contralateral proprioceptive loss, and ipsilateral tongue weakness, without Horner or crossed pain/temperature findings. A lacunar pure motor stroke causes isolated contralateral weakness with no sensory, cerebellar, or bulbar signs. Recognizing the crossed pattern is the key to localizing brainstem strokes and explains this young man's constellation of findings from his dissection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute treatment given the timing of his deficits?
options: Immediate high-intensity IV heparin bolus targeting a high PTT
Antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) with stroke-unit admission
stages.explanation: His deficits began more than 24 hours before arrival, placing him well outside the 4.5-hour window for IV alteplase, which would also add hemorrhage risk in dissection. Mechanical thrombectomy targets retrievable large-vessel anterior-circulation occlusions within defined windows, not a completed lateral medullary infarct. For extracranial vertebral artery dissection, antithrombotic therapy, either antiplatelet or anticoagulation, is standard; the CADISS trial showed no significant difference between the two, so an antiplatelet agent with stroke-unit admission is appropriate and safe. Carotid endarterectomy addresses anterior-circulation carotid stenosis, not a posterior-circulation vertebral dissection. An immediate high-intensity heparin bolus is unnecessary and raises bleeding risk, particularly if the dissection extends intracranially. The correct path is antithrombotic therapy plus monitored stroke-unit care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate supportive measure?
options: Keep him NPO and obtain a formal swallow evaluation before any oral intake
Start a statin and discharge home
Encourage early oral feeding to maintain nutrition
stages.explanation: Lateral medullary infarcts frequently damage the swallowing centers (nucleus ambiguus), producing severe dysphagia and a high aspiration risk; this patient already has a diminished gag, hoarseness, and an asymmetric palate. Keeping him NPO and obtaining a formal swallow evaluation before any oral intake is the single most important immediate supportive measure. Starting a statin and discharging ignores his evolving deficits and aspiration risk. Thrombolysis is contraindicated outside the window and irrelevant here. Encouraging early oral feeding is exactly the wrong move and would likely precipitate aspiration. Aspiration pneumonia is among the most common and preventable causes of morbidity in posterior-circulation stroke, so swallow safety takes priority alongside antithrombotic therapy and neurologic monitoring.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over the next 48-72 hours, which complication most threatens his life?
options: Brainstem/cerebellar edema compressing the fourth ventricle, causing obstructive hydrocephalus and herniation
stages.explanation: Large posterior-fossa infarcts, especially cerebellar and brainstem strokes, can swell over 2-4 days, compressing the fourth ventricle to produce obstructive hydrocephalus and directly compressing the brainstem with herniation, a rapidly fatal complication that may require neurosurgical decompression or external ventricular drainage. Close neuro checks and a low threshold for repeat imaging are essential. Hemorrhagic conversion requiring warfarin reversal is unlikely because he is not on warfarin and received no thrombolysis. Seizures are uncommon in isolated brainstem or cerebellar infarcts. Myocardial infarction is not the principal short-term threat in this young man without cardiac disease. Recognizing early signs of posterior-fossa swelling, declining consciousness, new cranial-nerve signs, or vomiting, can be lifesaving.

cases.diagnosis: Right vertebral artery dissection causing a lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome / posterior-circulation infarct.
pearls: Spontaneous or trauma-related vertebral artery dissection is a leading cause of posterior-circulation stroke in patients under 45.
Wallenberg (lateral medullary) syndrome = ipsilateral Horner, facial sensory loss, and ataxia with contralateral body pain/temperature loss, plus dysphagia and hoarseness.
CADISS showed antiplatelet and anticoagulation are comparable for cervical artery dissection.
Dysphagia and aspiration risk are high in lateral medullary stroke, so screen swallowing before any oral intake.

cases.title: A gray curtain over one eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Twice this week my right eye went dark like a shade coming down, then cleared in a few minutes.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to clinic after two episodes this week of painless visual loss in the right eye, each described as a gray shade or curtain descending from above, obscuring vision for 2-5 minutes before clearing completely. There was no associated eye pain, redness, flashing lights, or floaters, and no headache. He denies jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, weight loss, or proximal muscle aches. He has had no weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and a prior myocardial infarction. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. Social history is significant for a 40-pack-year smoking history; he continues to smoke half a pack daily and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes a father who had an ischemic stroke in his 60s. He is a retired postal worker, lives with his wife, and is functionally independent.
cases.exam: Visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes with the episode resolved; there is no relative afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements are full and visual fields are full to confrontation. Dilated fundoscopy reveals a bright, refractile cholesterol (Hollenhorst) plaque lodged at a right retinal arteriolar bifurcation, without disc edema or pallor. A soft bruit is audible over the right carotid artery. The temporal arteries are nontender and without nodularity. The remainder of the cranial-nerve exam is normal; motor strength, sensation, coordination, and gait are intact with no drift or dysarthria. Cardiac exam shows a regular rhythm without murmurs and no carotid thrill.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial diagnostic test?
stages.explanation: Transient, painless, monocular vision loss described as a descending curtain that resolves within minutes is classic amaurosis fugax, usually from an embolus to the retinal/ophthalmic artery arising from ipsilateral carotid atherosclerosis. The highest-yield, noninvasive first test is carotid duplex ultrasonography to assess internal carotid stenosis. MRI of the orbit evaluates optic-nerve or orbital pathology, not the embolic source. Tonometry screens for glaucoma or acute angle closure, which cause eye pain and persistent visual change rather than transient curtain loss. EEG evaluates seizures, not transient retinal ischemia. Giant cell arteritis should be considered above age 50, but he has no headache, jaw claudication, or scalp tenderness, so an ESR/CRP would screen for that while the duplex pursues the most likely embolic carotid source.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Carotid duplex: 80% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery; left ICA <40%
Dilated fundus exam: bright refractile (Hollenhorst) plaque in a right retinal arteriole
ESR 12 mm/hr; CRP 0.3 mg/dL (normal)
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
LDL 96 mg/dL; HbA1c 7.4%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and mechanism?
options: Amaurosis fugax from a cholesterol embolus arising from symptomatic carotid stenosis
stages.explanation: The transient monocular curtain, a visible Hollenhorst (cholesterol) plaque on fundoscopy, an ipsilateral carotid bruit, and 80% internal carotid stenosis confirm amaurosis fugax from artery-to-artery embolism, a transient ischemic attack of the eye and a warning of impending stroke. Retinal migraine occurs in younger patients with a headache history and is a diagnosis of exclusion. Central retinal vein occlusion causes sudden but persistent painless loss with a 'blood-and-thunder' fundus, not transient curtain vision. Optic neuritis causes subacute painful vision loss with an afferent pupillary defect in younger patients, not brief embolic episodes. Giant cell arteritis is important over age 50 but is unlikely with normal inflammatory markers and no cranial symptoms. Identifying the carotid as the embolic source is what drives urgent management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management of his carotid stenosis?
options: Continue medical therapy alone; no procedure for an eye symptom
Repeat carotid duplex in one year
Refer for carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks plus intensified medical therapy
stages.explanation: Amaurosis fugax is a symptomatic cerebrovascular event; with 70-99% ipsilateral internal carotid stenosis, revascularization markedly reduces subsequent stroke risk and is most beneficial performed early, ideally within 2 weeks, alongside aggressive medical therapy. Carotid endarterectomy is the standard revascularization with the strongest evidence in this age group. Medical therapy alone undertreats high-grade symptomatic stenosis. Warfarin is not first-line for atherosclerotic carotid disease, which is managed with antiplatelet therapy plus a statin. Repeating duplex in a year dangerously delays intervention during the highest-risk window. Carotid stenting is a reasonable alternative in selected patients (younger or high surgical risk), but it is not mandated over endarterectomy, which often carries a lower periprocedural stroke rate in older patients. Early endarterectomy plus medical optimization is best.

stages.label: SECONDARY PREVENTION
stages.question: Which medical optimization is most important alongside surgery?
options: High-intensity statin, antiplatelet therapy, blood-pressure control, and smoking cessation
Treat LDL only if it exceeds 160 mg/dL
stages.explanation: Optimal secondary prevention pairs revascularization with aggressive risk-factor modification: a high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 80 mg) targeting LDL well below 70 mg/dL, antiplatelet therapy, blood-pressure control, diabetes management, and, critically in this 40-pack-year smoker, smoking cessation. Permanently stopping aspirin removes essential antiplatelet protection. Long-term dual anticoagulation is not indicated for atherosclerotic carotid disease and increases bleeding. Treating LDL only above 160 mg/dL is outdated; established atherosclerotic disease warrants a high-intensity statin regardless of baseline LDL. Smoking cessation alone substantially lowers future cardiovascular and stroke risk, making it one of the most powerful interventions available to this patient. The combination of intensive medical therapy and timely endarterectomy gives the greatest reduction in recurrent events.

cases.diagnosis: Amaurosis fugax from artery-to-artery embolism due to symptomatic high-grade right internal carotid artery stenosis.
pearls: Amaurosis fugax is a TIA of the eye, so treat it as a stroke warning and image the carotids urgently.
A Hollenhorst plaque (refractile cholesterol embolus) on fundoscopy points to a carotid source.
Symptomatic carotid stenosis 70-99% warrants endarterectomy within 2 weeks for maximum stroke-risk reduction.
Always consider giant cell arteritis in transient vision loss over age 50 and check ESR/CRP.

cases.title: The room won't stop spinning
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, constant dizziness with vomiting and I can't walk straight since this morning.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with the sudden onset of severe, continuous spinning vertigo that began abruptly this morning while he was getting dressed. The sensation is constant rather than positional, accompanied by repeated vomiting and profound unsteadiness; he has been unable to walk without falling toward the right. He denies hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness, headache, chest pain, or palpitations, and has had no preceding upper respiratory illness. He noticed no focal limb symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, for which he was prescribed apixaban, though he admits to frequently missing doses. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, and apixaban 5 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. Social history: former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 10 years ago), occasional alcohol, lives with his wife, and is independent at baseline. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease. He has never experienced anything like this before, and the symptoms have persisted unchanged for several hours.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable with continuous vertigo and vomiting. He has spontaneous nystagmus that changes direction with gaze and a skew deviation on alternate cover testing; the head impulse test is normal. There is marked truncal ataxia, and he cannot sit unsupported or stand without falling toward the right. Finger-to-nose testing shows mild right-sided dysmetria. Hearing is intact bilaterally and there is no facial weakness or numbness. Speech is mildly slurred. Strength is full in all limbs with symmetric reflexes and flexor plantar responses. The cardiac rhythm is irregularly irregular without murmurs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best bedside approach to distinguish a central from a peripheral cause?
options: Immediate non-contrast CT head to rule out stroke
HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew)
Dix-Hallpike maneuver followed by Epley repositioning
stages.explanation: In acute, continuous vertigo with nystagmus and gait instability (acute vestibular syndrome), the validated bedside HINTS battery distinguishes central from peripheral causes and is actually more sensitive than early MRI for posterior-circulation stroke. A reassuring peripheral pattern shows an abnormal head impulse with a corrective saccade, unidirectional horizontal nystagmus, and no skew; central or dangerous findings are a normal head impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, and skew deviation. Non-contrast CT is insensitive for acute posterior-fossa ischemia and is frequently normal early. Dix-Hallpike and Epley diagnose and treat BPPV, but his vertigo is continuous rather than positional, and he cannot sit unaided. The Romberg test is nonspecific and will not differentiate stroke from labyrinthitis. The HINTS exam is the highest-yield bedside discriminator here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
HINTS exam: normal head impulse test, direction-changing horizontal nystagmus, skew deviation present (central pattern)
ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
MRI brain (DWI): acute infarct in the right posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) territory
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Cerebellar (PICA-territory) ischemic infarct, likely cardioembolic from atrial fibrillation
stages.explanation: Inability to stand or walk unaided, a central HINTS pattern, atrial fibrillation, and DWI showing a PICA-territory lesion confirm a cerebellar infarct, most likely cardioembolic. Severe truncal ataxia out of proportion to limb findings is a hallmark of cerebellar stroke. BPPV causes brief, positional vertigo with fatigable nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike, not continuous symptoms with inability to sit. Vestibular neuritis is peripheral, with a positive head impulse, unidirectional nystagmus, no skew, and patients can usually walk cautiously, features opposite to this case. Ménière disease features recurrent vertigo with fluctuating hearing loss and tinnitus, not a single acute event with gait failure. Misclassifying a cerebellar stroke as peripheral risks missing life-threatening posterior-fossa swelling, so the central findings plus imaging are decisive.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Discharge with meclizine and outpatient ENT follow-up
Discharge with the Epley maneuver and reassurance
Admit to a monitored stroke/neurologic unit for close observation
Arrange an outpatient MRI in two weeks
