Migraine with aura is a category 4 contraindication to combined estrogen-containing contraceptives—switch to progestin-only or non-hormonal methods, especially in smokers.

cases.title: Headaches more days than not
cases.chiefComplaint: "I get headaches 20 days a month now — I can't keep living like this."
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman presents with worsening headaches. She has had episodic migraines since her early twenties—unilateral throbbing pain with nausea and light sensitivity—but over the past two years attacks have escalated from four days a month to roughly 20 headache days per month for the last six months. On about nine or ten of those days the pain is severe, throbbing, one-sided, and accompanied by nausea and photophobia; on the remainder it is a milder, featureless pressure. She uses sumatriptan 100 mg on about seven days per month and ibuprofen on about five days per month, deliberately rationing them after reading about rebound headaches. She has missed eight workdays in three months. Past medical history includes mild persistent asthma on an inhaled corticosteroid with albuterol as needed, and obesity (BMI 31); she is actively trying to lose weight. She uses a levonorgestrel IUD and has no plans for pregnancy. She drinks one coffee daily, does not smoke. Family history: sister with migraine. No known drug allergies.
cases.exam: Comfortable, alert woman with a mild bifrontal headache today (3/10). Fundi: sharp disc margins, spontaneous venous pulsations present. Visual fields full to confrontation; acuity 20/20 bilaterally. Cranial nerves intact. Motor, sensory, reflex, and cerebellar examinations normal; gait normal. Mild bilateral trapezius tenderness without true pericranial allodynia today. No temporal artery tenderness. Neck supple. BMI 31 kg/m². No features suggesting raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits her current headache pattern?
stages.explanation: Chronic migraine is defined as headache on at least 15 days per month for more than three months, with migrainous features (or triptan response) on at least eight of those days, in a patient with prior migraine history—exactly her picture: ~20 headache days with 9–10 migraine days, evolving from episodic migraine. Medication overuse headache requires triptan or combination-analgesic use on ≥10 days/month or simple analgesics on ≥15 days/month; she is below both thresholds (triptan 7, NSAID 5). Chronic tension-type headache lacks the severe unilateral throbbing attacks with nausea and photophobia she has on a third of days. New daily persistent headache is daily and unremitting from a clearly remembered onset, not a gradual escalation of pre-existing migraine. Hemicrania continua is strictly side-locked, continuous, and absolutely responsive to indomethacin.

stages.label: DIARY & BASELINE DATA
stages.content:
4-week prospective headache diary: 19 headache days
Days meeting migraine criteria (throbbing, unilateral, nausea/photophobia): 9
Acute medication use: sumatriptan 7 days, ibuprofen 5 days (below overuse thresholds)
Neurologic examination: normal; fundoscopy normal
BMI 31 kg/m²; BP 122/76 mmHg
CBC, CMP, TSH: within normal limits
PHQ-9: 6 (mild); sleep: frequent snoring reported by partner, Epworth 8

stages.label: PREVENTIVE THERAPY
stages.question: Which oral preventive medication is the most appropriate first choice for this patient?
options: Increase sumatriptan to daily scheduled use
stages.explanation: Topiramate has the strongest randomized-trial evidence among oral agents specifically in chronic migraine, and its tendency to cause weight loss aligns with her goals at BMI 31; titrate slowly (e.g., 25 mg/week to 100 mg/day) and warn about paresthesias, cognitive slowing, and kidney stones. Propranolol is a first-line migraine preventive in general but is relatively contraindicated with her asthma because nonselective beta-blockade can provoke bronchospasm. Amitriptyline is a reasonable alternative but commonly causes weight gain and sedation, undesirable here. Scheduling a triptan daily is never appropriate—it guarantees medication overuse headache and adds vascular risk. Valproate is effective but causes weight gain, tremor, and hair loss, and its teratogenic and PCOS-related effects make it a poor choice in a woman of childbearing age even with an IUD in place.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After three months on topiramate 100 mg daily she has intolerable word-finding difficulty and still has 16 headache days/month. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add daily butalbital-acetaminophen-caffeine for breakthrough pain
Switch to onabotulinumtoxinA injections every 12 weeks or a CGRP-pathway monoclonal antibody
stages.explanation: When a first oral preventive fails due to inefficacy or intolerance, guideline-supported options for chronic migraine include onabotulinumtoxinA—proven in the PREEMPT trials and FDA-approved specifically for chronic migraine—and CGRP-pathway monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab), which are effective, well tolerated, and work even in patients with prior preventive failures. Butalbital compounds have no preventive role and the highest propensity to cause medication overuse headache and dependence. High-flow oxygen is the abortive treatment for cluster headache, not migraine. SSRIs such as fluoxetine lack convincing evidence for migraine prevention (the tricyclic amitriptyline and SNRI venlafaxine have better data). Scheduled opioids promote chronification, tolerance, and overuse headache and are contraindicated as a migraine strategy.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: To prevent her disorder from being complicated by medication overuse headache, which instruction is correct?
options: She may use sumatriptan on up to 15 days per month safely
Ibuprofen is risk-free at any frequency because it is over-the-counter
Acute medications should be stopped entirely, even during severe attacks
Keep triptan or combination-analgesic use under 10 days per month and simple analgesics under 15 days per month
stages.explanation: The ICHD-3 thresholds for medication overuse are regular intake on ≥10 days/month for triptans, ergots, opioids, and combination analgesics, and ≥15 days/month for simple analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen), sustained for more than three months. Counseling patients to stay below these ceilings—triptans/combinations under 10 days and simple analgesics under 15 days monthly—is a core part of chronic migraine management, alongside an effective preventive that reduces the need for acute dosing. Fifteen triptan days per month exceeds the overuse threshold and would risk converting her headache pattern into rebound. Over-the-counter status does not make NSAIDs exempt; frequent ibuprofen drives overuse headache and adds GI and renal risk. Total prohibition of acute therapy is unnecessary and inhumane—appropriately limited abortive treatment remains a standard component of care.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic migraine (≥15 headache days/month with ≥8 migraine days, evolved from episodic migraine), without medication overuse.
pearls: Chronic migraine = ≥15 headache days/month for >3 months with ≥8 days of migrainous features; always screen acute medication frequency to exclude overuse.
Topiramate and onabotulinumtoxinA have the best trial evidence in chronic migraine; CGRP monoclonal antibodies are effective even after prior preventive failures.
Match preventives to comorbidity: avoid propranolol in asthma, valproate in women of childbearing potential, amitriptyline when weight gain is a concern.
Overuse thresholds: triptans, opioids, and combination analgesics ≥10 days/month; simple analgesics ≥15 days/month.

cases.title: Stabbing eye pain that wakes him at 2 a.m.
cases.chiefComplaint: "It's like a hot poker behind my right eye — this is the third night in a row it's woken me up."
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents to the ED at 2:40 a.m. with excruciating right-sided pain centered behind and around the right eye. The pain began abruptly 25 minutes ago, reached maximal intensity within five minutes, and he rates it 10/10, boring and stabbing in quality. During attacks his right eye waters and reddens, the right nostril runs, and his wife says his right eyelid droops. He cannot sit still—he paces and rocks, in contrast to his brother whose migraines make him lie motionless in the dark. Over the past two weeks he has had one to three identical attacks daily, each lasting 45 to 90 minutes, frequently waking him about 90 minutes after falling asleep. He recalls similar six-to-eight-week stretches each of the past three springs that resolved completely between bouts. A single beer reliably triggers an attack during these periods but not at other times. He smokes one pack per day for 15 years. Takes no regular medications; ibuprofen has been useless. No head trauma, fever, or visual loss. No known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: Agitated man pacing the exam room, clutching the right side of his head. Right eye: conjunctival injection, prominent tearing, partial ptosis, and miosis compared with the left; visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally, no pain with eye movement. Right nostril with clear rhinorrhea. Forehead sweating on the right. Pupils otherwise reactive; fundi normal with sharp discs. No periorbital trauma, no proptosis. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; facial sensation normal. Motor, sensory, reflexes, coordination normal. Temporal arteries nontender. Neck supple.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Strictly unilateral periorbital pain of extreme severity lasting 45–90 minutes, occurring one to three times daily in seasonal bouts, with ipsilateral lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis), nocturnal predilection, alcohol triggering only during bouts, restlessness, and male sex with heavy smoking is classic episodic cluster headache. Migraine attacks last 4–72 hours, and patients seek stillness and darkness rather than pacing. Paroxysmal hemicrania produces similar autonomic attacks but they are shorter (2–30 minutes), far more frequent (>5/day), and absolutely responsive to indomethacin. Trigeminal neuralgia causes seconds-long electric shocks triggered by touch or chewing, without autonomic features of this magnitude. Angle-closure glaucoma causes a painful red eye with a mid-dilated, poorly reactive pupil, halos, and decreased acuity—his acuity is normal and the pupil is small, not mid-dilated.

stages.label: ACUTE TREATMENT
stages.question: Which abortive treatment should be administered right now?
options: 100% oxygen at 12–15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15–20 minutes
stages.explanation: High-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 12–15 L/min with the patient seated aborts cluster attacks in roughly 70–80% of patients within 15 minutes and has essentially no contraindications—it is the first-line ED abortive. Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg is the other proven abortive and can be combined with oxygen for refractory attacks. Oral sumatriptan, by contrast, takes 30–60 minutes to act—longer than many attacks last—so the oral route is inappropriate for cluster headache; subcutaneous or intranasal formulations are required. Opioids are ineffective for cluster pathophysiology, delay effective therapy, and risk dependence in a disorder with daily attacks for weeks. Indomethacin is the specific treatment for paroxysmal hemicrania and hemicrania continua, not cluster headache, and its onset is too slow for an attack already at peak intensity.

stages.label: TREATMENT RESPONSE & IMAGING
stages.content:
100% oxygen via non-rebreather at 15 L/min started
Pain 10/10 → 2/10 at 10 minutes → 0/10 at 16 minutes
Right conjunctival injection and tearing resolved; mild residual ptosis
Non-contrast CT head: no acute hemorrhage, mass, or hydrocephalus
Intraocular pressures (tonometry): right 16 mmHg, left 15 mmHg (normal 10–21)
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, PR interval 162 ms, no conduction abnormality
MRI brain with pituitary views: ordered as outpatient to exclude secondary causes

stages.label: PREVENTIVE THERAPY
stages.question: Attacks are expected to continue daily for several more weeks. Which preventive medication should be started?
stages.explanation: Verapamil is the first-line preventive for cluster headache, typically started at 240 mg/day and titrated upward (sometimes to 480–960 mg/day); because high doses can cause PR prolongation and heart block, an ECG should be checked at baseline and with each dose increase. Since verapamil takes weeks to become effective, a transitional 'bridge'—a tapering course of prednisone or a greater occipital nerve block—is commonly given while titrating. Propranolol and amitriptyline are preventives for migraine and tension-type headache respectively, with no efficacy in cluster. Lithium has evidence in cluster headache, particularly chronic cluster, but its narrow therapeutic index, need for level monitoring, and renal and thyroid toxicity make it a second-line agent after verapamil, not the first choice in a new episodic case.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which lifestyle instruction is most important for this patient right now?
options: Abstain completely from alcohol during the active cluster bout, and pursue smoking cessation
Use home oxygen continuously through the night to prevent attacks
Follow a strict tyramine-free elimination diet
stages.explanation: Alcohol is a potent and near-immediate trigger of attacks during an active cluster period—as he has already noticed—so complete abstinence during bouts is the single most useful behavioral instruction; between bouts alcohol typically loses this triggering effect. Tobacco use is strongly associated with cluster headache, and cessation counseling is appropriate, although quitting does not reliably abort an established bout. Caffeine has no established role as a cluster trigger, so permanent elimination is unfounded. Oxygen is an abortive treatment used at attack onset; continuous nocturnal oxygen is not an evidence-based preventive and would be impractical and wasteful—prevention is the job of verapamil and bridging steroids. Tyramine elimination diets address a largely historical migraine hypothesis and have no relevance to cluster headache.

cases.diagnosis: Episodic cluster headache.
pearls: Cluster headache: strictly unilateral periorbital pain 15–180 minutes with ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, injection, rhinorrhea, partial Horner) and restlessness, often nocturnal and in seasonal bouts.
First-line abortives are 100% oxygen at 12–15 L/min via non-rebreather and subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg — oral triptans are too slow.
Verapamil (with ECG monitoring for heart block) is first-line prevention; bridge with a prednisone taper or greater occipital nerve block while titrating.
Alcohol triggers attacks only during active bouts — counsel strict abstinence during the cluster period.

cases.title: A tight band around her head
cases.chiefComplaint: "It feels like a tight band squeezing around my whole head a few times a month."
cases.history: A 33-year-old accountant presents with eight months of recurrent headaches, currently three to four days per month. The pain is bilateral, pressing and tightening 'like a band or a too-small helmet,' of mild to moderate intensity (4/10), building gradually over an hour and lasting four to eight hours. She can continue working through it, and walking up stairs does not worsen the pain. There is no nausea or vomiting; she admits mild sensitivity to bright office lighting during some attacks but no sound sensitivity. There is no preceding visual disturbance, no unilateral throbbing, and no autonomic symptoms. Headaches cluster around quarterly deadlines, and she notes her neck and shoulders feel 'knotted' on those days; she sleeps five to six hours during busy periods. Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg on roughly four days per month relieves the pain within an hour. She denies waking from sleep with headache, fever, weight loss, visual change, weakness, or numbness. Past medical history is unremarkable; no medications besides acetaminophen, no tobacco, two coffees daily. Family history is negative for migraine. No known drug allergies.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman, currently with a mild headache (3/10), working on her laptop in the waiting room. Fundi: sharp disc margins with venous pulsations. Visual fields and acuity normal. Cranial nerves intact. Tenderness to palpation of bilateral trapezius, suboccipital, and temporalis muscles without trigger-point radiation. Temporal arteries nontender with normal pulses. Motor, sensory, and reflex examinations normal; gait and coordination normal. Neck supple with full, pain-free range of motion. No papilledema, no meningismus, no focal deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Non-contrast CT head to rule out a mass lesion
No neuroimaging — clinical diagnosis with reassurance and a headache diary
stages.explanation: She has a stable, eight-month pattern of episodic, featureless bilateral headaches with a completely normal neurologic examination and no red flags—no thunderclap onset, no progressive worsening, no awakening from sleep, no focal deficits, no papilledema, no fever or weight loss, no immunosuppression, and age under 50. Guidelines (Choosing Wisely, AAN) explicitly recommend against imaging in this situation because the yield is extremely low and incidental findings cause harm; reassurance plus a diary to confirm the pattern is correct. CT adds radiation without diagnostic value. Cervical MRI is not indicated for muscle tightness with a normal exam and full neck motion. ESR/CRP screen for giant cell arteritis, a disease of patients over 50 with temporal artery abnormalities she lacks. Routine referral is unnecessary for a primary headache a generalist can manage.

stages.label: DIARY & EXAM FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
6-week headache diary: 5 headache days total
Character: bilateral pressing/tightening, intensity 3–5/10, duration 4–8 hours
No nausea or vomiting on any day; mild photophobia alone on 2 days; never both photophobia and phonophobia
Not aggravated by routine physical activity on any day
Acetaminophen used 4 days with reliable relief
Associated factors: work deadlines, reduced sleep, neck/shoulder tightness
Repeat neurologic exam: normal; fundoscopy normal
BP 114/70 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Bilateral, pressing, non-pulsating pain of mild-to-moderate intensity, lasting hours, not aggravated by routine activity, without nausea, and with at most one of photophobia or phonophobia fulfills ICHD-3 criteria for tension-type headache; at three to four days monthly it is the frequent episodic subtype, and pericranial muscle tenderness is a common supportive finding. Migraine without aura requires at least two of unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, activity-aggravated pain plus nausea or both photo- and phonophobia—she fails these. Chronic tension-type headache requires ≥15 headache days per month for over three months; she has four. Cervicogenic headache arises from cervical spine pathology with reduced neck motion and pain provoked by neck maneuvers, none present. Medication overuse headache requires acute analgesic use ≥15 days/month for simple analgesics; she uses acetaminophen only four days monthly.

stages.label: ACUTE TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute treatment plan?
options: Ibuprofen or acetaminophen as needed, kept under 15 days per month
stages.explanation: Simple analgesics—NSAIDs such as ibuprofen 400 mg or naproxen, or acetaminophen 1000 mg—are first-line acute therapy for episodic tension-type headache and are already working for her; the key counseling point is to cap use below 15 days per month to avoid medication overuse headache. Butalbital combinations are sedating, habit-forming, and carry the highest risk of transforming episodic headache into chronic daily headache; guidelines discourage them for a benign, NSAID-responsive disorder. Triptans are serotonin agonists effective for migraine but demonstrably ineffective for pure tension-type headache. Opioids have no role in primary headache disorders: they are ineffective for the underlying mechanism, promote chronification and dependence, and would be a clear escalation error for a 4/10 headache she can work through.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Six months later her headaches increase to 12 days per month despite lifestyle changes. Which preventive therapy has the best evidence for her disorder?
options: Amitriptyline at low dose, titrated gradually
stages.explanation: Amitriptyline is the best-studied preventive for frequent episodic and chronic tension-type headache, effective at low doses (10–25 mg nightly, titrated as tolerated), with benefit usually apparent within one to two months; its anticholinergic effects and sedation warrant bedtime dosing and slow titration. Combining it with stress management or physical therapy targeting pericranial muscles improves outcomes. Propranolol is an evidence-based preventive for migraine, not tension-type headache. OnabotulinumtoxinA is approved only for chronic migraine; trials in tension-type headache were negative. Verapamil is the preventive of choice for cluster headache and has no role here. Nonpharmacologic measures—regular sleep, scheduled exercise, ergonomic adjustments, and cognitive behavioral or relaxation therapy—should continue alongside any preventive, since stress and sleep restriction are her clearest triggers.

cases.diagnosis: Frequent episodic tension-type headache.
pearls: Tension-type headache is bilateral, pressing, mild-moderate, not worsened by activity, and lacks nausea — at most one of photophobia or phonophobia is allowed.
No imaging is indicated for a stable primary headache pattern with a normal neurologic exam and no red flags.
Acute therapy is simple analgesics (NSAIDs/acetaminophen) capped below 15 days/month to prevent medication overuse headache; triptans, butalbital, and opioids have no role.
Amitriptyline is the best-evidenced preventive for frequent or chronic tension-type headache, ideally combined with stress management and sleep regularization.

cases.title: New headache with jaw pain and a graying curtain
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've had a new headache over my temple for three weeks, and yesterday my right eye went gray for five minutes."
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman presents to the ED after an episode of painless visual loss. Yesterday a 'gray curtain' descended over her right eye and resolved completely after about five minutes. For three weeks she has had a new, constant, boring right temporal headache, different from any prior headache, with scalp tenderness so pronounced that brushing her hair hurts. For two weeks her jaw muscles ache and fatigue after a minute of chewing, forcing her to pause mid-meal. She reports six weeks of fatigue, poor appetite with 3 kg unintentional weight loss, and aching with morning stiffness lasting over an hour in both shoulders and hips, making it hard to rise from chairs. She denies fever at home, diplopia, weakness, or speech difficulty. Past medical history: hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; osteopenia. She has never smoked. Family history is noncontributory. No known drug allergies. She lives independently and drove herself to the hospital, frightened she is having a stroke.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing woman. Right superficial temporal artery is tender, cord-like, and has a diminished pulse compared with the left; overlying scalp tenderness. Visual acuity 20/25 both eyes; pupils equal and reactive without relative afferent pupillary defect; fundi: discs sharp, no pallor or edema; visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Shoulder abduction and hip flexion limited by aching but formal strength intact (5/5). Reflexes normal. No carotid bruits. No synovitis of hands. Gait normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Begin high-dose glucocorticoids now and send ESR, CRP, CBC
Schedule a temporal artery biopsy and withhold steroids until the result returns
Obtain CT angiography of the head and neck and start aspirin alone
Routine ophthalmology referral within one week
Two-week trial of naproxen for temporal arthritis pain
stages.explanation: She has a classic presentation of giant cell arteritis—new headache after age 50, jaw claudication, temporal artery abnormality, constitutional symptoms, polymyalgia rheumatica features—plus transient monocular vision loss, which signals impending permanent blindness from arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Glucocorticoids must be started immediately, before any confirmatory testing, because vision lost to GCA rarely returns and the second eye can be affected within days. ESR, CRP, and CBC are drawn concurrently. Withholding steroids pending biopsy is the classic error: biopsy can wait, vision cannot. CTA and aspirin address atherosclerotic stroke/amaurosis but ignore the inflammatory arteritis driving her risk. A routine referral in a week accepts an unacceptable window for blindness. NSAIDs do not control the vasculitis and squander irreplaceable time.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ESR 98 mm/h (normal <30)
CRP 9.4 mg/dL (normal <0.8)
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, MCV 84 fL (normocytic anemia)
Platelets 512 ×10³/µL
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL with normal differential
Alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L (mildly elevated); AST/ALT normal
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; glucose 104 mg/dL
Non-contrast CT head: no acute infarct or hemorrhage
Methylprednisolone administered; vascular surgery contacted for temporal artery biopsy

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY TEST
stages.question: Which test most definitively confirms the suspected diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold-standard confirmatory test for giant cell arteritis, demonstrating granulomatous inflammation of the vessel wall with mononuclear infiltrates and often multinucleated giant cells and fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina. Because the vasculitis produces 'skip lesions,' a segment of at least 1–2 cm should be taken, and histology remains interpretable for at least two weeks after starting glucocorticoids—so treatment never waits for the biopsy. Temporal artery ultrasound showing a noncompressible 'halo sign' is an accepted alternative in experienced centers, but carotid duplex of the cervical carotids evaluates atherosclerosis, not medium-vessel cranial arteritis. MRI-DWI detects infarction, which she does not have. ANCA testing targets small-vessel vasculitides such as GPA, not GCA. Lumbar puncture has no role in this large/medium-vessel vasculitis.

stages.label: STEROID REGIMEN
stages.question: Given her transient monocular vision loss, which initial glucocorticoid regimen is most appropriate?
options: IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg daily for 3 days, then high-dose oral prednisone (about 1 mg/kg/day)
stages.explanation: When GCA presents with visual symptoms—transient or established visual loss, diplopia, or amaurosis—most guidelines recommend pulse IV methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg daily for three days) followed by high-dose oral prednisone around 1 mg/kg/day (typically 40–60 mg), because the threatened complication is irreversible arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy and contralateral eye involvement can occur within days. Uncomplicated GCA without visual symptoms can be treated with high-dose oral prednisone alone. Prednisone 10 mg daily is a polymyalgia rheumatica dose—grossly inadequate for arteritis with ocular ischemia. Intra-articular steroids address only joint symptoms and provide no systemic vasculitis control. Methotrexate (or tocilizumab, the IL-6 inhibitor) serves as a steroid-sparing adjunct during the long taper, never as initial monotherapy when vision is at stake.

stages.label: LONG-TERM SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: Biopsy confirms the diagnosis. Beyond tapering steroids, which long-term consideration is most important?
options: Annual pulmonary function tests for fibrosis
Monitoring for thoracic aortic aneurysm, plus bone protection and consideration of tocilizumab during prolonged steroid therapy
stages.explanation: GCA is a large-vessel vasculitis: patients carry roughly a 17-fold increased risk of thoracic aortic aneurysm and can develop aortitis, dissection, or large-artery stenoses years after diagnosis, so periodic imaging surveillance of the aorta (chest radiography, CT/MR angiography per local practice) is recommended. Equally practical: she faces 1–2 years of glucocorticoids with osteopenia at baseline, mandating calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonate prophylaxis, plus glucose and blood pressure monitoring; tocilizumab is an effective steroid-sparing agent that reduces cumulative steroid exposure and relapse. Her shoulder and hip girdle symptoms represent associated polymyalgia rheumatica, which responds to the same therapy. Pulmonary fibrosis surveillance belongs to ILD-associated diseases; glomerulonephritis monitoring belongs to ANCA vasculitides and lupus; routine EMG has no role—none are characteristic complications of GCA.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis with transient monocular visual loss (impending arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy), with associated polymyalgia rheumatica.
pearls: New headache after age 50 with jaw claudication, temporal artery abnormality, and elevated ESR/CRP is GCA — start high-dose steroids immediately; never wait for biopsy.
Visual symptoms warrant pulse IV methylprednisolone; temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic for at least 2 weeks after steroids begin.
Jaw claudication and transient vision loss are the strongest predictors of permanent blindness; the fellow eye can be lost within days untreated.
Long-term: screen for thoracic aortic aneurysm, protect bones during the prolonged taper, and consider tocilizumab as a steroid-sparing agent; ~50% of GCA patients have PMR.

cases.title: Daily headaches with whooshing in her ears
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've had a headache nearly every day for three months, and I hear my heartbeat whooshing in my ears."
cases.history: A 25-year-old woman presents with three months of near-daily holocephalic, pressure-like headaches, worst on waking and aggravated by bending over, coughing, and lying flat. She describes constant rhythmic 'whooshing' in both ears synchronous with her pulse, most noticeable at night. Several times daily, especially on standing, her vision dims or 'grays out' in both eyes for a few seconds and then clears. For two weeks she has noticed intermittent horizontal double vision when looking at distant objects. She denies fever, weakness, numbness, or seizures. She has gained 12 kg over the past year; current BMI is 38 kg/m². Four months ago her dermatologist started minocycline 100 mg twice daily for acne, which she still takes. She uses no other medications, including no retinoids, no hormonal contraception, and no vitamin A supplements. Past medical history is otherwise negative. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is noncontributory. No known drug allergies. She works as a dental assistant and is increasingly worried about her vision.
cases.exam: Obese woman (BMI 38) in no acute distress. Visual acuity 20/20 right, 20/25 left. Fundoscopy: bilateral optic disc elevation with blurred margins and obscuration of vessels at the disc edge — papilledema. Enlarged blind spots on confrontation testing; color vision intact. Incomplete abduction of the left eye on far lateral gaze with horizontal diplopia at distance (partial left CN VI palsy); other eye movements full. Pupils equal and reactive, no afferent pupillary defect. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Motor, sensory, reflexes, coordination, and gait normal. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic sequence?
options: Lumbar puncture in clinic today, then imaging only if CSF is abnormal
Start acetazolamide empirically and re-examine in one month
Non-contrast CT head alone; if normal, no further testing is required
MRI brain with MR venography first, then lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
stages.explanation: Papilledema mandates neuroimaging before lumbar puncture, both to exclude a mass lesion or hydrocephalus that would make LP dangerous and to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, the key mimic of this syndrome—hence MRI brain with dedicated MR venography is the correct first study, followed by lumbar puncture in the lateral decubitus position with opening pressure and CSF analysis. Performing LP before imaging in a patient with papilledema and a sixth nerve palsy risks herniation if a mass is present. Empiric treatment without measuring pressure or excluding venous thrombosis misses dangerous secondary causes and forfeits the diagnostic opening pressure. Non-contrast CT can miss venous sinus thrombosis, isodense masses, and meningeal disease; a normal CT alone never completes the workup of papilledema.

stages.label: IMAGING & LUMBAR PUNCTURE
stages.content:
MRI brain: no mass, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus
Partially empty sella; flattening of the posterior sclerae; mild distension and tortuosity of perioptic nerve sheaths
MR venography: bilateral transverse sinus narrowing, no filling defect or thrombosis
Lumbar puncture (left lateral decubitus, legs extended): opening pressure 34 cm H2O (normal ≤25)
CSF: WBC 1 /µL, RBC 0 /µL, protein 22 mg/dL, glucose 62 mg/dL — normal composition
Headache transiently improved after CSF removal
CBC, CMP, TSH: normal; urine hCG: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: She fulfills the modified Dandy criteria: papilledema, an otherwise normal neurologic examination except for a sixth nerve palsy (a nonlocalizing 'false localizing' sign of raised pressure), elevated opening pressure ≥25 cm H2O with normal CSF composition, and neuroimaging excluding structural disease and venous thrombosis—idiopathic intracranial hypertension, in a typical demographic (obese woman of childbearing age) with recent weight gain and minocycline exposure as an aggravating factor. MRV specifically excluded venous sinus thrombosis; smooth bilateral transverse sinus narrowing is a common consequence of raised pressure rather than evidence of clot. Chronic meningitis would show CSF pleocytosis or elevated protein, both absent. Chronic migraine causes neither papilledema, elevated opening pressure, sixth nerve palsy, nor pulse-synchronous tinnitus—attributing objective papilledema to migraine would risk her vision.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Therapeutic serial lumbar punctures as primary long-term therapy
Discontinue minocycline, structured weight loss program, and acetazolamide titrated upward
Observation with repeat MRI in 6 months
stages.explanation: First steps are removal of the precipitant (tetracyclines such as minocycline are well-established triggers and must be stopped, with the dermatologist offered alternatives), weight loss of 5–10% of body weight—which can induce remission—and acetazolamide, the carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that lowers CSF production and was proven in the IIH Treatment Trial to improve visual fields, papilledema, and quality of life, titrated as tolerated (often to 2–4 g/day) while warning of paresthesias and metallic taste. Serial LPs provide only hours-to-days of relief and are reserved for temporizing (e.g., pregnancy, fulminant disease awaiting surgery). Shunting is for medically refractory disease or fulminant visual decline, not initial therapy in a patient with 20/25 acuity. Furosemide is at best adjunctive. Observation alone gambles with her vision—papilledema and a sixth nerve palsy demand active treatment.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which monitoring strategy is most important to prevent the feared complication of this disease?
options: Serial formal automated visual field testing (perimetry) with ophthalmology co-management
Monthly lumbar punctures to track opening pressure
Following headache severity scores alone at each visit
stages.explanation: The feared outcome of idiopathic intracranial hypertension is permanent visual loss from chronic papilledema, and visual field loss—classically beginning with enlarged blind spots and inferonasal defects—precedes loss of central acuity, which is spared until late. Serial automated perimetry with dilated fundus examinations (ideally with optic disc photographs or OCT of the retinal nerve fiber layer) is therefore the cornerstone of monitoring and the trigger for escalation: progressive field loss despite maximal medical therapy indicates optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting. Repeated lumbar punctures are painful, poorly tolerated, and opening pressure correlates imperfectly with visual risk. Serial MRI adds nothing once structural disease is excluded. Headache severity tracks poorly with visual threat—vision can deteriorate while headache improves—so symptom scores alone are dangerously insufficient.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri), aggravated by weight gain and minocycline use.
pearls: Papilledema requires MRI with MR venography before lumbar puncture; IIH is diagnosed by opening pressure ≥25 cm H2O with normal CSF and normal imaging.
CN VI palsy is a false localizing sign of raised intracranial pressure and does not violate IIH criteria.
Treatment: stop offending drugs (tetracyclines, vitamin A derivatives, growth hormone), 5–10% weight loss, and acetazolamide (IIHTT-proven); surgery (ONSF or shunt) for refractory visual decline.
Monitor with serial automated visual fields and fundus exams — central acuity is preserved until late, so perimetry, not symptoms, drives escalation.

cases.title: Daily headaches despite a purse full of painkillers
cases.chiefComplaint: "I take something for my head almost every day now, but the headaches just keep coming back."
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman presents with near-daily headaches. She has had migraine without aura since her twenties, previously four to five attacks per month. Over the past 18 months headaches escalated; for at least the past six months she has a headache on 25 or more days per month—a dull, constant, bilateral background pain present on waking, punctuated several times weekly by more severe throbbing attacks with nausea. She takes butalbital-acetaminophen-caffeine on roughly 16 days per month (often two to three tablets), sumatriptan 100 mg on about 11 days, and ibuprofen sporadically. Each dose dulls the pain for two to three hours, after which it returns, and she describes waking at 4 a.m. 'needing' a tablet. A trial of topiramate 18 months ago 'did nothing,' though she was using daily analgesics throughout. Past medical history: generalized anxiety disorder on sertraline 50 mg daily. She drinks three cups of coffee daily, does not smoke. Family history: mother with migraines. No known drug allergies. Neurologic review of systems is otherwise negative; no visual change, fever, or weight loss.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly anxious woman with a dull bifrontal headache (4/10) during the visit. Fundi: sharp disc margins bilaterally, normal venous pulsations. Visual fields and acuity normal. Cranial nerves intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout; sensation, reflexes, coordination, and gait normal. Mild pericranial and trapezius tenderness. Temporal arteries nontender. Neck supple with full range of motion. No papilledema, no focal deficits, no signs of raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for her current daily headache pattern?
options: Medication overuse headache complicating chronic migraine
stages.explanation: Headache on ≥15 days per month in a patient with a pre-existing primary headache disorder, occurring alongside regular overuse of acute medications—butalbital combination analgesic on ≥10 days/month (she uses 16) and triptan on ≥10 days/month (she uses 11) for more than three months—defines medication overuse headache, here superimposed on migraine that has chronified. The early-morning headache relieved transiently by medication and 4 a.m. dosing 'need' reflect end-of-dose rebound, characteristic of butalbital and caffeine. Chronic tension-type headache cannot account for her severe throbbing attacks with nausea. New daily persistent headache begins abruptly and is daily from onset, not a gradual transformation. Hemicrania continua is strictly unilateral and indomethacin-responsive. IIH causes papilledema, pulsatile tinnitus, and visual obscurations—all absent with her normal fundi.

stages.label: HEADACHE DIARY
stages.content:
4-week prospective diary: 26 headache days of 28
Butalbital-acetaminophen-caffeine: taken on 16 days (32 tablets total)
Sumatriptan 100 mg: taken on 11 days
Ibuprofen 400 mg: taken on 4 days
Pattern: headache present on waking 18 of 26 days; each medication dose relieves pain for 2–3 hours
Severe migrainous attacks (throbbing, nausea, photophobia): 6 days
Neurologic exam: normal; fundoscopy: normal
BP 126/80 mmHg; CBC, CMP, TSH: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for this patient?
options: Structured withdrawal of the overused acute medications, with patient education and a clear follow-up plan
Switch butalbital to scheduled oxycodone to ease the transition
Increase butalbital to scheduled four-times-daily dosing for better coverage
MRI brain before any medication change
Reassure her and continue the current regimen with a relaxation app
stages.explanation: Medication overuse headache does not improve until the overused agents are withdrawn: education plus structured discontinuation of the butalbital compound and reduction of triptan days to fewer than 10 per month is the cornerstone, and the majority of patients revert toward an episodic pattern within about two months. Patients must be warned that headaches transiently worsen for days to weeks during withdrawal, and bridging strategies (a long-acting NSAID such as naproxen, or a short steroid course) plus scheduled follow-up improve adherence. Substituting an opioid trades one overuse-prone, dependence-producing drug for a worse one. Scheduling more butalbital intensifies rebound physiology. Imaging is not indicated for a longstanding headache pattern with a normal examination and no red flags. Reassurance without withdrawal abandons her to the perpetuating cause.

stages.label: WITHDRAWAL SAFETY
stages.question: Which statement about discontinuing her acute medications is correct?
options: All of her acute medications can be stopped abruptly with no safety concerns
Butalbital should be tapered gradually (or covered with a phenobarbital bridge), because abrupt cessation of frequent barbiturate use risks withdrawal seizures
Sumatriptan requires a months-long taper to avoid withdrawal seizures
She must be admitted to an intensive care unit for any analgesic withdrawal
stages.explanation: Butalbital is a barbiturate: in patients taking it frequently, abrupt discontinuation can provoke a withdrawal syndrome resembling alcohol withdrawal—anxiety, tremor, and in severe cases seizures and delirium—so it must be tapered gradually or covered with a standardized phenobarbital substitution protocol. This is the critical safety distinction among her medications. Triptans and NSAIDs, in contrast, can be stopped abruptly; their cessation causes transient rebound headache but no dangerous physiologic withdrawal and certainly no seizures—so a months-long sumatriptan taper is unnecessary. Most medication withdrawal in this setting is accomplished safely as an outpatient with education and bridging therapy; inpatient management is reserved for high-dose barbiturate or opioid dependence, significant psychiatric comorbidity, or prior failed outpatient withdrawal—ICU admission is not a routine requirement.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Regarding preventive therapy during her medication withdrawal, which approach is best supported?
options: Delay any preventive until three months after withdrawal is complete
Use daily butalbital as the bridge and preventive
Prescribe scheduled oxycodone for breakthrough attacks during withdrawal
Start an evidence-based migraine preventive (e.g., topiramate retrial or a CGRP monoclonal antibody) concurrently with withdrawal
stages.explanation: Current evidence and guidelines support starting preventive therapy at the same time as acute-medication withdrawal rather than waiting: randomized data show topiramate and CGRP-pathway monoclonal antibodies reduce headache days even while overuse is ongoing, and concurrent prevention improves the chance that withdrawal succeeds. Her previous topiramate 'failure' occurred during active overuse, which blunts preventive efficacy—so a retrial after withdrawal counts as a fair trial, and CGRP monoclonal antibodies are an excellent alternative given her anxiety (topiramate can worsen cognition and mood). Deferring prevention for three months reflects outdated dogma and leaves her unprotected during the most vulnerable period. Butalbital has no preventive efficacy and is the drug being eliminated. Opioids for breakthrough pain would simply re-establish overuse with a more dangerous agent.

cases.diagnosis: Medication overuse headache superimposed on chronic migraine (butalbital combination analgesic and triptan overuse).
pearls: Suspect medication overuse headache when headache is present ≥15 days/month with triptan, opioid, or combination-analgesic use ≥10 days/month (or simple analgesics ≥15 days/month) for >3 months.
Withdrawal of the overused drug is the cornerstone; warn patients headaches transiently worsen before improving, and bridge with naproxen or a short steroid course.
Butalbital and opioids require tapering — abrupt barbiturate cessation risks withdrawal seizures; triptans and NSAIDs can be stopped abruptly.
Start an evidence-based preventive concurrently with withdrawal; apparent preventive 'failures' during active overuse deserve a retrial once overuse stops.

cases.title: Electric shocks in her cheek when she chews
cases.chiefComplaint: "Chewing or even a breeze on my face sets off lightning bolts in my right cheek."
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with two months of recurrent right facial pain. The attacks are sudden, severe, electric-shock-like jolts in the right cheek and lower jaw, each lasting one to ten seconds, sometimes firing in short volleys, up to thirty times a day. Attacks are triggered by chewing, brushing her teeth, applying makeup, cold wind on her face, and lightly touching the right nasolabial fold; after a volley there is a brief refractory period during which touch does not provoke pain. Between attacks she is pain-free. She has begun avoiding meals on the right side and has lost 2 kg. She first saw her dentist, who found no dental pathology; an extraction of a right lower molar gave no relief. She denies facial numbness between attacks, rash or vesicles, hearing loss, vertigo, visual symptoms, limb weakness, or prior episodes of neurologic dysfunction. Past medical history: hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is noncontributory. No known drug allergies. She is of European ancestry.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman, anxious about triggering an attack. Facial sensation intact to light touch, pinprick, and temperature in all three trigeminal divisions bilaterally; corneal reflexes brisk and symmetric. Light touch over the right nasolabial fold provokes a 3-second paroxysm of lancinating pain with facial grimace. Masseter and temporalis bulk and strength normal; jaw opens midline. No vesicles or scars in trigeminal distribution. Facial symmetry normal; hearing intact; remaining cranial nerves normal. Motor, sensory, reflex, and cerebellar exams normal. No temporomandibular joint tenderness or click.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Dental pulpitis of a remaining right molar
stages.explanation: Recurrent unilateral facial pains that are brief (a second to a couple of minutes), electric-shock-like, severe, triggered by innocuous stimuli such as chewing, tooth-brushing, light touch, and wind in the V2/V3 distribution, with trigger zones, refractory periods, and a normal interictal examination define classical trigeminal neuralgia. The failed dental extraction is a classic part of the story—many patients undergo unnecessary dental procedures before diagnosis. Pulpitis causes thermal-sensitive, aching tooth pain localized to a diseased tooth, which her dentist excluded. Postherpetic neuralgia follows a zoster eruption and causes constant burning pain with allodynia plus a history of rash, which she lacks. TMJ dysfunction produces aching pain with jaw movement, joint tenderness, and clicking—her joint exam is normal. Cluster headache produces 15–180-minute periorbital attacks with autonomic features, not seconds-long shocks.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: No further evaluation; begin treatment and never image
MRI brain with thin cuts through the trigeminal nerve, with and without contrast
Nerve conduction studies of the facial nerve
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend MRI in patients presenting with trigeminal neuralgia to distinguish classical disease (neurovascular compression of the trigeminal root) from secondary causes—multiple sclerosis plaques in the pons (especially relevant in younger or bilateral cases), cerebellopontine angle tumors such as vestibular schwannoma or meningioma, and other structural lesions—found in up to 15% of cases. Red flags that particularly mandate imaging include sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, and young onset, but routine MRI is appropriate even in typical cases because examination cannot reliably exclude secondary pathology. Forgoing imaging entirely risks missing a treatable structural cause. Dental radiographs duplicate the dentist's negative workup. Lumbar puncture has no role absent suspicion of meningeal disease. Facial nerve conduction studies assess CN VII (motor) function and are irrelevant to a trigeminal sensory disorder.

stages.label: IMAGING & BASELINE LABS
stages.content:
MRI brain with thin-section trigeminal protocol:
Vascular loop (superior cerebellar artery) contacting and mildly deforming the right trigeminal nerve root entry zone
No demyelinating lesions in pons or elsewhere; no cerebellopontine angle mass
No restricted diffusion; no enhancement
Baseline labs: Na 139 mEq/L, CBC normal, AST/ALT normal, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no AV block

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment?
stages.explanation: Carbamazepine is the established first-line therapy for classical trigeminal neuralgia, effective in roughly 70–80% of patients; it is started low (100–200 mg twice daily) and titrated to pain control, with oxcarbazepine an equally accepted alternative with better tolerability. Counsel about dizziness, sedation, and hyponatremia, monitor sodium and CBC (rare agranulocytosis and aplastic anemia), and screen for HLA-B*15:02 before starting in patients of Han Chinese or Southeast Asian ancestry because of Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk—unnecessary in this patient of European ancestry. Opioids are ineffective for paroxysmal neuropathic facial pain and inappropriate for a chronic disorder. Amitriptyline treats continuous neuropathic pain and headache prophylaxis but performs poorly against trigeminal neuralgia's paroxysms. Sumatriptan is migraine/cluster therapy. Steroids treat inflammatory conditions like GCA; there is no inflammatory process here.

stages.label: REFRACTORY DISEASE
stages.question: After 18 months, attacks recur despite maximum tolerated carbamazepine plus adjunctive baclofen, with dose-limiting dizziness. Which option is most appropriate to discuss next?
options: Push carbamazepine above the maximum tolerated dose despite ataxia
Therapeutic tooth extractions on the affected side
Referral for microvascular decompression of the trigeminal nerve
stages.explanation: For medically refractory classical trigeminal neuralgia with documented neurovascular compression, microvascular decompression—repositioning the offending superior cerebellar artery loop away from the nerve root with a Teflon pledget—offers the highest rate of immediate and durable pain relief (initial relief around 90%, with most patients still pain-free at 5–10 years) and preserves facial sensation; her imaging showing a compressive vascular loop makes her an ideal candidate. Less invasive alternatives for poorer surgical candidates include percutaneous rhizotomy (radiofrequency, glycerol, balloon) and stereotactic radiosurgery, which trade lower invasiveness for higher recurrence and risk of facial numbness. Escalating carbamazepine beyond tolerability simply converts pain into disabling ataxia and hyponatremia risk. Opioids remain ineffective for this paroxysmal pain. Further dental extractions repeat a documented error—her teeth were never the problem.

cases.diagnosis: Classical trigeminal neuralgia (right V2/V3) due to neurovascular compression by a superior cerebellar artery loop.
pearls: Trigeminal neuralgia: seconds-long unilateral electric-shock pains in V2/V3 with cutaneous trigger zones and refractory periods; the exam is normal in classical disease — sensory loss or bilateral pain suggests a secondary cause.
Obtain MRI in all patients to exclude secondary causes (MS in younger patients, cerebellopontine angle masses) and to identify neurovascular compression.
Carbamazepine (or oxcarbazepine) is first-line; monitor sodium and CBC, and screen HLA-B*15:02 in patients of Asian ancestry before starting.
Microvascular decompression provides the most durable relief in medically refractory classical disease with demonstrated vascular compression.

cases.title: Headache that disappears when she lies down
cases.chiefComplaint: I get a pounding headache within minutes of standing up, and it melts away when I lie flat.
cases.history: For the past three weeks she has had a daily headache that begins within 15 minutes of sitting or standing and resolves almost completely after she lies down for 20-30 minutes. The pain is bilateral, occipital, and described as a heavy pulling sensation, occasionally radiating to the neck. She reports muffled hearing, a 'whooshing' tinnitus, mild neck stiffness, and nausea when upright. There has been no fever, photophobia, visual loss, or weakness. She denies recent lumbar puncture, epidural anesthesia, or head trauma, though she took up vigorous Pilates two months ago. PMH: hypermobile joints, seasonal allergies. Medications: combined oral contraceptive; ibuprofen and acetaminophen have given no relief. No allergies. Social: works as a pediatric nurse, no tobacco, social alcohol. Family history: mother with migraine. She is frustrated because over-the-counter analgesics and increased caffeine have not helped, and the orthostatic pattern is interfering with her ability to stand at work.
cases.exam: Alert, in no distress while supine. Neck is mildly stiff with full range of motion. Cranial nerves are intact; fundoscopy shows no papilledema. There is no focal motor or sensory deficit; reflexes are symmetric and plantars flexor. Gait is normal, and Kernig and Brudzinski signs are negative. Mild bilateral hearing dullness is noted. When asked to stand for several minutes she develops the characteristic headache and appears uncomfortable, with prompt relief on lying down. Skin shows hyperextensible features and small-joint hypermobility. No rash and no proptosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test best evaluates her orthostatic headache?
options: Immediate lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
CT angiography of the head and neck
stages.explanation: MRI brain with gadolinium is the best first study: in spontaneous intracranial hypotension it reveals the classic signs of low CSF volume - diffuse smooth pachymeningeal (dural) enhancement, brain 'sagging', engorged venous sinuses, pituitary hyperemia, and subdural fluid collections. Non-contrast CT is insensitive and would miss meningeal enhancement, useful mainly to exclude gross hemorrhage. Immediate lumbar puncture is premature and can worsen a CSF leak; opening pressure may also be normal, and imaging should come first. CT angiography evaluates vessels for dissection or aneurysm, which the orthostatic, postural-relieving pattern does not suggest. The strongly positional headache that resolves when recumbent is the hallmark of low CSF pressure, so contrast MRI targeting the meninges is the highest-yield initial step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with gadolinium: diffuse smooth pachymeningeal enhancement
Downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils ('brain sag')
Distended dural venous sinuses; pituitary engorgement
Small bilateral subdural fluid collections
CBC, CMP, CRP: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The combination of an orthostatic headache (worse upright, relieved supine) with MRI showing diffuse pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag, venous engorgement, and subdural collections is diagnostic of spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a spinal CSF leak. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension causes papilledema, high opening pressure, and headache often worse lying down - the opposite pattern. Chiari I malformation can cause cough or exertional headache and tonsillar descent but lacks pachymeningeal enhancement and strict orthostatic relief; here tonsillar descent is secondary to brain sag. Chronic migraine does not produce these imaging findings or the rapid postural resolution. Her connective-tissue laxity is a recognized risk factor for dural weakness and spontaneous leaks.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best next step to localize the source of her CSF leak?
options: Spinal imaging - CT myelography or heavily T2-weighted spine MRI
Radionuclide cisternography of the brain only
stages.explanation: Once spontaneous intracranial hypotension is established on brain MRI, the leak almost always arises from the spine, so spinal imaging - heavily T2-weighted spine MRI or dynamic CT myelography - is used to localize the dural defect, meningeal diverticula, or a CSF-venous fistula before targeted treatment. Repeating the brain MRI delays care and does not find the leak. Radionuclide cisternography can support a leak but limiting it to the brain is wrong - the spine must be imaged, and it is less precise than CT myelography for pinpointing the site. EEG has no role; she has no features of seizure. Identifying the leak level guides whether a targeted versus non-targeted blood patch or surgical repair is needed.

stages.label: SPINAL IMAGING
stages.content:
CT myelography: contrast extravasation at the right T7-T8 nerve root sleeve
No CSF-venous fistula identified elsewhere
Lumbar opening pressure (lateral decubitus): 4 cm H2O (low)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Conservative measures have failed. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Therapeutic lumbar puncture with CSF drainage
stages.explanation: Epidural blood patch is the treatment of choice for spontaneous intracranial hypotension when conservative measures (bed rest, hydration, caffeine) fail. Autologous blood injected into the epidural space tamponades the leak and seals the dural defect; it can be targeted to the identified T7-T8 level or given lumbar, and may be repeated. Corticosteroids provide no durable benefit and are not standard. Therapeutic lumbar puncture with CSF drainage would lower CSF volume further and worsen symptoms - exactly the wrong direction. Acetazolamide reduces CSF production and is used for intracranial hypertension, not hypotension, so it would aggravate the problem. If multiple blood patches fail, surgical repair or transvenous embolization of a CSF-venous fistula is considered.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She improves after the blood patch. Which rebound symptom should you counsel her to expect and monitor for?
options: Rebound intracranial hypertension with headache worse when lying down
stages.explanation: After a successful blood patch some patients develop transient rebound intracranial hypertension - a headache that paradoxically worsens when recumbent and improves upright, sometimes with nausea or blurred vision, as CSF dynamics normalize. It is usually self-limited and responds to head elevation and short-term acetazolamide if severe; recognizing it prevents misdiagnosis as a failed patch. Permanent hearing loss is unlikely - the muffled hearing and tinnitus of the condition typically resolve with treatment. Seizures are not an expected consequence of blood patching. Acute angle-closure glaucoma is unrelated to CSF dynamics. Counseling about the reversed postural pattern reassures the patient and guides appropriate, conservative management.

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous intracranial hypotension due to a spinal CSF leak
pearls: Orthostatic headache (worse upright, relieved within minutes of lying flat) is the hallmark of low CSF pressure.
Brain MRI with gadolinium shows diffuse smooth pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag, venous engorgement, and subdural collections (SEEPS).
Connective-tissue laxity predisposes to spontaneous dural tears; the leak is almost always spinal - image the spine to localize it.
Epidural blood patch is first-line definitive therapy; watch for transient rebound intracranial hypertension afterward.

cases.title: A trembling hand and moving in slow motion
cases.chiefComplaint: My right hand shakes when it is just resting in my lap, and everyone says I have gotten slower.
cases.history: Over the past 12-18 months his wife has noticed his right hand trembling when at rest, especially while watching television; the shaking lessens when he reaches for objects and disappears in sleep. He feels generally slower - buttoning shirts and rising from chairs take longer - and his handwriting has become small and cramped. He has lost his sense of smell, acts out vivid dreams (kicking and shouting) at night, and has chronic constipation. There is no history of falls, fainting, or urinary incontinence. He denies exposure to antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or other dopamine blockers. PMH: hypertension, well controlled. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. No allergies. Social: retired accountant, never smoked, occasional wine. Family history: no known movement disorders. He is independent in daily activities but worried because his father, who 'had the shakes', eventually needed a walker.
cases.exam: A 4-6 Hz pill-rolling resting tremor of the right hand attenuates with voluntary movement. There is cogwheel rigidity at the right wrist, greater than the left. Bradykinesia is evident on finger tapping with decrementing amplitude. Hypomimia and reduced blink rate are present. Gait shows stooped posture, shortened stride, and reduced right arm swing; he turns en bloc. The pull test is normal with no retropulsion. Extraocular movements are full with no vertical gaze restriction. There are no cerebellar signs, strength and sensation are normal, reflexes are symmetric, and plantars are flexor. No orthostatic blood-pressure drop is detected.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease?
options: Asymmetric resting tremor with bradykinesia and cogwheel rigidity
Early falls within the first year
stages.explanation: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease classically begins asymmetrically with the triad of a resting tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity (often cogwheel). This patient's unilateral 4-6 Hz pill-rolling rest tremor, decrementing finger taps, and right-sided cogwheeling fit perfectly, supported by premotor clues - hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, and constipation. Symmetric onset is more typical of drug-induced or atypical parkinsonism, not Parkinson's disease, which starts on one side. Early falls in the first year suggest progressive supranuclear palsy. Prominent early autonomic failure - orthostatic hypotension, urinary or erectile dysfunction - points toward multiple system atrophy. The asymmetry, rest-predominant tremor, and supportive non-motor history make idiopathic Parkinson's disease the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: age-appropriate atrophy, no vascular or structural lesion
TSH, B12, CMP: within normal limits
Clinical assessment: asymmetric rest tremor, bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity
No orthostatic hypotension on repeat standing measurements

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The clinical picture - asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, and a normal structural MRI - is diagnostic of idiopathic Parkinson's disease, which remains a clinical diagnosis. Essential tremor produces a bilateral action and postural tremor without rest tremor, bradykinesia, or rigidity, and often improves with alcohol. Drug-induced parkinsonism is excluded by the absence of dopamine-blocking medications and is typically symmetric. Multiple system atrophy features early, prominent autonomic failure and poor levodopa response, neither present here. The rest-predominant, asymmetric onset with a classic non-motor prodrome makes Parkinson's disease the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication is the most effective for controlling his motor symptoms?
stages.explanation: Levodopa combined with carbidopa is the most effective therapy for the motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease and provides the greatest benefit for bradykinesia and rigidity; a clear, sustained response also supports the diagnosis. Propranolol treats essential tremor, not the rest tremor and bradykinesia of Parkinson's disease. Trihexyphenidyl, an anticholinergic, can help tremor in younger patients but is poorly tolerated in older adults because of confusion, constipation, and urinary retention - a bad choice at 68 with constipation. Quetiapine is an antipsychotic used for Parkinson's disease psychosis, not motor symptoms, and its dopamine antagonism could worsen parkinsonism. Dopamine agonists are alternatives but cause more impulse-control and sleep side effects in older patients, so levodopa is preferred here.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: His symptoms improve dramatically on levodopa. What long-term motor complication should you counsel him about?
options: Acute dystonic reaction within hours of the first dose
Cervical dystonia caused by the medication
stages.explanation: With years of levodopa therapy and disease progression, patients commonly develop motor fluctuations ('wearing off' before the next dose) and levodopa-induced dyskinesias - involuntary choreiform movements at peak dose; counseling sets expectations and guides later adjustments such as dose fractionation or adding a COMT or MAO-B inhibitor. Irreversible pulmonary fibrosis is associated with ergot dopamine agonists such as pergolide, not levodopa. Acute dystonic reactions within hours are caused by dopamine-blocking drugs like antipsychotics and metoclopramide - the opposite mechanism. Cervical dystonia is not a typical levodopa complication, although painful 'off'-period dystonia can occur. A robust, sustained levodopa response is itself confirmatory of idiopathic Parkinson's disease, so anticipating these long-term motor complications is part of routine counseling.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease
pearls: Parkinson's disease is a clinical diagnosis: asymmetric rest tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity, often with hyposmia, REM sleep behavior disorder, and constipation as a prodrome.
A rest tremor that disappears with action and a dramatic levodopa response strongly support Parkinson's disease.
Levodopa-carbidopa is the most effective motor therapy; long-term use brings motor fluctuations and dyskinesias.
Red flags for atypical parkinsonism: early falls (PSP), early autonomic failure (MSA), symmetric onset (drug-induced).

cases.title: Shaky hands when holding a coffee cup
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands shake when I hold a cup or write, and it is getting embarrassing at work.
cases.history: He reports a gradually worsening tremor of both hands over roughly ten years, present when holding a posture (arms outstretched) and during action such as writing, eating soup, or pouring coffee. The tremor disappears when his hands are at rest in his lap and is absent during sleep. It worsens with stress, caffeine, and fatigue, and he has noticed that a glass of wine in the evening temporarily calms it. He also has a subtle 'yes-yes' head tremor and a slightly quavering voice. There is no slowness, stiffness, or shuffling gait, and no falls. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. Social: works as a surgical instrument technician, drinks alcohol a few times weekly, no tobacco. Family history: his father and older brother have similar hand tremors, suggesting an autosomal-dominant pattern. He is increasingly self-conscious because the tremor interferes with fine tasks at work and his coworkers have commented on it.
cases.exam: A bilateral, roughly symmetric postural and kinetic tremor of 6-10 Hz is evident with arms outstretched and during finger-to-nose testing, worsening near the target. Handwriting is large and tremulous, and a spiral drawing shows oscillations. A mild 'yes-yes' titubating head tremor and a vocal tremor are present. There is no rest tremor, no bradykinesia, and no cogwheel rigidity. Gait is normal with preserved arm swing, and tandem gait is intact. There is no dysmetria beyond the tremor, tone is normal, and there are no Kayser-Fleischer rings. Cranial nerves and reflexes are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes his tremor from a parkinsonian tremor?
options: It is present at rest and disappears with action
It is an action and postural tremor that worsens with movement and is absent at rest
It is associated with bradykinesia and rigidity
stages.explanation: Essential tremor is an action tremor - postural (arms outstretched) and kinetic (during movement) - that worsens as the hand approaches a target and is absent when the limbs are fully at rest. This is the mirror image of the parkinsonian rest tremor, which is maximal at rest and dampens with purposeful movement, so the rest-predominant option describes Parkinson's disease, not essential tremor. Bradykinesia and rigidity are cardinal features of parkinsonism, not essential tremor, which has normal tone and speed. Levodopa responsiveness characterizes Parkinson's disease; essential tremor does not respond to levodopa. The bilateral, symmetric action tremor with head and voice involvement, family history, and alcohol responsiveness all point to essential tremor.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
TSH, free T4: normal (excludes hyperthyroidism)
CMP including glucose and calcium: normal
Ceruloplasmin: 32 mg/dL (normal)
Medication review: no stimulants, valproate, or beta-agonists
Neurologic exam: no bradykinesia or rigidity

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Cerebellar intention tremor from a structural lesion
stages.explanation: A bilateral, symmetric postural and kinetic tremor of the hands with head and voice involvement, a positive family history (autosomal dominant), improvement with alcohol, and a normal neurologic exam and workup is classic essential tremor. Parkinson's disease causes an asymmetric rest tremor with bradykinesia and rigidity, none present here. Enhanced physiologic tremor is usually triggered by an identifiable cause - hyperthyroidism, stimulants, anxiety, or medications - all excluded by history and labs, and it lacks a strong family history. An isolated cerebellar intention tremor would be accompanied by other cerebellar signs (dysmetria, ataxia, dysdiadochokinesia) and points to a structural lesion, which his normal exam argues against. The decades-long course and hereditary pattern seal the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He wants treatment because the tremor interferes with work. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Botulinum toxin injections to the forearm
Deep brain stimulation of the thalamus
stages.explanation: Propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker, is a first-line therapy for essential tremor and reduces the amplitude of the action tremor; primidone is the other first-line option and may be combined with it. Levodopa-carbidopa treats Parkinson's disease and has no role in essential tremor. Botulinum toxin is reserved for refractory focal tremor (e.g., head or voice) and can cause hand weakness, so it is not first-line for a bilateral limb tremor. Deep brain stimulation of the ventral intermediate (VIM) thalamic nucleus is highly effective but reserved for severe, medication-refractory cases given its invasiveness. Starting propranolol - after checking for contraindications such as asthma, bradycardia, or decompensated heart failure - is the appropriate next step.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before prescribing propranolol, which comorbidity most warrants caution?
stages.explanation: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker and can precipitate bronchospasm, so asthma or other reactive airway disease is an important relative contraindication; a cardioselective agent or primidone may be preferred in such patients. It should also be used cautiously with bradycardia, high-degree AV block, and decompensated heart failure. Hyperlipidemia is not a contraindication to beta-blockade. Osteoarthritis has no interaction with propranolol. Gastroesophageal reflux disease is unrelated and not a reason to avoid the drug. Screening for pulmonary and cardiac contraindications before starting propranolol is essential; if present, primidone becomes the preferred first-line alternative for essential tremor.

cases.diagnosis: Essential tremor
pearls: Essential tremor is a bilateral action (postural and kinetic) tremor, often with head/voice involvement, a family history, and improvement with alcohol.
Unlike Parkinson's disease, essential tremor has no rest tremor, bradykinesia, or rigidity and does not respond to levodopa.
First-line treatment is propranolol or primidone; DBS of the VIM thalamus is reserved for refractory cases.
Screen for asthma and cardiac contraindications before prescribing a non-selective beta-blocker.

cases.title: Fidgety movements and a changing personality
cases.chiefComplaint: My wife says I cannot sit still and I have been irritable and forgetful for the past year.
cases.history: Over the past 18 months his family has noticed near-constant fidgety, dance-like movements of his fingers, hands, and face that he often blends into purposeful gestures. Initially attributed to 'nervousness', the movements have become more pronounced and now affect his gait, which appears irregular and lurching. Concurrently he has become irritable, impulsive, and apathetic, with declining work performance, poor planning, and short-term memory lapses; his wife reports two months of low mood. He denies neuroleptic or stimulant use and drinks minimal alcohol. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. Social: works as a warehouse supervisor, married with two teenage children, no illicit drugs. Family history is striking: his father developed 'the shakes', personality change, and dementia in his late 40s and died in a care facility; a paternal aunt had similar problems - an apparent autosomal-dominant pattern. He is worried both about himself and about what this means for his children.
cases.exam: There are continuous, irregular, non-rhythmic choreiform movements involving the face (grimacing), tongue, fingers, and trunk that flow from one body part to another and are partly masked as voluntary movements. Motor impersistence is evident - he cannot maintain tongue protrusion or a sustained hand grip ('milkmaid grip'). Vertical and horizontal saccades are slow with delayed initiation. Gait is wide-based and dance-like. Tone is variable and reflexes are normal. There is no rest tremor, rigidity, or cerebellar dysmetria. Cognitive screening reveals reduced executive function and processing speed; affect is irritable and flat. No Kayser-Fleischer rings are seen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How would you best characterize his involuntary movements?
stages.explanation: His continuous, irregular, non-rhythmic, flowing movements that migrate from one body part to another and are partially incorporated into voluntary actions are classic chorea; motor impersistence (inability to sustain tongue protrusion or grip) supports it. A resting tremor is rhythmic and oscillatory, seen in Parkinson's disease, not random and flowing. Myoclonus consists of sudden, brief, shock-like jerks, not the writhing, continuous quality described. Dystonia produces sustained or patterned twisting postures rather than the unpredictable, dance-like movements here. Recognizing the movement as chorea, combined with behavioral change and a dominant family history, narrows the differential toward a hereditary choreic syndrome.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MRI brain: atrophy of the caudate nuclei with enlargement of the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles
TSH, CBC, CMP: normal
Peripheral smear: no acanthocytes
Ceruloplasmin and copper studies: normal
HIV and RPR: non-reactive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test would confirm the most likely diagnosis?
options: Genetic testing for CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene
stages.explanation: The picture - adult-onset chorea, behavioral and cognitive decline, caudate atrophy on MRI, and an autosomal-dominant family history - is Huntington's disease, confirmed by detecting a pathologic CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion (>=36, fully penetrant >=40) in the HTT gene on chromosome 4. Ceruloplasmin and urinary copper test for Wilson's disease, already excluded by normal studies and the absence of Kayser-Fleischer rings; Wilson's also typically presents younger. Anti-streptolysin O screens for Sydenham chorea, a post-streptococcal childhood disorder, not relevant here. Acanthocyte count would suggest neuroacanthocytosis, but the smear was normal and the dominant inheritance fits Huntington's. Genetic testing is definitive and also informs reproductive and family counseling.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Before sending the genetic test, what is the most appropriate step?
options: Order the test routinely without special discussion
Refer for formal genetic counseling and obtain informed consent
Test his teenage children first to plan ahead
