stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial approach to this rapidly progressive dementia?
options: Reassure the family and arrange routine outpatient memory-clinic follow-up
Pursue a broad workup including MRI brain with diffusion sequences, lumbar puncture, and labs to exclude treatable causes
Start empiric IV acyclovir for presumed viral encephalitis
Begin donepezil for presumed Alzheimer disease
stages.explanation: Rapidly progressive dementia, cognitive decline over weeks to months, demands an urgent, broad workup to find treatable causes before attributing symptoms to an irreversible degenerative process. Brain MRI with diffusion sequences, lumbar puncture, and labs (TSH, B12, HIV, autoimmune/paraneoplastic antibodies, metabolic panel) screen for autoimmune encephalitis, CNS infection, toxic-metabolic derangements, and neoplasm, several of which are reversible. Reassurance and outpatient follow-up dangerously delay diagnosis of a fulminant process. Empiric acyclovir targets HSV encephalitis, which usually presents acutely with fever and seizures, not this subacute afebrile course. Donepezil treats Alzheimer disease, but starting a symptomatic drug without excluding reversible etiologies is premature and ignores the alarming tempo. Establishing the cause directs both treatment and prognosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL
Na 139 mEq/L
TSH 1.8 mIU/L
Vitamin B12 540 pg/mL
HIV antigen/antibody: negative
MRI brain (DWI/FLAIR): cortical ribboning of bilateral cortices with hyperintensity in the caudate and putamen; no mass or infarct
LP: WBC 2/µL, protein 58 mg/dL, glucose 64 mg/dL; autoimmune/paraneoplastic panel pending; 14-3-3 and RT-QuIC pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the clinical picture and MRI, which diagnosis is most likely?
stages.explanation: The combination of rapidly progressive dementia, startle myoclonus, and cerebellar/visual signs, with MRI showing cortical ribboning and basal ganglia restricted diffusion, is classic for sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Alzheimer disease progresses over years, not weeks, and lacks early myoclonus and DWI ribboning. Autoimmune limbic encephalitis is an important mimic that must be excluded with antibody panels, but it typically shows mesial temporal T2/FLAIR change and often responds to immunotherapy; the diffuse cortical and striatal DWI pattern here favors prion disease. Wernicke encephalopathy causes confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia in malnourished or alcoholic patients with mammillary body and periaqueductal signal, not this presentation. The fulminant course plus characteristic DWI changes point strongly to CJD, which RT-QuIC can confirm.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which test offers the best in-vivo confirmation of prion disease?
stages.explanation: CSF real-time quaking-induced conversion (RT-QuIC) detects pathologic prion protein with very high specificity (~99%) and good sensitivity, and is now incorporated into diagnostic criteria for probable CJD. EEG periodic sharp-wave complexes (~1 Hz) support the diagnosis but are insensitive and can be absent or appear late. Repeating MRI delays diagnosis and adds little once cortical ribboning is seen. Brain biopsy or autopsy remains the definitive gold standard but is invasive, carries transmission risk, and is rarely necessary now that RT-QuIC is available. The older CSF 14-3-3 and total-tau markers are supportive but nonspecific, rising with any rapid neuronal injury. RT-QuIC offers the best confirmation while avoiding biopsy.

stages.label: EEG & CSF
stages.content:
EEG: periodic sharp-wave complexes at approximately 1 Hz
CSF RT-QuIC: positive
CSF 14-3-3 protein: positive
CSF total tau: markedly elevated
Autoimmune/paraneoplastic antibody panel: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: Supportive care, clonazepam for myoclonus, and discussion of prognosis with early palliative involvement
stages.explanation: Sporadic CJD is invariably fatal, typically within a year, and no therapy halts progression; care is supportive with attention to symptom control, safety, and goals-of-care and palliative planning. Myoclonus responds to clonazepam or levetiracetam. High-dose corticosteroids treat autoimmune encephalitis, not prion disease. IVIG and plasma exchange are immunomodulatory therapies with no role here once CJD is confirmed by RT-QuIC. Standard sterilization does not inactivate prions, so reusable instruments require special decontamination, and cases should be reported to prion surveillance programs. Honest prognostic counseling and early palliative involvement are central to management.

cases.diagnosis: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (prion disease)
pearls: Rapidly progressive dementia plus myoclonus and cerebellar/visual signs is the classic CJD triad.
MRI DWI shows cortical ribboning and basal ganglia restricted diffusion before atrophy appears.
CSF RT-QuIC is highly sensitive and specific and has largely replaced biopsy for diagnosis.
CJD is uniformly fatal with no cure; prions resist standard sterilization, so report cases and use special instrument decontamination.

cases.title: Fever, stiff neck, and a spreading rash
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a terrible headache and fever, and my neck is so stiff I can't touch my chin to my chest.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college freshman living in a crowded dormitory presents with a 12-hour history of severe headache, high fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and nausea with one episode of vomiting. He felt mildly unwell this morning and deteriorated rapidly over the day. A few hours ago he noticed small reddish-purple spots on his lower legs that do not fade when pressed. He denies recent travel, head trauma, or known sick contacts, though he lives in close quarters with many other students. He completed his childhood vaccinations but never received a meningococcal booster. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies, which is reassuring for antibiotic selection. Social history: drinks alcohol socially on weekends, denies tobacco or recreational drugs, and is sexually active. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing and photophobic, lying still in a dim room. There is clear nuchal rigidity with positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs. Non-blanching petechiae are scattered over the lower extremities and trunk. He is alert with a GCS of 15 but uncomfortable, and there are no focal neurologic deficits. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. Mucous membranes are dry. The cardiovascular exam is notable for tachycardia, and the lungs are clear. There is no joint swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform lumbar puncture first and withhold antibiotics until CSF results return
Obtain a CT head before doing anything else
Draw blood cultures and immediately give dexamethasone plus empiric ceftriaxone and vancomycin, then perform the lumbar puncture
Await lumbar puncture results before starting any treatment
stages.explanation: In suspected bacterial meningitis, empiric therapy must not be delayed because mortality rises with every hour. This young, immunocompetent patient with normal mental status, no focal deficits, and no papilledema does not require CT before LP, but the priority is still to draw blood cultures and immediately give dexamethasone plus empiric ceftriaxone and vancomycin, then perform the LP. Doing the LP before any antibiotics is acceptable only if it causes no delay; here treatment should not wait. Routine CT before LP is unnecessary without focal signs, altered mentation, immunocompromise, or papilledema, and only delays care. Awaiting LP results before treating is dangerous in a rapidly progressive, potentially fatal infection. Antibiotics given shortly before LP rarely sterilize CSF enough to obscure the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & LP
stages.content:
WBC 18.4 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Platelets 96 ×10³/µL
Lactate 3.1 mmol/L
CSF opening pressure: 28 cm H2O
CSF WBC 4500/µL (90% neutrophils)
CSF glucose 20 mg/dL (serum glucose 96 mg/dL)
CSF protein 250 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: gram-negative diplococci

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which organism is the most likely cause?
stages.explanation: Gram-negative diplococci in the CSF of a college student living in a dormitory, with a non-blanching petechial rash, indicate Neisseria meningitidis. Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis overall but appears as gram-positive diplococci and rarely causes petechiae. Listeria monocytogenes is a gram-positive rod that affects neonates, the elderly, pregnant, and immunocompromised hosts and is covered empirically with ampicillin in those groups. Haemophilus influenzae appears as pleomorphic gram-negative coccobacilli and is now uncommon due to vaccination. The combination of morphology, the crowded-living risk factor, and the purpuric rash (reflecting meningococcemia) confirms N. meningitidis, which can progress to fulminant sepsis and adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome).

stages.label: ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY
stages.question: What is the correct role and timing of adjunctive dexamethasone?
options: Give it only after 48 hours of antibiotics
Avoid corticosteroids entirely in bacterial meningitis
It has no proven benefit in any form of bacterial meningitis
Give it before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce neurologic complications
stages.explanation: Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces neurologic sequelae, notably hearing loss, and mortality in bacterial meningitis and is most beneficial in pneumococcal disease; the key is timing. It must be given before or concurrently with the first antibiotic dose, because its benefit comes from blunting the inflammatory response to bacterial lysis. Giving it 48 hours later is ineffective. Withholding steroids or claiming no benefit contradicts the evidence; although benefit is clearest for pneumococcus, dexamethasone is started empirically before the organism is known. It is generally discontinued if a non-pneumococcal cause such as meningococcus is confirmed, but starting it up front, before or with the first antibiotic dose, is the standard of care.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: How should the patient's roommates and close contacts be managed?
options: Chemoprophylaxis (e.g., rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) and reporting to public health
No action is needed for contacts
A full 7-day course of IV antibiotics for everyone in the dormitory
stages.explanation: Close contacts of meningococcal disease, including household members, roommates, and anyone exposed to oral secretions, require chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases; the case must be reported to public health, and droplet precautions are maintained for the first 24 hours of therapy. Doing nothing risks an outbreak. Treating an entire dormitory with full IV courses is unnecessary and harmful; only close contacts need prophylaxis. Vaccination supports outbreak control but acts too slowly to protect already-exposed contacts, so chemoprophylaxis is the immediate priority.

cases.diagnosis: Meningococcal (Neisseria meningitidis) meningitis with meningococcemia
pearls: Never delay antibiotics for imaging or LP when bacterial meningitis is suspected.
Give dexamethasone before or with the first antibiotic dose; its benefit is greatest in pneumococcal meningitis.
A petechial or purpuric rash with meningitis signals meningococcemia; watch for shock and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.
Close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone) and the case must be reported to public health.

cases.title: Fever and sudden confusion with strange behavior
cases.chiefComplaint: Her husband says she's burning up, talking nonsense, and just had a seizure.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman is brought in by her husband for a 3-day history of fever, headache, and progressive confusion. He reports she became uncharacteristically agitated and irritable, with word-finding difficulty and trouble remembering recent events. This morning she described a strange smell that no one else noticed, and an hour ago she had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about a minute, followed by drowsiness. She has had no recent travel, no immunosuppression, and no known sick contacts. PMH: hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, drinks one glass of wine a few nights weekly, no recreational drugs, works as an accountant. No tuberculosis exposure. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient is febrile and disoriented, oriented only to self, with an expressive aphasia and difficulty naming objects. She is postictal and drowsy but rousable. There is no neck stiffness or only mild discomfort on flexion, and no photophobia. Cranial nerves are grossly intact, with no facial droop. Motor exam shows no focal weakness, and reflexes are symmetric. There is no rash. Fundoscopy reveals no papilledema. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start empiric IV acyclovir immediately and obtain neuroimaging and a lumbar puncture, without waiting for PCR
Await the HSV PCR result before starting any antiviral therapy
Begin high-dose corticosteroids as primary therapy
stages.explanation: Fever, altered mentation, and focal features such as aphasia and seizure over days strongly suggest encephalitis, with HSV-1 the most common sporadic cause and a true emergency. The single most important action is to start empiric IV acyclovir immediately while obtaining neuroimaging (MRI preferred) and an LP; treatment must not await PCR confirmation, because delay markedly worsens outcomes. Awaiting HSV PCR before treating is the classic error, since untreated HSV encephalitis carries roughly 70% mortality. Ceftriaxone alone covers bacterial meningitis but not HSV; antibiotics may be added empirically, but acyclovir is essential. Corticosteroids are not first-line and never substitute for acyclovir. Acyclovir is safe enough that early empiric use is justified even before the diagnosis is secured.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: asymmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right medial temporal lobe and insula, with petechial hemorrhage and mild restricted diffusion
EEG: periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over the right temporal region
CSF WBC 180/µL (lymphocyte predominant)
CSF RBC 250/µL
CSF glucose 62 mg/dL
CSF protein 95 mg/dL
CSF HSV-1 PCR: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?
stages.explanation: MRI showing asymmetric medial temporal and insular T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, sometimes with hemorrhage, temporal PLEDs on EEG, and a lymphocytic CSF with red cells points to HSV-1 encephalitis. Bacterial meningitis produces neutrophilic CSF with low glucose and high protein and lacks this temporal-lobe imaging signature. Anti-NMDA-receptor encephalitis can mimic this with psychiatric change and seizures, often in young women with an ovarian teratoma, but typically lacks the necrotizing temporal MRI pattern and is confirmed by antibody testing, making it a reasonable parallel workup. Ischemic stroke follows a vascular territory, presents abruptly, and does not cause fever with diffuse encephalopathy. The temporal-lobe predilection is the hallmark of HSV encephalitis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The initial CSF HSV PCR returns negative at 24 hours, but clinical suspicion remains high. What is the best next step?
options: Stop acyclovir, since the PCR is negative
Continue acyclovir and repeat the lumbar puncture with HSV PCR in 3-7 days
Discharge the patient home with follow-up
stages.explanation: HSV PCR sensitivity is high but can be falsely negative very early, within the first 72 hours, before sufficient viral DNA accumulates in the CSF. With a compatible clinical and imaging picture, you should continue acyclovir and repeat the LP in 3-7 days rather than stopping therapy. Stopping acyclovir on a single early negative PCR risks a catastrophic relapse of a treatable, lethal infection. Corticosteroids are adjunctive at best and do not address the virus. Discharge is unsafe in an unstable encephalitic patient. Empiric acyclovir is continued until HSV is reliably excluded or an alternative diagnosis is confirmed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which measure is most important to prevent a complication of the therapy itself?
options: Restrict IV fluids during acyclovir infusion
Administer nephrotoxic contrast frequently to monitor the lesion
No specific monitoring is required during acyclovir therapy
Maintain adequate IV hydration and monitor renal function to prevent crystalline nephropathy
stages.explanation: IV acyclovir can precipitate in the renal tubules and cause crystalline nephropathy, especially with rapid infusion or volume depletion; maintaining good IV hydration, infusing over at least an hour, and monitoring renal function and dosing are essential. Fluid restriction increases the risk of crystal nephropathy. Repeated nephrotoxic contrast compounds renal injury and is not indicated. Claiming no monitoring is needed ignores a well-known, preventable adverse effect. Treatment continues for 14-21 days of IV acyclovir for confirmed HSV encephalitis, and patients should also be watched for cerebral edema, seizures, and SIADH. Hydration plus renal monitoring is the highest-yield safety measure here.

cases.diagnosis: Herpes simplex virus type 1 encephalitis
pearls: HSV-1 is the most common sporadic fatal encephalitis and favors the medial temporal lobes and insula.
Start empiric IV acyclovir immediately; never wait for the PCR result.
Early CSF HSV PCR can be falsely negative; continue acyclovir and repeat the LP if suspicion is high.
Maintain IV hydration and monitor renal function to prevent acyclovir crystalline nephropathy.

cases.title: Weeks of worsening headache and now blurry vision
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a headache for two weeks that keeps getting worse, with fevers, and now my vision is blurry.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with a 2-3 week history of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fevers, malaise, and nausea, now accompanied by photophobia, blurry vision, and intermittent double vision. He has known HIV but stopped his antiretroviral therapy several months ago and has not followed up. He reports a 10-pound weight loss, night sweats, and white patches in his mouth. There is no acute confusion at home but his partner notes he seems slowed down. PMH: HIV infection. Medications: none currently, having self-discontinued ART. No known drug allergies. Social history: man who has sex with men, former injection drug use years ago, current smoker, drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill and cachectic with oral thrush. There is mild nuchal discomfort on flexion and photophobia. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. A right cranial nerve VI palsy is present, with the eye unable to fully abduct. He is mildly inattentive but follows commands, with no focal limb weakness or sensory deficit. Reflexes are symmetric. There is no rash. The remainder of the exam is notable for generalized lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial approach?
options: Perform an immediate lumbar puncture without prior imaging
Obtain a CT head first, then perform a lumbar puncture measuring the opening pressure with CSF studies including cryptococcal antigen
Start broad-spectrum antibacterials only and observe
Obtain an MRI and discharge if no mass is seen
stages.explanation: In a patient with advanced HIV and signs of raised intracranial pressure (papilledema, CN VI palsy) or focal deficits, neuroimaging such as CT should precede LP to exclude a mass lesion and reduce herniation risk. Once safe, LP is essential and must include measurement of the opening pressure plus cryptococcal antigen, India ink, culture, and routine studies. Immediate LP without imaging is unsafe given papilledema and immunocompromise. Empiric antibacterials alone miss the fungal and other opportunistic causes and do not address raised pressure. Obtaining an MRI then discharging ignores a potentially fatal CNS infection requiring admission. Measuring the opening pressure is critical because elevated intracranial pressure drives morbidity and mortality in cryptococcal meningitis and dictates therapeutic LP management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CD4 count 35 cells/µL
CT head: no mass lesion; mildly prominent ventricles
CSF opening pressure: 40 cm H2O
CSF WBC 25/µL (lymphocyte predominant)
CSF glucose 38 mg/dL
CSF protein 88 mg/dL
India ink: encapsulated budding yeast seen
CSF cryptococcal antigen: positive, titer 1:1024
Serum cryptococcal antigen: positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A CD4 count of 35, subacute headache, encapsulated budding yeast on India ink, and a positive CSF and serum cryptococcal antigen confirm cryptococcal meningitis. Tuberculous meningitis is a key differential in AIDS, also causing subacute lymphocytic meningitis with high protein and low glucose, but it lacks encapsulated yeast and a positive CrAg; basilar enhancement and a high CSF adenosine deaminase would suggest it. Bacterial meningitis is acute with neutrophilic CSF and very low glucose. Viral meningitis is usually self-limited with normal glucose and would not show yeast or a strikingly elevated opening pressure. The positive cryptococcal antigen, highly sensitive and specific, plus India ink visualization clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the appropriate induction antifungal regimen?
stages.explanation: Induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis is liposomal amphotericin B combined with flucytosine for at least two weeks, followed by consolidation and maintenance with fluconazole. This combination achieves the fastest CSF sterilization and the best survival. Fluconazole monotherapy is inferior for induction and reserved for consolidation, maintenance, or resource-limited settings. Ceftriaxone plus vancomycin treats bacterial meningitis and has no antifungal activity. Voriconazole is not standard induction for cryptococcus. Flucytosine requires monitoring for cytopenias, and amphotericin requires attention to renal function and electrolytes. Effective antifungal induction plus aggressive control of intracranial pressure are the two pillars of management.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How should the markedly elevated opening pressure (40 cm H2O) be managed?
options: Serial therapeutic lumbar punctures to reduce the opening pressure
stages.explanation: Elevated intracranial pressure is the leading cause of early death in cryptococcal meningitis, and the cornerstone of management is therapeutic large-volume lumbar puncture, repeated daily as needed, to reduce a markedly elevated opening pressure. Corticosteroids do not lower pressure in cryptococcal meningitis and increased mortality in trials, so they are reserved for IRIS. Acetazolamide and mannitol are ineffective or harmful here. Starting ART immediately raises the risk of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome and is deferred. Serial LPs, and a shunt or lumbar drain for refractory pressure, directly reduce morbidity and mortality, making pressure control as important as antifungal therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: When should antiretroviral therapy be (re)started?
options: Immediately, on the same day antifungals are begun
Delayed roughly 4-6 weeks after starting antifungal therapy to reduce IRIS risk
Never, since ART is contraindicated in CNS infection
Only after corticosteroids are given prophylactically
stages.explanation: Antiretroviral therapy should be delayed roughly 4-6 weeks (generally 2-10 weeks) after starting antifungal therapy in cryptococcal meningitis to lower the risk of paradoxical immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS), which can cause life-threatening rises in intracranial pressure and inflammation. Starting ART immediately or very early increased mortality in clinical trials for cryptococcal meningitis. Never starting ART abandons treatment of the underlying HIV. Corticosteroids are not used prophylactically and are reserved for established IRIS or other specific indications. Controlling the fungal burden and intracranial pressure first, then introducing ART, optimizes survival.

cases.diagnosis: Cryptococcal meningitis in advanced HIV/AIDS
pearls: Raised intracranial pressure is the main killer in cryptococcal meningitis; manage with serial therapeutic LPs.
Induction therapy is liposomal amphotericin B plus flucytosine, then fluconazole consolidation and maintenance.
CSF and serum cryptococcal antigen are highly sensitive and specific; India ink shows encapsulated yeast.
Delay ART about 4-6 weeks after antifungal start to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome.

cases.title: Stabbing leg pains and unsteady walking in the dark
cases.chiefComplaint: I get sudden stabbing pains in my legs, and I'm unsteady, especially in the dark.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with several months of intermittent, brief, lightning-like shooting pains in both legs and progressive gait unsteadiness that is noticeably worse in the dark or when his eyes are closed. He has had a few falls, new urinary incontinence, and erectile dysfunction. He recalls a painless genital ulcer decades ago that he never had treated, and several untreated sexually transmitted infections in his youth. He denies weakness, back pain, or recent illness. PMH: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: multiple lifetime sexual partners, current smoker, drinks alcohol regularly, works as a contractor. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The pupils are small and irregular; they constrict to accommodation but do not react to light (Argyll Robertson pupils). There is markedly reduced vibration and proprioception in both lower limbs with preserved pain and temperature. Romberg sign is strongly positive, and the gait is broad-based and stamping (sensory ataxia). Ankle reflexes are absent and tone is reduced in the legs. Motor strength is full, with no spasticity and downgoing plantar responses. There is no cranial nerve deficit other than the pupils.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Serologic syphilis testing (treponemal and nontreponemal) followed by lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including CSF-VDRL
MRI of the lumbar spine only
EMG and nerve conduction studies as the primary test
stages.explanation: The triad of small pupils that accommodate but do not react to light (Argyll Robertson pupils), lancinating lightning pains, and sensory ataxia with a positive Romberg sign points to dorsal column and dorsal root pathology, and neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis) must top the differential. The first step is syphilis serology, a treponemal test (FTA-ABS or TP-PA) with a nontreponemal titer (RPR or VDRL), followed by lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including CSF-VDRL to confirm CNS involvement. Lumbar spine MRI evaluates compressive myelopathy but would not explain the pupils or capture the diagnosis. B12 deficiency causing subacute combined degeneration is a key dorsal-column mimic and should be checked, but it does not cause Argyll Robertson pupils. EMG and nerve conduction studies assess peripheral nerve, not dorsal column or root disease.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
RPR: reactive, titer 1:64
FTA-ABS: reactive
Vitamin B12: 480 pg/mL (normal)
HIV antigen/antibody: negative
CSF WBC 22/µL (lymphocyte predominant)
CSF protein 70 mg/dL
CSF-VDRL: reactive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency
stages.explanation: Reactive serum RPR and FTA-ABS with a reactive CSF-VDRL and CSF pleocytosis confirm neurosyphilis; the clinical picture of dorsal column and dorsal root degeneration, with sensory ataxia, lightning pains, areflexia, and Argyll Robertson pupils, defines tabes dorsalis. Subacute combined degeneration from B12 deficiency causes similar dorsal column loss but with a normal B12 here and no Argyll Robertson pupils, and it often adds spasticity from corticospinal involvement. Diabetic neuropathy causes distal sensory loss but not Argyll Robertson pupils or a reactive CSF-VDRL. Multiple sclerosis produces CNS demyelination with different MRI and CSF findings such as oligoclonal bands and does not cause reactive syphilis serology. The reactive CSF-VDRL is essentially confirmatory in this context.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate treatment?
options: A single IM dose of benzathine penicillin G
IV aqueous crystalline penicillin G, 18-24 million units per day for 10-14 days
stages.explanation: Neurosyphilis requires IV aqueous crystalline penicillin G, 18-24 million units per day, typically 3-4 million units IV every 4 hours or as a continuous infusion, for 10-14 days, because adequate treponemicidal CSF concentrations are needed. A single IM dose of benzathine penicillin treats early or latent syphilis but does not achieve CSF levels and is inadequate for neurosyphilis. Oral doxycycline is only a weaker fallback alternative. A single ceftriaxone dose is insufficient; a 10-14 day ceftriaxone course is a possible alternative, but penicillin is preferred. IV penicillin G is the clear standard of care for CNS involvement.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The patient reports a documented severe penicillin allergy. What is the best approach?
options: Give a single IM benzathine dose as an alternative
Perform penicillin desensitization and treat with IV penicillin G
stages.explanation: Penicillin is the only therapy with proven reliable efficacy for neurosyphilis, so a penicillin-allergic patient should undergo desensitization and then receive IV penicillin G; this mirrors the strategy used in pregnant patients with syphilis. Azithromycin has documented resistance and is not adequate for neurosyphilis. Withholding treatment allows irreversible neurologic progression. A single IM benzathine dose fails to treat CNS disease regardless of allergy. Clinicians should also warn patients about the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, myalgias, worsening symptoms) within hours of the first dose, and should follow CSF and serologic titers to confirm cure.

cases.diagnosis: Neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis)
pearls: Argyll Robertson pupils accommodate to near vision but do not react to light.
Tabes dorsalis is dorsal column and root degeneration: sensory ataxia, lightning pains, areflexia, and Charcot joints.
Treat neurosyphilis with IV aqueous penicillin G for 10-14 days, not a single IM benzathine dose.
Penicillin-allergic patients should be desensitized and treated with penicillin; watch for the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction.

cases.title: Worsening headache, fever, and a weakening arm
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a headache and fevers for over a week, and now my left arm feels weak.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man is admitted for a 10-day history of progressively worsening headache and low-grade fevers, now with new left-arm weakness and a single brief focal seizure of the left hand witnessed today. The headache is dull, constant, and worse when lying flat. He has poor dental hygiene and underwent a molar extraction about three weeks ago for a painful dental abscess, without prophylactic antibiotics. He denies sinus symptoms, ear pain, recent travel, or known immunosuppression. PMH: untreated dental disease, otherwise healthy. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: current smoker, drinks alcohol moderately, works in construction. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented. There is a mild left pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the left arm and increased reflexes on the left, with an equivocal left plantar response. The oral exam shows poor dentition and gingival disease near a recent extraction site. There is no neck stiffness and no photophobia. Fundoscopy shows early blurring of the disc margins. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, sensation is normal, and there is no rash. The cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the safest next step in evaluation?
options: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain before any lumbar puncture
EEG as the initial diagnostic test
stages.explanation: A subacute headache with fever, a new focal deficit, and a focal seizure suggests a focal intracranial process, and the safe next step is contrast-enhanced neuroimaging, with MRI preferred and CT if MRI is unavailable, before any LP. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when a mass lesion with focal deficits is suspected because of herniation risk, and CSF is often nondiagnostic in abscess anyway. Reassurance ignores red-flag focal and infectious features. EEG may characterize the seizure but does not identify the structural cause. Imaging localizes the lesion, characterizes it through enhancement and diffusion, and guides whether neurosurgical sampling is needed, making it the essential first step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL
ESR 64 mm/hr
CRP 88 mg/L
Blood cultures: pending
MRI brain with contrast: 2.8 cm ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontal lobe with central restricted diffusion on DWI and surrounding vasogenic edema; mild local mass effect

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A ring-enhancing lesion whose center shows restricted diffusion on DWI is characteristic of a pyogenic brain abscess; the viscous, cellular pus restricts water diffusion, unlike most tumors. Glioblastoma and metastasis also ring-enhance but typically show facilitated, not restricted, central diffusion with central necrosis, and metastases are usually multiple with a known primary. Toxoplasmosis causes ring-enhancing lesions but occurs in advanced HIV or immunosuppression, which this patient lacks. The combination of an infectious prodrome, a clear odontogenic source, and central restricted diffusion strongly favors abscess. DWI is the key sequence distinguishing abscess from necrotic tumor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate management?
options: Empiric IV antibiotics alone without any sampling
Stereotactic aspiration plus empiric IV ceftriaxone and metronidazole
stages.explanation: Management of a pyogenic brain abscess combines neurosurgical stereotactic aspiration, which both decompresses the lesion and provides material for Gram stain and culture to target therapy, with prolonged empiric IV antibiotics. For an odontogenic source, a regimen covering streptococci and oral anaerobes such as ceftriaxone plus metronidazole is appropriate, with vancomycin added if MRSA is a concern. Antibiotics alone may suffice for small lesions under about 2.5 cm, but a sizeable lesion with deficits warrants aspiration plus culture-directed therapy. Corticosteroids reduce edema but do not treat infection and can impair antibiotic penetration, so they are reserved for significant mass effect. Radiation has no role in infection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the dental source, which organisms are most likely?
options: Staphylococcus aureus from a hematogenous skin source
Viridans/anginosus-group streptococci and oral anaerobes (e.g., Fusobacterium, Prevotella)
stages.explanation: Odontogenic brain abscesses are typically polymicrobial, dominated by oral flora: viridans and Streptococcus anginosus (milleri) group streptococci together with anaerobes such as Fusobacterium, Prevotella, and Peptostreptococcus. This is why empiric coverage pairs a third-generation cephalosporin with metronidazole. Pseudomonas is associated with otogenic sources or neurosurgery and trauma, not teeth. S. aureus suggests endocarditis, penetrating trauma, or hematogenous skin and soft-tissue seeding. Tuberculosis causes tuberculomas in endemic or immunocompromised settings with a different clinical and imaging course. Matching empiric antibiotics to the likely oral pathogens is key while awaiting cultures, and the dental source should be definitively treated.

cases.diagnosis: Pyogenic brain abscess (odontogenic source)
pearls: A ring-enhancing lesion with central restricted diffusion on DWI favors abscess over necrotic tumor.
Avoid lumbar puncture when a mass lesion with focal deficits is suspected; image first.
Treat with stereotactic aspiration plus IV antibiotics and control the primary source.
Odontogenic abscesses are polymicrobial (strep plus oral anaerobes); cover with ceftriaxone plus metronidazole.

cases.title: First-ever seizure in a previously healthy man
cases.chiefComplaint: I had a seizure for the first time; my coworkers said I collapsed and was shaking.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man is brought in after a witnessed first-ever generalized tonic-clonic seizure at work, lasting about 90 seconds with postictal confusion that has since resolved. He recalls occasional mild headaches over the past months but no prior seizures, head trauma, or focal weakness. He immigrated two years ago from a rural region of Central America and works in agriculture and food preparation. He denies alcohol use, recreational drugs, and fevers. PMH: none. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. Social history: lives with extended family, no alcohol or tobacco, has had exposure to pork and areas with limited sanitation. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient is now alert and fully oriented after a brief postictal period. There is a small lateral tongue laceration. The neurologic exam shows no focal weakness, normal sensation, symmetric reflexes, and no meningismus. Cranial nerves are intact. Fundoscopic exam reveals no papilledema and no visible intraocular lesions. He is afebrile with no rash. The general exam is otherwise unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step after a first unprovoked adult seizure?
options: Start a lifelong antiepileptic and discharge without imaging
Obtain neuroimaging (CT or MRI of the brain) to identify a structural cause
Perform a lumbar puncture as the first test
stages.explanation: Any first unprovoked seizure in an adult requires neuroimaging to evaluate for a structural cause, and in an immigrant from an endemic region this is especially important. CT or MRI of the brain is the essential next step. Starting a lifelong antiepileptic and discharging without imaging misses a treatable structural lesion and is below the standard of care. EEG helps characterize seizure type and recurrence risk but does not reveal structural pathology. Lumbar puncture is not the initial step for a first seizure and is hazardous if imaging shows mass effect. Imaging both identifies the etiology, here cysts, and guides whether antiparasitic therapy and steroids are indicated.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: multiple punctate calcified lesions in the parenchyma plus a cystic lesion with an eccentric hyperdense nodule (scolex) and surrounding edema
MRI brain: confirms a cystic lesion with a 'cyst-with-dot' appearance and mild rim enhancement; several calcified foci
Serum cysticercus antibody (EITB): positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Multiple lesions including calcified foci plus a cystic lesion containing an eccentric scolex, the cyst-with-dot sign, in a patient from a Taenia solium-endemic area are diagnostic of neurocysticercosis, the leading cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide. CNS tuberculomas occur in endemic or immunocompromised patients but lack a visible scolex and usually show different enhancement. Metastases are typically at the gray-white junction with surrounding edema and a known primary, without a scolex. Toxoplasmosis causes ring-enhancing lesions in advanced HIV, not calcified cysts with scolices. Serology such as EITB Western blot supports the diagnosis, but the imaging appearance with a scolex is characteristic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of viable parenchymal cysts?
options: Antiepileptic drugs plus albendazole given together with corticosteroids
Immediate surgical resection of all lesions
stages.explanation: Management is staged. Symptomatic seizures are controlled with antiepileptic drugs, and for viable cystic parenchymal lesions, antiparasitic therapy with albendazole (with or without praziquantel) is given together with corticosteroids to blunt the inflammatory reaction provoked by dying cysts. Antiparasitics alone can dramatically worsen edema and seizures by triggering inflammation around degenerating cysts, so steroids must accompany them. Corticosteroids alone do not clear viable parasites. Immediate surgery is reserved for hydrocephalus or intraventricular cysts, not routine parenchymal disease. Importantly, if only calcified, dead lesions are present, antiparasitics are not indicated and only seizure management is needed. Always pair antiparasitic therapy with steroids and antiepileptics.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which step is essential before starting albendazole?
options: Begin antiparasitics immediately without further testing
Start lifelong phenytoin regardless of lesion type
Perform a funduscopic/ophthalmologic exam to exclude intraocular cysticercosis
stages.explanation: A dilated funduscopic and ophthalmologic examination is required before antiparasitic therapy because killing an intraocular cyst provokes intense inflammation that can cause irreversible vision loss; ocular and high-burden disease are contraindications or require special precautions. Starting albendazole without this step risks blindness if ocular cysts are present. Echocardiography is unrelated. Antiepileptic choice should be individualized rather than lifelong regardless of findings; many patients can eventually taper, especially with calcified-only disease. Confirming the absence of ocular cysts and excessive intracranial cyst burden ensures antiparasitic therapy is given safely.

cases.diagnosis: Neurocysticercosis (Taenia solium)
pearls: Neurocysticercosis is the most common cause of acquired adult-onset seizures in endemic regions.
The 'cyst-with-dot' (scolex) sign and calcified lesions are characteristic imaging findings.
Give corticosteroids with antiparasitics to limit the inflammatory response from dying cysts.
Exclude ocular cysticercosis before antiparasitics; calcified-only lesions need only antiepileptic therapy.

cases.title: Slurred speech and a clumsy hand over several weeks
cases.chiefComplaint: Over the past month my speech has gotten slurred and my right hand has become clumsy.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis presents with about four weeks of progressive right-hand clumsiness, increasingly slurred speech, and mild changes in personality and concentration noted by his wife, along with a sense that his left visual field is 'off.' Unlike his prior relapses, which came on over days and improved, these deficits have crept up steadily without remission and without an obvious sensory level. He has been on natalizumab infusions monthly for the past 2.5 years with good control until now. He had prior immunosuppressant exposure before natalizumab. He denies fever, headache, or recent infection. PMH: relapsing-remitting MS. Medications: natalizumab monthly. No known drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, no alcohol or drugs, works as an engineer. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient has dysarthria and mild right-sided weakness (4+/5) with right-hand incoordination and dysmetria. Visual fields show a partial left homonymous deficit. He has mild cognitive slowing on bedside testing. There is no fever and no meningismus. The deficits are clearly multifocal and have progressed steadily over weeks rather than appearing acutely. Reflexes are mildly asymmetric. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Treat as a typical MS relapse with high-dose IV corticosteroids alone
Obtain MRI brain with contrast and assess JC virus status while holding natalizumab
Increase the natalizumab dose to control disease activity
Reassure the patient and continue current therapy
stages.explanation: A relapsing-remitting MS patient on natalizumab for over two years who develops subacute, progressive, multifocal deficits over weeks should raise immediate concern for progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), not a typical relapse. The next step is urgent MRI of the brain with contrast and assessment of JC virus status while holding further natalizumab. Treating it as an MS relapse with steroids alone can mask and worsen PML by further immunosuppression. Continuing or increasing natalizumab perpetuates the risk. Reassurance ignores a potentially fatal condition. PML risk rises with JCV seropositivity, treatment beyond 24 months, and prior immunosuppressant use, all relevant here, so prompt imaging and JCV testing are essential.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI brain: asymmetric, confluent subcortical/juxtacortical T2/FLAIR white-matter hyperintensities involving the U-fibers, with little mass effect and no significant contrast enhancement
Serum JC virus antibody: positive, high index
CSF cell count: normal
CSF JC virus PCR: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this picture?
stages.explanation: MRI showing asymmetric, subcortical white-matter T2/FLAIR lesions involving the U-fibers, with little mass effect and typically no enhancement, in a JCV-seropositive natalizumab-treated patient is characteristic of PML. An MS relapse usually evolves over days with discrete enhancing demyelinating plaques and often responds to steroids, unlike this insidious multifocal decline. Primary CNS lymphoma forms enhancing masses, frequently periventricular, with restricted diffusion and mass effect. ADEM is typically monophasic and postinfectious with multifocal enhancing lesions, more common in children. The progressive course, lesion pattern, and risk profile point to PML, which is confirmed by detecting JC virus DNA in the CSF.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: What is the best test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics as a diagnostic trial
stages.explanation: Detection of JC virus DNA in the CSF by PCR, in the right clinical and radiologic context, confirms PML; if CSF PCR is negative but suspicion remains high, brain biopsy is the definitive test. An autoimmune encephalitis panel investigates a different syndrome and will not establish PML. Delaying with a repeat MRI in 6 months is unacceptable for a rapidly disabling, potentially fatal disease. Empiric antibiotics target bacterial infection, which this is not. CSF JC virus PCR is the key confirmatory, minimally invasive test and should be obtained promptly.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for natalizumab-associated PML?
options: Discontinue natalizumab and perform plasma exchange to accelerate drug clearance
stages.explanation: There is no specific antiviral for JC virus; the cornerstone of treatment in natalizumab-associated PML is restoring immune surveillance by stopping the drug and accelerating its clearance with plasma exchange (PLEX). Continuing natalizumab with steroids worsens the underlying immunosuppression driving viral replication. Acyclovir is ineffective against JC virus. High-dose steroids alone do not treat PML and could deepen immunosuppression, though they are used to manage subsequent IRIS. Removing natalizumab via PLEX allows lymphocytes to re-enter the CNS and control the infection, the best available strategy in this setting.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After plasma exchange the patient transiently worsens with new contrast enhancement on MRI. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Take no action, as this is expected uncomplicated recovery
Recognize immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) and treat with corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Restoring immune function predictably triggers immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in PML, with paradoxical clinical worsening and new enhancement or edema on MRI as inflammatory cells attack infected tissue; this is managed with corticosteroids to control the harmful inflammation. Restarting natalizumab would again suppress the immune response needed to clear JC virus. Acyclovir has no activity against JC virus. Treating worsening as benign risks dangerous cerebral edema. Anticipating and treating IRIS with steroids is essential after immune reconstitution in PML.

cases.diagnosis: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (JC virus) associated with natalizumab
pearls: PML risk on natalizumab rises with JCV seropositivity, more than 24 months of therapy, and prior immunosuppression.
Suspect PML with subacute, progressive, multifocal deficits and non-enhancing subcortical white-matter lesions.
Confirm with CSF JC virus PCR; brain biopsy is definitive if PCR is negative.
Treat by stopping natalizumab and doing plasma exchange, then anticipate and treat IRIS with corticosteroids.

cases.title: Jaw stiffness and painful full-body spasms
cases.chiefComplaint: I can't open my jaw, and my whole body keeps cramping up.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents five days after stepping on a rusty nail while gardening, which caused a deep puncture wound to the sole of his right foot that he cleaned only superficially and never sought care for. Over the past two days he developed jaw stiffness and difficulty opening his mouth, followed by neck and back stiffness, difficulty swallowing, and now intermittent painful spasms of his trunk and limbs that are triggered by noise or touch. He remains fully awake during these episodes. He has not had a tetanus booster in over 30 years. PMH: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: lives in a rural area, gardens frequently, current smoker, occasional alcohol. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient has marked trismus (lockjaw) and a fixed grimace (risus sardonicus). There is nuchal and truncal rigidity with episodes of opisthotonus and board-like abdominal wall rigidity, and reflex generalized spasms are provoked by minimal stimulation. He is diaphoretic and tachycardic with labile blood pressure. Critically, he is fully alert and aware throughout the spasms. A healing puncture wound is present on the right sole. There is no meningismus from infection, no focal weakness, and cranial nerves are otherwise intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step?
options: Secure the airway, admit to the ICU, and minimize sensory stimulation in a quiet, darkened environment
Perform a lumbar puncture to confirm the diagnosis
Discharge with an oral muscle relaxant
Obtain an MRI of the brain first
stages.explanation: Generalized tetanus is a clinical diagnosis, and the immediate priorities are airway protection and minimizing stimulation, because reflex spasms and laryngospasm can be triggered by light, noise, and touch and can be rapidly fatal. The patient should be admitted to a quiet, darkened ICU with airway equipment ready and early intubation if spasms threaten ventilation. LP is unnecessary and risky; the CSF is normal in tetanus and the diagnosis is clinical. Discharge with an oral muscle relaxant is dangerous given progressive spasms and autonomic instability. MRI does not diagnose tetanus, and transporting the patient risks provoking spasms. Securing the airway and controlling the environment come first.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonus, and stimulus-induced generalized spasms with preserved consciousness, occurring days after a contaminated puncture wound in an under-vaccinated patient, are diagnostic of generalized tetanus caused by Clostridium tetani toxin (tetanospasmin). Strychnine poisoning closely mimics tetanus but is excluded by a negative toxicology screen and lacks trismus as a hallmark. An acute dystonic reaction follows dopamine-blocking drugs and responds rapidly to anticholinergics or diphenhydramine. Meningitis causes fever and meningismus with abnormal CSF, not trismus with normal CSF. Crucially, tetanus patients remain fully awake and aware during painful spasms, a key clinical clue. No laboratory test confirms it; diagnosis rests on the clinical picture.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CSF: normal cell count, protein, and glucose
Serum CK: 1500 U/L
Urine/serum toxicology (including strychnine): negative
Wound culture: pending (often negative)
ECG: sinus tachycardia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy neutralizes circulating, unbound toxin?
stages.explanation: Human tetanus immune globulin (TIG) neutralizes circulating, unbound tetanospasmin before it enters nerve terminals and is a central, time-sensitive treatment; toxin already bound to neurons cannot be neutralized, so early administration limits progression. Management also includes wound debridement for source control and metronidazole, the preferred antibiotic, to eradicate C. tetani. Tetanus toxoid vaccine is given, since the disease does not confer immunity, but it works too slowly to treat acute illness and instead provides active immunization for the future. Corticosteroids and IVIG are not specific treatments for tetanus. TIG plus debridement and metronidazole address the toxin and its source.

stages.label: SPASM CONTROL
stages.question: What is the first-line therapy for the muscle spasms and rigidity?
options: Benzodiazepines (diazepam or midazolam) for spasm control and sedation
Withholding sedation to monitor mental status
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines such as diazepam or midazolam are first-line for the muscle spasms and rigidity of tetanus; they enhance GABAergic inhibition, providing both muscle relaxation and sedation. Severe, refractory spasms that compromise ventilation require neuromuscular blockade and mechanical ventilation. Oral baclofen alone is inadequate for the severe generalized spasms seen here. Antipsychotics can lower the seizure threshold and do not address the pathophysiology. Withholding sedation leaves the patient with agonizing, life-threatening spasms. High-dose benzodiazepines, sometimes supplemented with intrathecal baclofen or propofol in refractory cases, are the mainstay of spasm control alongside a quiet environment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: How should the patient's labile hypertension and tachycardia be managed?
options: Ignore them, as they resolve on their own
Manage autonomic instability with magnesium sulfate and short-acting agents such as labetalol
stages.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction, with labile hypertension, tachycardia, arrhythmias, and diaphoresis, is a major cause of death in severe tetanus and reflects unopposed sympathetic overactivity. Magnesium sulfate is widely used to help control spasms and autonomic instability, and labetalol with its combined alpha and beta blockade helps manage cardiovascular swings; short-acting agents are preferred given the lability. Ignoring autonomic storm risks fatal arrhythmia or cardiovascular collapse. Routine atropine is inappropriate and could worsen tachycardia. Prophylactic vasopressors are not indicated and could exacerbate hypertension. Careful, titratable control of the autonomic instability, often with magnesium and short-acting antihypertensives, is essential in the ICU.

cases.diagnosis: Generalized tetanus
pearls: Tetanus is a clinical diagnosis: trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonus, and spasms with preserved consciousness.
Treat with human tetanus immune globulin, metronidazole, and wound debridement; also give toxoid since infection confers no immunity.
Benzodiazepines are first-line for spasms; refractory cases need neuromuscular blockade and ventilation.
Autonomic instability is a leading cause of death; control it with magnesium and short-acting agents like labetalol.

cases.title: Months of worsening headaches and a weakening leg
cases.chiefComplaint: My headaches have been getting worse for two months, and now my left leg drags.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with two months of progressively worsening headaches that are worst in the early morning and sometimes associated with nausea. Over the past few weeks his family has noticed subtle personality changes and mild memory lapses, and he has developed dragging of his left leg. Yesterday he had a brief focal seizure of the left leg. He denies fever, weight loss, or known malignancy. PMH: hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. Social history: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), drinks alcohol socially, retired accountant. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The patient is alert with mild cognitive slowing and reduced attention. There is left lower-extremity weakness (4/5) with a left pronator drift, hyperreflexia on the left, and an upgoing left plantar response. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There is no fever and no meningismus. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact and sensation is preserved. Gait shows left leg circumduction. The general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Treat as migraine and arrange routine follow-up
stages.explanation: Progressive morning headaches with nausea, a new focal deficit, a seizure, and papilledema indicate raised intracranial pressure from a likely intracranial mass, and contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain is the appropriate next step. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with papilledema and suspected mass effect because of herniation risk and would not establish the diagnosis. Treating it as migraine ignores focal signs and papilledema, which are clear red flags. Plain skull radiographs provide no useful information about brain parenchyma. MRI characterizes the lesion's location, enhancement, and relationship to eloquent structures, guiding biopsy or resection.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: large heterogeneously ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis crossing the corpus callosum in a 'butterfly' configuration, extending into both frontal lobes
Surrounding vasogenic edema with mass effect and mild midline shift
DWI: no central restricted diffusion (favoring tumor over abscess)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A large, heterogeneously ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis and surrounding vasogenic edema that crosses the corpus callosum in a butterfly configuration in an older adult is classic for glioblastoma. A brain abscess also ring-enhances but shows central restricted diffusion on DWI and an infectious prodrome, both absent here. Primary CNS lymphoma is the main alternative for a butterfly lesion and can look similar, but it typically shows homogeneous rather than ring enhancement with restricted diffusion and a dramatic, transient response to steroids; it is more common in immunosuppressed patients. Metastases are usually multiple, at the gray-white junction, with a known primary. Tissue diagnosis confirms glioblastoma, but the imaging is highly suggestive.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best next step for the symptomatic peritumoral edema?
stages.explanation: Symptomatic peritumoral vasogenic edema causing headache and focal deficits is treated with corticosteroids, typically dexamethasone, which reduce edema and improve symptoms relatively quickly. A mannitol bolus is for acute herniation, and discharging this patient is unsafe. Therapeutic LP is contraindicated with a mass and raised intracranial pressure. Empiric antibiotics target infection, which the imaging and course argue against. Note that if primary CNS lymphoma is still being considered, steroids can confound biopsy by shrinking lymphoma tissue, so neurosurgery is often consulted about timing; but for a presentation classic for glioblastoma with symptomatic edema, dexamethasone is the appropriate next step for symptom control.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the standard definitive management?
options: Maximal safe surgical resection with histopathology, followed by radiotherapy plus temozolomide
stages.explanation: The standard of care for glioblastoma is maximal safe surgical resection to obtain a histopathologic and molecular diagnosis and to debulk the tumor, followed by the Stupp protocol of concurrent radiotherapy with temozolomide and then adjuvant temozolomide. Whole-brain radiation alone is not standard for a single primary glioma and omits surgical diagnosis and chemotherapy. Antibiotics treat infection, not tumor. Observation is inappropriate for an aggressive, rapidly fatal malignancy. Molecular markers such as MGMT promoter methylation predict temozolomide responsiveness and prognosis, and IDH status defines the entity. Even with optimal therapy, glioblastoma carries a poor prognosis with median survival around 15 months, so resection plus chemoradiation is the established approach.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4)
pearls: A butterfly lesion crossing the corpus callosum suggests glioblastoma, with primary CNS lymphoma the main mimic.
Avoid lumbar puncture with a mass and raised intracranial pressure; obtain MRI with contrast.
Ring enhancement with central necrosis and no central restricted diffusion favors tumor over abscess.
Standard of care is maximal safe resection plus radiotherapy and temozolomide (Stupp protocol); MGMT methylation predicts response.

cases.title: Months of dull morning headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a dull headache almost every morning for a few months.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with 4 months of dull, bilateral frontal headaches. They are mild to moderate, often present on waking, and ease over the morning; they are not throbbing and are not associated with nausea, photophobia, or aura. She denies visual changes, limb weakness, numbness, seizures, or personality change. Over-the-counter acetaminophen gives partial relief. She has no prior headache history, and her primary doctor ordered imaging given the new persistent pattern. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily; she takes no anticoagulants and has no drug allergies. She is a recently retired schoolteacher, a never-smoker, drinks one glass of wine weekly, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for breast cancer in her mother at age 70 and hypertension in her father. She reports normal energy, sleep, and mood, with no recent weight loss, fevers, or night sweats. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, alert, and fully oriented. No scalp tenderness or cranial bruits. Visual acuity and confrontation fields are normal; fundoscopy shows sharp disc margins without papilledema. Extraocular movements are full and pupils are equal and reactive. Facial sensation and strength are symmetric and speech is fluent. Motor exam shows 5/5 strength in all four limbs with normal tone. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with downgoing plantar responses. Sensation to light touch, pinprick, and vibration is intact. Coordination, finger-to-nose, and gait including tandem are normal. There are no meningeal signs, and cognitive screening is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A new, persistent headache pattern beginning after age 50 with morning predominance prompts what most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and start amitriptyline for tension-type headache
Order MRI brain with and without contrast
Obtain non-contrast CT head and discharge
Perform lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
stages.explanation: A new, persistent headache beginning after age 50, present on waking, warrants neuroimaging to exclude a structural lesion. MRI brain with and without gadolinium is the most sensitive study for parenchymal and extra-axial masses and is the correct choice. Reassurance with amitriptyline assumes benign tension headache and ignores the red flags of new onset over 50 and morning timing. Non-contrast CT misses many tumors, especially small extra-axial or posterior fossa lesions, and contrast adds diagnostic value here. Lumbar puncture is premature and potentially hazardous before imaging excludes a mass, given herniation risk. Imaging first, tailored to a tumor-sensitive modality, is standard of care for red-flag headaches.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
- 2.2 cm extra-axial, dural-based mass over the right frontal convexity
- Homogeneous, avid contrast enhancement with a 'dural tail' sign
- Broad dural attachment with well-circumscribed margins
- No surrounding vasogenic edema, mass effect, or midline shift
No restricted diffusion. No hydrocephalus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the imaging appearance?
stages.explanation: An extra-axial, dural-based mass with homogeneous avid enhancement and a 'dural tail' is the classic appearance of a meningioma, the most common benign primary intracranial tumor, especially in middle-aged women. Glioblastoma is intra-axial and infiltrative, typically with irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, and marked edema. Brain metastases are usually intra-axial at the gray-white junction, often multiple, and accompanied by prominent vasogenic edema; a solitary dural-based lesion would be atypical. Dural-based lymphoma can mimic meningioma but is rare, often shows restricted diffusion, and would be unusual in an otherwise healthy patient. The broad dural attachment, sharp margins, and dural tail strongly favor meningioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this small, asymptomatic lesion without edema, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate craniotomy with gross total resection
stages.explanation: This is a small (2.2 cm), asymptomatic convexity meningioma without edema or mass effect, discovered incidentally. The appropriate management is observation with serial MRI (for example at 6-12 months, then annually), since many such tumors grow slowly or not at all and intervention carries surgical risk. Immediate craniotomy is overtreatment for an asymptomatic, stable-appearing lesion. Stereotactic radiosurgery is reserved for growing, surgically inaccessible, or residual/recurrent tumors, not first-line for a small incidental one. Corticosteroids and chemotherapy are inappropriate; there is no edema to treat and meningiomas are not chemosensitive. Surveillance balances the low growth risk against procedural morbidity and is the guideline-concordant choice.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which finding on follow-up would most strongly favor proceeding to surgical resection?
options: New seizures, documented growth, or symptomatic mass effect/edema
stages.explanation: Intervention is triggered by clinical or radiographic progression. Documented tumor growth on serial imaging, new neurologic symptoms, seizures, or development of symptomatic mass effect or peritumoral edema would justify resection (or radiosurgery in select cases). Stable size at 12 months reassures and supports continued observation rather than surgery. A dural tail is a typical imaging feature of meningioma and, by itself, says nothing about behavior or the need to operate. Patient anxiety alone is not a surgical indication; it is addressed with counseling and a clear surveillance plan. The decision to treat hinges on demonstrated growth or symptom development, which is why structured follow-up imaging is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Asymptomatic right frontal convexity meningioma (presumed WHO grade I) found incidentally on headache imaging.
pearls: Meningiomas are extra-axial, dural-based tumors; the enhancing 'dural tail' is characteristic but not pathognomonic.
Small, asymptomatic meningiomas without edema can be safely observed with serial MRI.
Surgery is indicated for symptomatic mass effect, documented growth, or new seizures.
Most meningiomas are WHO grade I, are more common in women, and are frequently incidental.

cases.title: First-ever seizure in an older smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: He had a shaking spell and bit his tongue at home.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man is admitted after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home, his first ever, lasting about two minutes with tongue-biting and brief postictal confusion. For six weeks he has had dull, progressively worse morning headaches and intermittent right-hand clumsiness. He reports a 6 kg unintentional weight loss, reduced appetite, and a chronic cough productive of occasional blood-streaked sputum. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Medications are a tiotropium inhaler, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and as-needed albuterol; he takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and is a retired machinist with prior asbestos exposure. Family history is noncontributory. He denies fever, neck stiffness, recent head trauma, or prior seizures. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and exertional dyspnea but negative for focal weakness at presentation beyond the hand clumsiness.
cases.exam: The patient is drowsy but rousable and oriented after the postictal period. Pupils are equal and reactive with no gaze deviation. There is subtle right pronator drift and mildly reduced fine finger movements on the right; strength is otherwise 5/5. Deep tendon reflexes are slightly brisker on the right with an equivocal right plantar response. Sensation is intact, and speech is fluent without aphasia. There are no meningeal signs. Cardiac exam is normal. Pulmonary exam reveals decreased breath sounds and scattered wheezes at the right apex. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatomegaly, and the skin shows no suspicious lesions. Fundoscopy reveals early blurring of the disc margins bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial study for this first seizure with focal features?
options: MRI brain with and without contrast
stages.explanation: A first seizure with focal features (right-hand clumsiness, pronator drift) and systemic red flags mandates neuroimaging, and MRI brain with and without contrast is the most sensitive test to detect tumors, metastases, and small cortical lesions. Non-contrast CT is a reasonable rapid screen acutely but misses small or multiple enhancing lesions and lacks contrast characterization, so it is insufficient as the definitive study. EEG helps classify seizures but does not reveal structural causes, and empiric phenytoin without imaging delays diagnosis. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when a mass lesion is suspected because of herniation risk, and the picture here points to structural pathology, not meningitis. Contrast MRI is the appropriate next step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head (initial): multiple hyperdense foci with surrounding low attenuation
MRI brain with contrast:
- At least 4 rim-enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction (frontal, parietal, cerebellar)
- Extensive surrounding vasogenic edema with local mass effect
- No midline shift; no hydrocephalus
CBC: Hb 11.8 g/dL, WBC 8.1 x10^3/uL, Plt 240 x10^3/uL
Na 137 mEq/L, Ca 10.1 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: 4 cm right upper-lobe mass with hilar adenopathy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the imaging and clinical picture?
stages.explanation: Multiple rim-enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction with prominent vasogenic edema, in an older smoker with weight loss, hemoptysis, and a lung mass, are characteristic of brain metastases, the most common intracranial tumor in adults. The gray-white junction is favored because tumor emboli lodge where vessel caliber narrows. Primary CNS lymphoma is usually a solitary, homogeneously enhancing periventricular lesion and is more common in immunocompromised patients. Brain abscesses produce ring enhancement but typically present with fever and an infectious source, with central restricted diffusion as the clue, and this patient is afebrile. Glioblastoma is usually a single infiltrative lesion crossing white matter tracts, not multiple discrete junctional nodules. The lung mass identifies the likely primary.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to identify and confirm the primary tumor?
options: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and biopsy of the lung mass
Whole-body PET only, without tissue sampling
stages.explanation: The chest radiograph already reveals a right upper-lobe mass with hilar adenopathy, and in a heavy smoker this is the probable primary. Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis plus tissue biopsy of the most accessible lesion (commonly the lung mass or an accessible node) establishes histology and guides therapy. Colonoscopy is not indicated without GI symptoms or bleeding and ignores the obvious lung finding. Prostate evaluation is low-yield here and does not explain the lung mass or hemoptysis. PET imaging aids staging but cannot replace histologic confirmation; treatment with targeted therapy, immunotherapy, or chemoradiation depends on tissue type and molecular markers. Pursuing the lung lesion for both staging and biopsy is the highest-yield, guideline-based step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management on the ward?
options: Start phenytoin and discharge with neurology follow-up
IV dexamethasone for vasogenic edema and levetiracetam for seizures
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to lower intracranial pressure
stages.explanation: Symptomatic vasogenic edema from metastases is treated with corticosteroids; dexamethasone is preferred for its potency and low mineralocorticoid effect, reducing edema and mass effect. After a structural seizure, an antiseizure medication such as levetiracetam is started for its tolerability and few interactions. Mannitol and emergency craniotomy are reserved for impending herniation, not stable multifocal disease, and surgery is not first-line for multiple metastases. Phenytoin can control seizures but has more interactions, and discharging a drowsy patient with new multifocal brain lesions and a lung mass is unsafe. Lumbar puncture is dangerous with mass lesions and edema due to herniation risk and does not treat the underlying problem. Dexamethasone plus levetiracetam stabilizes the patient pending oncologic staging.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple brain metastases, most likely from a primary non-small cell lung cancer.
pearls: Metastases favor the gray-white junction and are usually multiple with disproportionate vasogenic edema.
Lung, breast, melanoma, renal, and colorectal cancers are the most common sources of brain metastases.
Dexamethasone is first-line for symptomatic peritumoral edema; reserve mannitol/surgery for herniation.
A first focal seizure in an adult over 40 requires contrast MRI to exclude a mass.

cases.title: One-sided hearing loss and ringing
cases.chiefComplaint: My right ear has been getting worse and rings all the time.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with 18 months of gradually worsening hearing in her right ear. She first noticed difficulty understanding callers when holding the phone to that ear and trouble following conversations in noisy restaurants. A constant, high-pitched ringing in the right ear accompanies the hearing loss. Over recent months she has felt mildly off-balance, especially when turning quickly, but denies discrete spinning vertigo, drop attacks, or aural fullness. She reports subtle numbness over the right cheek in the past few weeks. There is no facial weakness, otorrhea, headache, or prior ear surgery, and no excessive noise exposure. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes only a daily multivitamin and has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is negative for early hearing loss, neurofibromatosis, or brain tumors. She works as an accountant and reports the hearing problem is affecting her job. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no tinnitus or hearing change in the left ear.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and comfortable. Otoscopy shows normal external canals and intact, mobile tympanic membranes bilaterally. Weber test lateralizes to the left (better) ear, and Rinne shows air conduction greater than bone conduction in both ears, a sensorineural pattern on the right. Whispered-voice testing is reduced on the right. Facial sensation is mildly diminished to light touch over the right cheek, and the right corneal reflex is slightly decreased. Facial nerve motor function is symmetric with no weakness. Extraocular movements are full without nystagmus at rest. Finger-to-nose is mildly dysmetric on the right, and gait is steady but tandem is slightly unsteady. The remainder of the cranial nerve and motor exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first test for asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus?
options: Non-contrast CT of the temporal bone
stages.explanation: Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss and unilateral tinnitus require objective characterization, and pure-tone plus speech (word recognition) audiometry is the first test. It confirms the laterality and pattern of loss and quantifies word recognition, which is disproportionately reduced in retrocochlear lesions. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear/conductive function and would be normal here, missing the sensorineural process. Empiric meclizine treats vertigo symptomatically and reassurance ignores a potentially serious retrocochlear cause; asymmetric loss must be investigated. Non-contrast CT of the temporal bone is good for bony anatomy but poorly visualizes small cerebellopontine-angle and intracanalicular soft-tissue tumors. Audiometry guides the decision to obtain the definitive study, gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals, when retrocochlear pathology is suspected.

stages.label: AUDIOMETRY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Audiometry:
- Right ear: moderate-to-severe high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss
- Right word recognition score: 48% (poor)
