- Left ear: normal thresholds, word recognition 100%
MRI internal auditory canals with gadolinium:
- 1.8 cm enhancing mass at the right cerebellopontine angle extending into the internal auditory canal ('ice-cream-cone' shape)
- Mild effacement of adjacent cisternal CSF; brainstem not compressed
- No lesion on the left

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the findings?
stages.explanation: An enhancing mass centered on the internal auditory canal that extends into the cerebellopontine angle in an 'ice-cream-cone' configuration, with asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss and poor word recognition, is the classic picture of a vestibular schwannoma arising from the vestibular division of cranial nerve VIII. CPA meningiomas enhance and may show a dural tail, but they are typically based on the petrous dura, eccentric to the canal, and rarely extend deep into it. Glomus jugulare tumors arise at the jugular foramen, are highly vascular, and cause pulsatile tinnitus and lower cranial nerve palsies. Epidermoid cysts follow CSF signal, do not enhance, and show restricted diffusion. The intracanalicular epicenter and enhancement make schwannoma the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a symptomatic 1.8 cm tumor with progressive deficits, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Continued observation with annual MRI only
Treatment with microsurgical resection or stereotactic radiosurgery
stages.explanation: This is a symptomatic 1.8 cm vestibular schwannoma causing progressive hearing loss, imbalance, and early trigeminal involvement (facial numbness, reduced corneal reflex). For a tumor of this size with neurologic symptoms in a 50-year-old, active treatment, either microsurgical resection or stereotactic radiosurgery, is indicated, with choice guided by size, hearing status, and patient preference. Observation with serial MRI is appropriate for small, asymptomatic, or incidental tumors, or in elderly/comorbid patients, but ongoing symptoms and trigeminal signs argue against watchful waiting here. Cochlear implantation does not treat the tumor and is not the issue, since the nerve pathology is retrocochlear. Schwannomas are benign and not chemosensitive, so systemic chemotherapy has no role. Facial nerve preservation is the key procedural goal.

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: Finding bilateral vestibular schwannomas would most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are the hallmark of neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the NF2 (merlin) tumor-suppressor gene on chromosome 22. Patients also develop other schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas, typically presenting at a younger age. Recognizing bilateral tumors should prompt genetic counseling and screening of the neuraxis and family members. Paget disease causes bony hearing loss through skull involvement but not schwannomas. MEN1 involves parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors, not vestibular schwannomas. Von Hippel-Lindau causes hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytomas, not acoustic neuromas. This patient has a unilateral tumor and no family history, so a sporadic schwannoma is most likely, but the bilateral-equals-NF2 association is essential to know.

cases.diagnosis: Right vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) arising from cranial nerve VIII at the cerebellopontine angle.
pearls: Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss or unilateral tinnitus warrants audiometry and gadolinium MRI of the internal auditory canals.
Vestibular schwannomas show poor word recognition out of proportion to pure-tone loss (a retrocochlear pattern).
Bilateral vestibular schwannomas define neurofibromatosis type 2.
Management options (observation, stereotactic radiosurgery, microsurgery) balance tumor size, hearing, and facial nerve preservation.

cases.title: Bumping into things on the sides
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep clipping doorframes and missing cars in my side vision.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents because he keeps clipping doorframes and nearly missed merging cars on both sides while driving. Over roughly eight months his peripheral vision has narrowed, and he now turns his head to compensate. He also reports a dull bifrontal headache, declining libido, erectile dysfunction, low energy, and reduced shaving frequency. He denies galactorrhea, cold intolerance, polyuria, or visual flashes. There is no history of head trauma. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He is married, works as an architect, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke or use drugs. Family history includes type 2 diabetes in his mother and no known endocrine tumors or MEN syndromes. He has gained a few kilograms and feels generally run-down but denies headache-associated nausea, fever, or neck stiffness. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no acral enlargement, skin changes, or palpitations to suggest an alternative hormonal syndrome.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Visual acuity is 20/20 in each eye centrally. Confrontation testing reveals a bitemporal visual field defect, with loss of the temporal half of vision in both eyes, confirmed on bedside red-target testing. Pupils are equal and reactive without a relative afferent defect; extraocular movements are full and conjugate. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. Motor strength, tone, reflexes, sensation, coordination, and gait are all normal. There is no thyromegaly and no galactorrhea on examination, and secondary sexual characteristics show mildly reduced body hair. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: A bitemporal hemianopia localizes the lesion to which structure?
stages.explanation: A bitemporal hemianopia, loss of both temporal visual fields, localizes to the optic chiasm, where the crossing nasal retinal fibers (which carry temporal field information) are compressed. The most common cause is a sellar mass pushing up on the chiasm. A single optic nerve lesion causes monocular vision loss in that eye, not a symmetric bitemporal pattern. An optic tract lesion produces a contralateral homonymous hemianopia (same-sided field loss in both eyes), not bitemporal loss. Bilateral occipital lesions cause cortical visual loss or homonymous defects, often with macular sparing, not a clean bitemporal pattern. Recognizing the chiasmal pattern directs imaging to the sella; MRI of the pituitary with contrast is the next step to characterize the lesion.

stages.label: MRI & PITUITARY PANEL
stages.content:
MRI sella with and without contrast:
- 1.9 cm sellar mass (macroadenoma) with suprasellar extension
- Compression and upward displacement of the optic chiasm
- No cavernous sinus invasion; no hemorrhage
Pituitary panel:
- Prolactin 2,450 ng/mL (normal <20)
- Total testosterone 120 ng/dL (low)
- TSH 1.8 uIU/mL (normal), free T4 normal
- 8 a.m. cortisol 14 ug/dL (normal), IGF-1 normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What does the markedly elevated prolactin with this sellar mass indicate?
options: Stalk effect from a nonfunctioning adenoma
stages.explanation: A prolactin of 2,450 ng/mL is far above the level explained by stalk compression and indicates a prolactin-secreting macroadenoma (prolactinoma). Prolactin generally correlates with tumor size, and values in the thousands essentially confirm a lactotroph tumor. The low testosterone reflects prolactin-induced suppression of GnRH, explaining his low libido and erectile dysfunction. Stalk effect, where a nonfunctioning adenoma disinhibits prolactin, typically raises prolactin only modestly (usually under 150-200 ng/mL), not to thousands. Acromegaly would show an elevated IGF-1 and acral/soft-tissue changes, which are absent. Cushing disease would show hypercortisolism with typical features; the morning cortisol and exam are normal. The markedly elevated prolactin with a sellar macroadenoma makes prolactinoma the diagnosis and dictates medical therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment, even with chiasmal compression?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year
stages.explanation: Prolactinomas, even large ones causing visual field loss, are treated medically first with a dopamine agonist such as cabergoline. Dopamine agonists lower prolactin, shrink the tumor, and frequently reverse chiasmal compression and visual deficits, making them first-line over surgery. Transsphenoidal surgery is reserved for prolactinomas that fail or cannot tolerate medical therapy, or for acute apoplexy with severe progressive deficit; it is not first-line, which is unusual among pituitary tumors. Radiosurgery is a later option for resistant tumors, with slow effect and risk of hypopituitarism. Observation is inappropriate when there is chiasmal compression and visual loss requiring treatment. Visual fields and prolactin are monitored on therapy, and testosterone often recovers as prolactin normalizes. This medical-first approach is a key teaching point.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He returns weeks later with sudden severe headache, worsening vision, and a new oculomotor palsy. Which complication is most likely?
stages.explanation: Sudden severe ('thunderclap') headache with acute visual deterioration and an ophthalmoplegia (often cranial nerve III) in a patient with a known pituitary macroadenoma is classic for pituitary apoplexy, hemorrhage or infarction within the tumor. It is a neurosurgical and endocrine emergency requiring urgent MRI, stress-dose corticosteroids for likely acute adrenal insufficiency, and possible surgical decompression. Meningitis causes fever and meningismus, not a sudden focal ophthalmoplegia tied to a tumor. Migraine with aura does not produce a fixed third-nerve palsy or progressive visual loss and would be a dangerous assumption. Cavernous sinus thrombosis causes painful ophthalmoplegia with proptosis and chemosis, usually with infection or a hypercoagulable trigger. Recognizing apoplexy and giving glucocorticoids promptly is lifesaving.

cases.diagnosis: Pituitary macroadenoma (prolactinoma) causing bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression.
pearls: Bitemporal hemianopia localizes to the optic chiasm; image the sella.
Prolactin level roughly tracks tumor size; values in the thousands indicate a true prolactinoma, not stalk effect.
Prolactinomas are treated first-line with dopamine agonists (cabergoline), even with visual loss.
Sudden headache, vision loss, and ophthalmoplegia in a pituitary tumor signal apoplexy; give stress-dose steroids.

cases.title: Severe low back pain and can't urinate
cases.chiefComplaint: My back's killing me and I haven't been able to urinate since this morning.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents to the emergency department with two days of severe low back pain that began after lifting heavy boxes, now radiating down both legs. Since this morning he has been unable to urinate despite a strong urge and reports a sense of incomplete emptying. He has noticed numbness in his groin, buttocks, and inner thighs, and on the way to the ED had an episode of fecal soiling he could not feel. The leg pain is accompanied by progressive weakness and a pins-and-needles sensation in both feet. He has a history of intermittent mechanical low back pain but nothing this severe. Medications are ibuprofen as needed; he takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. He works in warehouse logistics, smokes half a pack daily, and drinks alcohol on weekends. He denies fever, chills, recent infection, intravenous drug use, weight loss, or a cancer history. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for the urinary and bowel changes noted above and negative for chest or abdominal pain.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and shifting in pain. There is midline lower-lumbar tenderness without overlying erythema. Straight-leg raise reproduces pain bilaterally. Motor exam shows 4-/5 weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion and knee flexion bilaterally; hip flexion is 4+/5. Sensation to pinprick and light touch is reduced over the perineum, perianal area, and posterior thighs in a saddle distribution. Ankle reflexes are absent bilaterally, and knee reflexes are diminished, with downgoing plantar responses. Digital rectal exam reveals reduced resting and squeeze tone with absent perianal sensation and an absent anal wink. The lower abdomen is distended with a palpable, dull suprapubic mass consistent with a full bladder. Peripheral pulses are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge with NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, and physical therapy
Lumbar spine radiographs with outpatient follow-up
Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine
CT abdomen/pelvis to evaluate the bladder mass
stages.explanation: Saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and decreased anal tone are red-flag features of cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency. Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the test of choice to identify and localize compression of the cauda equina, most often a large central disc herniation. Discharge with conservative therapy would miss a time-critical diagnosis and risk permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction. Plain radiographs do not visualize neural compression and outpatient follow-up is far too slow. The suprapubic mass is a distended bladder from retention, not a tumor; a bladder scan confirms this, but the priority is spinal MRI. When MRI is unavailable, CT myelography is an alternative. Rapid imaging enables urgent decompression.

stages.label: BLADDER SCAN & MRI
stages.content:
Bladder scan: post-void residual 720 mL
MRI lumbosacral spine:
- Large central L4-L5 disc herniation
- Severe canal stenosis with compression of the cauda equina nerve roots
- No epidural abscess, hematoma, or bony metastasis
Urinalysis: unremarkable
CBC and inflammatory markers within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical and imaging findings?
stages.explanation: Compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus by a large central disc, producing saddle anesthesia, areflexic (lower motor neuron) leg weakness, urinary retention with overflow, and loss of anal tone, defines cauda equina syndrome. Conus medullaris syndrome occurs at T12-L1, tends to cause earlier, symmetric bladder/bowel dysfunction with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs and possibly preserved leg strength; the L4-L5 level and pure LMN findings favor cauda equina. Transverse myelitis affects the cord itself, producing upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, upgoing toes) and a sensory level, not the areflexia seen here. Isolated bilateral L5 radiculopathy would not cause saddle anesthesia, sphincter dysfunction, or urinary retention. The combination of saddle sensory loss and retention is the key.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management?
options: Urgent surgical decompression (discectomy) and bladder catheterization
Epidural steroid injection in two weeks
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome from disc herniation is treated with urgent surgical decompression, typically discectomy/laminectomy, ideally as soon as possible (commonly cited within 24-48 hours) to maximize recovery of bladder, bowel, and motor function. Immediate bladder catheterization relieves retention and prevents detrusor injury. High-dose steroids are used for some causes of cord compression (e.g., neoplastic or inflammatory) but are not the treatment for compressive disc-related cauda equina and should not delay surgery. Physical therapy and analgesia address ordinary mechanical back pain, not an evolving neurologic emergency. Elective epidural steroid injection is for radicular pain without red flags and is far too slow here. Decompression timing is the principal modifiable determinant of neurologic outcome, so emergent referral is essential.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor most strongly determines this patient's neurologic outcome?
options: Whether decompression occurs before complete, established urinary retention
stages.explanation: Neurologic outcome in cauda equina syndrome correlates most strongly with the severity and duration of dysfunction before decompression, particularly whether surgery occurs before retention becomes complete and established (so-called CES-R). Incomplete cauda equina syndrome with preserved sensation and partial bladder function decompressed early has a far better prognosis than complete syndrome with painless retention. This is why rapid recognition and emergent surgery matter. Age has minor prognostic impact compared with timing and completeness of the deficit. The exact disc level does not drive recovery once compression is relieved. Smoking affects healing and disc disease risk but is not the key determinant of neurologic recovery here. Counsel the patient that early decompression offers the best chance to recover continence and strength.

cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from a large central L4-L5 disc herniation.
pearls: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention with overflow incontinence, and reduced anal tone are the cardinal features of cauda equina syndrome.
Emergent MRI and surgical decompression, ideally within 24-48 hours, are required to preserve neurologic function.
Cauda equina lesions cause lower motor neuron signs (areflexia, flaccid weakness); conus/cord lesions add upper motor neuron signs.
Always check a post-void residual; painless retention signals established, poorer-prognosis disease.

cases.title: Worsening back pain and heavy legs
cases.chiefComplaint: My back has hurt for weeks and now my legs feel weak.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with three weeks of progressive mid-back pain that is now constant, worse at night and when lying flat, and only partly relieved by acetaminophen. Over the past four days both legs have become weak and heavy, making stairs difficult, and today he noticed a tight, band-like sensation around his lower chest and new difficulty starting urination. He was diagnosed with prostate cancer two years ago and stopped follow-up; he reports a 7 kg weight loss and night sweats. Medications are acetaminophen and tamsulosin; he takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. He is a retired plumber, a former smoker (30 pack-years), and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes prostate cancer in his brother. He denies fever or recent trauma, and notes reduced sensation below the chest. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, back pain, and lower-limb weakness, and negative for bowel incontinence at presentation. He is anxious about the rapid change in his walking.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable lying flat. There is focal tenderness to percussion over the mid-thoracic spine. Lower-extremity strength is 4-/5 proximally and distally bilaterally, with increased tone. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk at the knees and ankles with bilateral upgoing plantar responses (Babinski). There is a sensory level to pinprick and temperature at approximately T8, with reduced sensation below it; vibration is also diminished in the toes. Upper-extremity exam is normal. Gait is unsteady and the patient cannot walk unassisted. Digital rectal exam shows a hard, nodular prostate with normal resting tone at presentation, and the bladder is mildly distended. No skin lesions or lymphadenopathy are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar spine X-rays and outpatient oncology referral
Bed rest, opioids, and reassess in 24 hours
Administer IV dexamethasone and obtain urgent MRI of the entire spine
CT abdomen/pelvis to restage the prostate cancer first
stages.explanation: Progressive back pain with bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level, upper motor neuron signs, and urinary symptoms in a man with known prostate cancer is metastatic spinal cord compression until proven otherwise, a neuro-oncologic emergency. When suspected, IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) are given immediately to reduce vasogenic edema and may preserve function, while urgent MRI of the entire spine localizes the compression and detects multilevel disease. Plain films and outpatient referral dangerously delay diagnosis and risk irreversible paraplegia. Bed rest and opioids ignore an evolving cord emergency. Restaging CT can follow, but it does not visualize the cord or guide acute decompression and must not delay steroids and MRI. Treat empirically and image the whole spine; the window to preserve ambulation is short.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI entire spine with and without contrast:
- Enhancing epidural soft-tissue mass at T8 compressing the spinal cord
- Diffuse marrow-replacing (sclerotic) vertebral lesions at multiple levels
- Cord signal change at T8 without frank transection
Labs:
- PSA 410 ng/mL (markedly elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase 240 U/L (high)
- Calcium 9.6 mg/dL, Hb 10.9 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the findings?
stages.explanation: An enhancing epidural mass at T8 with cord signal change, multilevel sclerotic vertebral metastases, and a markedly elevated PSA in a man with prostate cancer establishes metastatic epidural spinal cord compression. The upper motor neuron pattern and thoracic sensory level fit a cord (not root) process. A spinal epidural abscess is plausible with back pain and myelopathy, but the patient is afebrile, inflammatory markers are not elevated, and imaging shows tumor with bony metastases rather than a rim-enhancing fluid collection. Disc herniation does not produce diffuse marrow lesions, systemic weight loss, or a sky-high PSA, and rarely causes thoracic cord compression. Guillain-Barre causes ascending areflexic weakness without a sensory level or a compressive mass. The malignant epidural mass is diagnostic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond dexamethasone, what is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Urgent neurosurgery and radiation oncology consultation for decompression and/or radiotherapy
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed abscess
stages.explanation: Beyond immediate dexamethasone, definitive treatment of malignant cord compression requires urgent multidisciplinary input. Neurosurgical decompression, often followed by radiotherapy, is preferred when there is good performance status, a single compressive level, spinal instability, or unknown histology, and improves ambulation versus radiation alone; otherwise urgent radiotherapy is used, especially for radiosensitive tumors. Prostate cancer is also hormone-responsive, so androgen-deprivation therapy is started. Steroids alone do not treat the tumor and the patient would progress. Antibiotics target abscess, which the imaging and clinical picture exclude. Plasmapheresis treats immune neuropathies like Guillain-Barre, not compressive metastatic disease. Coordinating spine surgery and radiation oncology emergently, while continuing steroids, gives the best chance of preserving or recovering ambulation.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor most strongly predicts whether this patient will remain able to walk?
options: Ambulatory status at the time treatment begins
stages.explanation: The single strongest predictor of post-treatment ambulation in metastatic spinal cord compression is neurologic/ambulatory function at the time treatment starts: patients still walking when therapy begins are far more likely to remain ambulatory, whereas those who are paraplegic at presentation rarely regain walking. This is precisely why emergent recognition, steroids, imaging, and decompression matter. PSA reflects tumor burden and treatment response but does not predict neurologic recovery. Calcium matters for hypercalcemia management but is not the key neurologic prognostic factor here and is near-normal. The number of vertebral metastases informs overall prognosis and radiation planning but is secondary to functional status for predicting ambulation. Counsel that speed of intervention, before weakness progresses, is the main modifiable factor.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic epidural spinal cord compression at T8 from metastatic prostate carcinoma.
pearls: New or progressive back pain in a cancer patient, especially worse at night or recumbent, is cord compression until proven otherwise.
Give IV dexamethasone immediately on suspicion and obtain urgent MRI of the entire spine (lesions are often multilevel).
Ambulatory status at treatment onset is the best predictor of walking outcome; act before paraplegia develops.
Surgical decompression plus radiotherapy beats radiotherapy alone for selected patients with good performance status and instability.

cases.title: Numb feet and unsteady in the dark
cases.chiefComplaint: My feet are numb and I feel like I'll fall when the lights are off.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman presents with several months of progressive numbness and tingling in both feet that has spread up to her mid-calves and into her fingertips. She feels increasingly unsteady on her feet, especially in the dark or when washing her hair with eyes closed, and has nearly fallen twice. She reports fatigue, a sore, beefy tongue, and mild forgetfulness. She follows a strict vegetarian-leaning diet and has autoimmune hypothyroidism and vitiligo. Medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and over-the-counter omeprazole used daily for reflux for several years; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and works as a librarian. Family history includes pernicious anemia in her sister and autoimmune thyroid disease in her mother. She denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, back pain, recent infection, or alcohol misuse. There is no history of nitrous oxide use or bariatric surgery. Review of systems is positive for paresthesias, imbalance, glossitis, and fatigue, and negative for arm weakness, visual changes, or headache.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale with a smooth, erythematous tongue. Mental status is intact aside from mildly slowed recall. Cranial nerves are normal. Motor strength is 5/5 in the arms and 4+/5 in the legs distally with mildly increased tone. Strikingly, vibration and joint-position sense are markedly reduced at the toes and ankles, with a positive Romberg sign. Pinprick and light touch are mildly diminished distally. Ankle reflexes are absent, yet knee reflexes are brisk and plantar responses are extensor (Babinski) bilaterally, a mixed upper and lower motor neuron picture. Gait is wide-based and unsteady, worsening with eyes closed. Coordination is otherwise intact, and there is no spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: MRI brain to evaluate for multiple sclerosis
Nerve conduction studies as the definitive test
Serum folate, then empiric folic acid
Serum vitamin B12 with methylmalonic acid and homocysteine
stages.explanation: The combination of dorsal column dysfunction (lost vibration/proprioception, positive Romberg) and corticospinal signs (brisk knees, extensor plantars) with a peripheral neuropathy points to subacute combined degeneration, and the first step is to check serum vitamin B12 along with methylmalonic acid and homocysteine, which are more sensitive because both rise early in deficiency. MRI brain for MS does not fit a symmetric, length-dependent, posterior-column syndrome. Nerve conduction studies characterize neuropathy but will not reveal the metabolic cause. Giving folate without measuring or replacing B12 can correct the anemia while allowing neurologic damage to progress, a classic error. Her risk factors (vegetarian diet, chronic PPI use, autoimmune disease, family pernicious anemia) all favor B12 deficiency, making targeted B12 testing the right move.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum vitamin B12: 95 pg/mL (low; normal >300)
Methylmalonic acid: markedly elevated
Homocysteine: elevated
CBC: Hb 10.4 g/dL, MCV 109 fL (macrocytic)
Peripheral smear: hypersegmented neutrophils, macro-ovalocytes
Anti-intrinsic factor antibody: positive
MRI cervical/thoracic spine: T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal columns ('inverted V' sign), no compressive lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the findings?
options: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord
stages.explanation: Low B12 with elevated methylmalonic acid and homocysteine, macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils, and dorsal-column T2 signal produce the diagnosis of subacute combined degeneration, demyelination of the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts from B12 deficiency. The mixed UMN/LMN exam and inverted-V cord sign are characteristic. Multiple sclerosis causes asymmetric, relapsing CNS lesions disseminated in space and time, not this symmetric posterior-column picture with macrocytosis. Tabes dorsalis also affects dorsal columns but is due to neurosyphilis, presents with lancinating pains and Argyll Robertson pupils, and would show positive treponemal serologies. Diabetic polyneuropathy is a distal sensory neuropathy without corticospinal signs, dorsal-column MRI changes, or megaloblastic anemia. The positive intrinsic factor antibody further pinpoints the mechanism, pernicious anemia.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying cause of this patient's B12 deficiency?
stages.explanation: The positive anti-intrinsic factor antibody, together with autoimmune hypothyroidism, vitiligo, and a family history of pernicious anemia, identifies pernicious anemia, autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells and loss of intrinsic factor, as the cause of B12 malabsorption. Chronic PPI use and a low-animal-product diet are contributing factors. Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia but not the neurologic syndrome or the elevated methylmalonic acid, which is normal in pure folate deficiency. Hereditary spastic paraplegia is a progressive pure corticospinal disorder without sensory or megaloblastic findings or low B12. Lead poisoning causes a motor-predominant neuropathy with abdominal pain and basophilic stippling, not low B12 with dorsal-column signs. Recognizing pernicious anemia guides lifelong parenteral replacement and monitoring for associated autoimmune and gastric disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
stages.explanation: Treatment is prompt vitamin B12 (cobalamin) replacement, typically intramuscular initially given malabsorption from intrinsic factor deficiency, with the goal of halting and partially reversing neurologic injury; early treatment yields better recovery, though long-standing deficits may persist. Lifelong maintenance is required in pernicious anemia. Folic acid alone is contraindicated as sole therapy, as it can correct the anemia while masking and worsening the neurologic disease. Corticosteroids treat inflammatory or demyelinating conditions like MS, not a nutritional deficiency. Plasma exchange is used for antibody-mediated neurologic emergencies (e.g., Guillain-Barre, myasthenic crisis), not B12 deficiency. Monitor potassium during early repletion (risk of hypokalemia), recheck blood counts and B12, address the underlying cause, and counsel on the need for ongoing therapy. Neurologic improvement may take weeks to months.

cases.diagnosis: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord due to vitamin B12 deficiency from pernicious anemia.
pearls: Subacute combined degeneration affects dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts; expect lost vibration/proprioception with brisk reflexes and extensor plantars.
Check methylmalonic acid and homocysteine; both are elevated in B12 deficiency and more sensitive than the B12 level alone.
Never give folate alone in suspected B12 deficiency; it can precipitate or worsen neurologic damage.
Pernicious anemia, PPI/metformin use, vegan diet, and nitrous oxide are key causes of B12 deficiency.

cases.title: Burns I never felt and weakening hands
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep burning my hands without realizing it.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents concerned about repeated burns and cuts on his hands and forearms that he never felt happening; he has scalded his fingers on a stove and found cuts without recalling any pain. Over the past two to three years he has noticed difficulty feeling hot and cold over his shoulders and arms, while light touch seems normal. His hands have become weaker and look thinner, and he drops objects. He has a dull, aching neck and shoulder pain that worsens with coughing, sneezing, or straining. There is no leg weakness or bowel or bladder change initially. Past medical history is notable for scoliosis diagnosed in adolescence. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He works as a carpenter, does not smoke, and drinks socially. Family history is unremarkable. He denies trauma, neck surgery, fevers, or weight loss. Review of systems is positive for painless injuries, sensory loss over the upper trunk and arms, hand weakness, and cough-induced neck pain, and negative for visual symptoms or sphincter dysfunction.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing with thoracic scoliosis. Cranial nerves are intact. Strikingly, pinprick and temperature sensation are reduced in a cape distribution over both shoulders, upper arms, and the upper trunk, while light touch, vibration, and joint-position sense are preserved, a dissociated sensory loss. There are healed burn scars on the fingers. Intrinsic hand muscles show wasting with mild weakness and reduced biceps and triceps reflexes (lower motor neuron at the cervical level). Lower-extremity strength is normal, but reflexes are mildly brisk with equivocal plantar responses. Gait is normal. There is no saddle anesthesia, nystagmus, or lower cranial nerve deficit at rest.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Where does this dissociated, cape-distribution sensory loss localize?
options: Central cervical cord lesion at the anterior commissure
stages.explanation: Dissociated sensory loss, reduced pain and temperature with preserved light touch, vibration, and proprioception, in a cape distribution localizes to the central cervical cord. Spinothalamic (pain/temperature) fibers cross in the anterior (white) commissure, and a central, expanding lesion interrupts these decussating fibers over several segments while sparing the dorsal columns, which run posteriorly. Peripheral polyneuropathy is length-dependent (feet first) and affects multiple modalities, not a suspended cape pattern. Pure posterior-column dysfunction would impair vibration and proprioception, the opposite of what is seen. Cortical (parietal) lesions cause contralateral hemisensory loss with cortical sensory features, not a bilateral suspended pattern with hand wasting. The associated lower motor neuron findings in the hands (anterior horn involvement) further confirm an intramedullary central cervical process.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
options: MRI of the cervical spine and brain (craniocervical junction)
Non-contrast CT of the cervical spine
stages.explanation: The clinical picture demands MRI of the cervical spine, including the brain and craniocervical junction, which is the definitive test for an intramedullary fluid-filled cavity (syrinx) and for an associated Chiari I malformation. EMG and nerve conduction studies evaluate peripheral nerve and anterior horn function but cannot show a syrinx and would miss the structural cause. Non-contrast CT poorly demonstrates cord parenchyma and a syrinx, though it shows bone; MRI is far superior for soft tissue. Skin biopsy assesses small-fiber peripheral neuropathy, which does not explain a suspended cape-distribution loss with hand wasting and cough-exacerbated neck pain. Imaging the craniocervical junction is essential because syringomyelia is frequently associated with Chiari I, and identifying that drives surgical planning.

stages.label: MRI
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine and brain:
- Longitudinal fluid-filled cavity (syrinx) within the central cervical cord from C2 to C7
- Cord expansion at the level of the cavity
- Cerebellar tonsils herniating 8 mm below the foramen magnum (Chiari I malformation)
- No intramedullary enhancing tumor
No hydrocephalus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the findings?
options: Syringomyelia associated with Chiari I malformation
stages.explanation: A longitudinal central cervical cord cavity with cord expansion, plus cerebellar tonsillar herniation 8 mm below the foramen magnum, is syringomyelia associated with a Chiari I malformation, the classic pairing. The dissociated cape sensory loss, hand wasting, and cough-exacerbated neck pain all fit. Multiple sclerosis causes discrete enhancing demyelinating plaques and a relapsing course, not a single longitudinal syrinx with tonsillar descent. ALS produces mixed upper and lower motor neuron degeneration without sensory loss; sensation is spared in ALS, unlike here. An intramedullary tumor can cause a syrinx but typically enhances and shows a nodular mass; this scan shows no enhancing tumor and a clear Chiari malformation, which is the driving lesion. Recognizing the Chiari association directs treatment toward the craniocervical junction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this progressive, symptomatic patient?
stages.explanation: For symptomatic, progressive syringomyelia driven by a Chiari I malformation, the treatment that addresses the underlying mechanism is posterior fossa (craniocervical) decompression, which restores normal CSF flow at the foramen magnum and commonly leads to syrinx reduction and symptom stabilization. Observation with neuropathic-pain medication is reasonable only for mild, non-progressive disease; this patient has progressive deficits and painless injuries, so intervention is warranted. Corticosteroids do not treat a CSF-flow/structural problem. Lumbar puncture does not correct the craniocervical obstruction and may be hazardous by altering pressure gradients. Direct syrinx shunting is reserved for cases without Chiari or when decompression fails. Treating the Chiari malformation is the key, which is why imaging the craniocervical junction was essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication best reflects the natural history of this condition?
options: Charcot (neuropathic) arthropathy and recurrent painless burns from lost pain/temperature sensation
stages.explanation: Because syringomyelia destroys crossing pain and temperature fibers while sparing other modalities, patients lose protective sensation and suffer recurrent painless burns and cuts and ultimately Charcot (neuropathic) arthropathy, progressive joint destruction from repeated unfelt trauma, classically in the shoulder or upper limb. Patient education on injury prevention is important. Bitemporal hemianopia results from optic chiasm compression (e.g., a pituitary tumor), unrelated to a cervical syrinx. A resting tremor suggests parkinsonism, not central cord pathology. Conductive hearing loss reflects middle-ear disease; a syrinx does not cause it. As a syrinx enlarges it may also extend (syringobulbia) or cause progressive weakness and autonomic dysfunction, underscoring the value of timely decompression. The dissociated sensory loss is the anatomical basis for these painless injuries.

cases.diagnosis: Syringomyelia (cervical syrinx) associated with a Chiari I malformation.
pearls: Dissociated sensory loss (lost pain/temperature, preserved touch/proprioception) in a cape distribution localizes to the central cord.
Syringomyelia is classically associated with Chiari I malformation; always image the craniocervical junction.
Painless burns and Charcot joints result from loss of protective pain and temperature sensation.
Symptomatic Chiari-related syrinx is treated with posterior fossa decompression to restore CSF flow.

cases.title: Stabbed in the back, one leg won't move
cases.chiefComplaint: He was stabbed in the back and can't move his right leg.
cases.history: A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being stabbed in the back during an altercation about 30 minutes ago. He reports a single knife wound to the right side of his mid-back. Since the injury he cannot move his right leg well and it feels weak and wooden, while he has trouble feeling hot water and pinprick on his left leg. He localizes a band of altered sensation around his trunk. He denies neck pain, arm symptoms, loss of consciousness, or breathing difficulty. There was moderate external bleeding, now controlled with pressure. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He is a college student, smokes occasionally, drinks alcohol on weekends, and denies intravenous drug use. Immunizations are up to date, including tetanus. Family history is noncontributory. He is alert and frightened but cooperative. Review of systems is positive for right leg weakness and left-sided numbness to temperature and pain, and negative for bowel or bladder incontinence and for abdominal or chest pain.
cases.exam: Primary survey is intact: airway patent, breathing unlabored, hemodynamically stable. There is a 2 cm stab wound just right of the midline at the T10 level with controlled bleeding. Neurologic exam shows right lower-extremity weakness (3/5) with increased tone, a brisk right knee reflex, and an upgoing right plantar response below the lesion. On the right leg, vibration and joint-position sense are markedly reduced. On the left leg, pain and temperature sensation are lost beginning a couple of segments below the lesion, while proprioception is preserved. There is a narrow band of ipsilateral sensory loss at the level of the wound. Sphincter tone is intact, and the upper limbs are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After the primary survey, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediately explore the wound at the bedside
Administer high-dose IV methylprednisolone before imaging
Maintain spinal immobilization, complete the trauma survey, and obtain CT/MRI of the spine
Discharge after wound closure since he is hemodynamically stable
stages.explanation: In penetrating spinal trauma, first complete the trauma primary survey, maintain spinal immobilization, and stabilize the patient, then obtain imaging, CT to assess bony injury and a retained blade or fragments, and MRI to evaluate the cord and soft tissue. This sequence identifies the level and extent of injury and guides surgical consultation. Blind bedside wound exploration risks worsening injury and bleeding and is not the initial neurologic workup. High-dose methylprednisolone is not recommended in penetrating spinal cord injury (no benefit, increased complications) and should not precede assessment. Discharge is unsafe given clear neurologic deficits and a focal cord syndrome. Hemodynamic stability does not exclude serious cord injury, so structured assessment and imaging are mandatory before any disposition.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
FAST exam: negative for intra-abdominal free fluid
CT spine: stab tract entering the right hemicord at T10; no unstable fracture; no retained metallic fragment
MRI spine: focal signal change in the right hemicord at T10 consistent with hemisection injury; no large epidural hematoma
Hemoglobin: 13.6 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which syndrome do these crossed findings define?
stages.explanation: Ipsilateral motor weakness with upper motor neuron signs and ipsilateral loss of vibration/proprioception, combined with contralateral loss of pain and temperature beginning a few segments below the lesion, is the hallmark of Brown-Sequard syndrome from cord hemisection, here a penetrating injury to the right hemicord at T10. Complete transection would cause bilateral loss of all modalities and motor function below the level, not a crossed pattern. Anterior cord syndrome causes bilateral motor and pain/temperature loss with preserved proprioception, not a unilateral pattern. Central cord syndrome (often older patients after hyperextension) causes weakness worse in the arms than the legs with a cape-like sensory loss. The crossed, hemisection-specific findings, ipsilateral dorsal column/corticospinal loss with contralateral spinothalamic loss, define Brown-Sequard.

stages.label: NEUROANATOMY
stages.question: Why is the loss of pain and temperature on the side opposite the injury?
options: The dorsal columns cross at the spinal entry level
Spinothalamic fibers cross within a few segments of entry, so pain/temperature loss is contralateral
The corticospinal tract crosses in the spinal cord at each level
Pain and temperature fibers ascend ipsilaterally to the thalamus
stages.explanation: The crossed deficits arise from where each tract decussates. Pain and temperature fibers enter, synapse, and cross within one to two segments in the spinal cord (anterior commissure) before ascending in the contralateral spinothalamic tract, so a right hemicord lesion causes contralateral (left) pain/temperature loss beginning a couple of levels below. The dorsal columns do not cross in the cord; they ascend ipsilaterally and cross in the medulla, which is why vibration/proprioception loss is ipsilateral. The corticospinal tract crosses in the medulla (pyramidal decussation), not at each spinal level, so motor weakness is ipsilateral below a cord lesion. Pain and temperature fibers do not ascend ipsilaterally, which is the dorsal column pattern. Understanding decussation levels explains the entire Brown-Sequard picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Urgent neurosurgical consultation, supportive care, wound management with antibiotics, and avoidance of routine high-dose steroids
stages.explanation: Management of penetrating cord injury is urgent neurosurgical consultation for possible debridement/exploration (especially with CSF leak, retained fragment, or progressive deficit), supportive care, meticulous wound care with prophylactic antibiotics, and tetanus prophylaxis as needed. Routine high-dose corticosteroids are not recommended in penetrating spinal cord injury because evidence shows no benefit and increased infection and complication risk. The methylprednisolone protocol is discouraged here for those reasons. Full anticoagulation is inappropriate acutely after penetrating trauma with bleeding risk; mechanical VTE prophylaxis is preferred initially. A lumbar drain is not a standard acute intervention for this injury. Early surgical input plus infection prevention and rehabilitation optimize outcomes, and clinicians should monitor for delayed deterioration from hematoma or CSF leak.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the expected prognosis of this syndrome?
options: The worst prognosis of all cord syndromes, with rare recovery
The best prognosis among incomplete cord syndromes, with most patients regaining ambulation
stages.explanation: Brown-Sequard syndrome carries the best prognosis among the incomplete spinal cord syndromes: most patients recover substantial motor function and the majority regain the ability to ambulate, particularly with early management and rehabilitation. The claim that it has the worst prognosis is the opposite of reality, since outcomes are comparatively favorable. Progression to complete paraplegia is not the expected course of a hemisection, especially when the contralateral cord is intact. Bladder and bowel function are often preserved or recover, unlike statements of no recovery. Functional outcome depends on the degree of cord disruption and timely care; the relatively intact contralateral pathways support meaningful recovery. Rehabilitation and prevention of secondary injury (infection, pressure ulcers, VTE) are central to optimizing this favorable trajectory.

cases.diagnosis: Brown-Sequard syndrome (right spinal cord hemisection) at T10 from a penetrating stab wound.
pearls: Brown-Sequard: ipsilateral motor and dorsal-column (vibration/proprioception) loss with contralateral pain/temperature loss below the lesion.
Spinothalamic fibers cross within 1-2 segments in the cord; dorsal columns and corticospinal tract cross in the medulla, explaining the crossed deficits.
Avoid routine high-dose steroids in penetrating spinal cord injury; prioritize surgery, wound care, antibiotics, and tetanus prophylaxis.
Brown-Sequard has the best prognosis of the incomplete cord syndromes; most patients regain ambulation.

cases.title: Can't move her legs after aortic surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Post-op, she cannot move either leg.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman in the surgical ICU is evaluated on postoperative day 1 after open thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm repair for new inability to move both legs. The repair was complicated by a prolonged aortic cross-clamp time and a period of intraoperative hypotension. On waking she could not lift either leg and reports she cannot feel pinprick or temperature below the lower chest, yet she can still sense the position of her toes when tested. She has had no leg movement since extubation. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prior tobacco use; home medications are amlodipine, atorvastatin, and aspirin (held perioperatively). She has no known drug allergies. She is a retired bookkeeper. Family history includes aortic disease in her father. She denies back pain at rest and has no prior neurologic deficits. Review of systems is limited by the postoperative setting but is positive for bilateral leg paralysis, loss of pain and temperature sensation below the chest, and new urinary catheter dependence, and negative for arm weakness or facial symptoms.
cases.exam: The patient is awake and following commands. Cranial nerves and upper-extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation are normal. The lower extremities are flaccid with 0/5 strength bilaterally and absent deep tendon reflexes (early spinal shock). Pain and temperature sensation are absent below a level at the lower thorax bilaterally, whereas vibration and joint-position sense are preserved in the toes and ankles, a dissociated pattern sparing the posterior columns. Plantar responses are initially mute. There is urinary retention requiring catheterization and decreased rectal tone. The abdomen is soft with a clean surgical dressing, and distal pulses are present. No spinal tenderness is elicited.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management of this new postoperative paraplegia?
options: Raise mean arterial pressure, initiate/optimize lumbar CSF drainage, and obtain urgent MRI of the spine
Obtain a CT head to evaluate for stroke
stages.explanation: New paraplegia after thoracoabdominal aortic repair is spinal cord ischemia until proven otherwise, and the immediate priority is to restore spinal cord perfusion: augment mean arterial pressure (often a MAP goal around 90-100 mmHg) and institute or optimize lumbar CSF drainage to lower CSF pressure and improve cord perfusion pressure, while obtaining urgent spinal MRI to exclude a compressive hematoma. High-dose steroids are not the established treatment for this ischemic, postoperative deficit and delay perfusion-directed care. CT head evaluates the brain; the deficit localizes to the spinal cord with a sensory level, not a cerebral lesion. Mannitol and hyperventilation target raised intracranial pressure, not spinal cord ischemia. Rapid MAP augmentation plus CSF drainage is the time-critical response and can be partially reversible.

stages.label: MRI & RESPONSE
stages.content:
MRI spine (after stabilization):
- T2 hyperintensity in the anterior two-thirds of the cord over several lower thoracic segments ('owl's eye' anterior horn signal)
- Posterior columns spared; no epidural hematoma or compressive mass
MAP increased to 95 mmHg with vasopressors; lumbar drain placed
Neurologic exam: no immediate change

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the findings?
stages.explanation: Bilateral flaccid paraplegia with loss of pain and temperature below a thoracic level but preserved vibration and proprioception, after aortic surgery, with MRI showing infarction of the anterior two-thirds of the cord, defines anterior spinal artery (anterior cord) syndrome. The anterior spinal artery supplies the corticospinal tracts, spinothalamic tracts, and anterior horns, while the posterior columns (posterior spinal arteries) are spared, explaining preserved proprioception. Guillain-Barre causes ascending areflexic weakness but is symmetric without a sensory level and is not tied to aortic cross-clamping or anterior-cord MRI signal. Posterior cord syndrome would impair proprioception and vibration while sparing pain/temperature, the opposite pattern. Conversion disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion and cannot explain objective MRI infarction and a clear vascular mechanism. The surgical context and dissociated findings are classic.

stages.label: NEUROANATOMY
stages.question: Why is proprioception preserved in this patient?
options: The posterior columns are supplied by the posterior spinal arteries, which are spared
Proprioceptive fibers cross immediately in the cord and are protected
The anterior spinal artery supplies the dorsal columns
Vibration sense is carried in the spinothalamic tract
stages.explanation: Proprioception and vibration are preserved because the dorsal (posterior) columns are perfused by the paired posterior spinal arteries, which remain patent in anterior spinal artery syndrome. The single anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the cord, the corticospinal tracts (motor), spinothalamic tracts (pain/temperature), and anterior horns, so those functions are lost while dorsal-column functions persist. Proprioceptive fibers do not cross immediately; they ascend ipsilaterally in the dorsal columns and cross in the medulla, so their preservation here is vascular, not due to early crossing. The anterior spinal artery does not supply the dorsal columns, which is precisely why they are spared. Vibration is carried in the dorsal columns, not the spinothalamic tract. The vascular anatomy fully explains the dissociated, proprioception-sparing deficit.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: Compromise of which vessel most likely caused this syndrome?
options: The artery of Adamkiewicz (great anterior radiculomedullary artery)
stages.explanation: The dominant blood supply to the anterior spinal artery in the lower thoracic and upper lumbar cord is the artery of Adamkiewicz (great anterior radiculomedullary artery), typically arising from a left intercostal or lumbar branch around T9-T12. During thoracoabdominal aortic surgery, aortic cross-clamping, intercostal artery sacrifice, and hypotension can compromise this vessel, infarcting the anterior cord and producing this syndrome. The vertebral arteries supply the brainstem and upper cervical structures, not the thoracic anterior cord. PICA occlusion causes lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome, a brainstem stroke. MCA embolism causes a cerebral hemispheric stroke with cortical signs, not a spinal sensory level with preserved proprioception. Recognizing the artery of Adamkiewicz explains both the mechanism and prevention strategies (CSF drainage, MAP goals, intercostal reimplantation).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which strategy is the cornerstone of treating and preventing this complication?
options: Therapeutic hypothermia to 33 C for 24 hours
Lumbar CSF drainage and blood-pressure augmentation to maximize spinal cord perfusion pressure
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of both prevention and treatment of spinal cord ischemia in thoracoabdominal aortic surgery is maximizing spinal cord perfusion pressure, which equals mean arterial pressure minus CSF (or central venous) pressure. Lumbar CSF drainage lowers CSF pressure while blood-pressure augmentation raises MAP, together improving cord perfusion, and these measures can partially reverse delayed-onset deficits. Systemic therapeutic hypothermia is not a standard, proven therapy for this complication, though regional cooling is investigational. Thrombolysis is not indicated, since the mechanism is watershed/branch-vessel hypoperfusion in a postoperative patient with bleeding risk, not a thrombolyzable large-vessel clot. Fasciotomy treats compartment syndrome, unrelated to cord ischemia. Maintaining MAP goals and CSF drainage, sometimes with deficit-triggered protocols, is the evidence-based approach and a key teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Anterior spinal artery syndrome (anterior cord syndrome) from spinal cord ischemia after thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm repair.
pearls: Anterior cord syndrome: lost motor and pain/temperature below the lesion with preserved vibration/proprioception (dorsal columns spared).
The artery of Adamkiewicz is the major feeder to the lower anterior spinal cord and is vulnerable during thoracoabdominal aortic surgery.
Spinal cord perfusion pressure = MAP minus CSF pressure; treat ischemia with CSF drainage and MAP augmentation.
Preserved proprioception with lost pain/temperature distinguishes anterior cord syndrome from a complete transection.
